ISG0192 - Evidence on Industrial strategyWritten evidence from Richard Blausten (ISG 192) NO MORE FOREIGN TAKEOVERS - INSTEAD FOREIGN PARTNERSHIPS AND FOREIGN INVESTMENT "BEING OPEN FOR BUSINESS" DOESN'T HAVE TO MEAN "BEING OPEN TO FOREIGN TAKEOVERS" THEMES - This submission argues against any further takeovers of UK companies by foreign organisations and enterprises and argues instead for promoting new and alternative partnership arrangements between UK firms and foreign entities and new investment structures to channel money into UK firms. (Please treat the terms UK and British as synonymous.)---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------RECOMMENDATIONS:* Foreign takeovers of UK companies should no longer be allowed.* Foreign entities should be encouraged to form partnerships with up to 49% ownership in UK companies' subsidiaries and special projects where they have the option of management roles and general operational involvement.* Foreign entities should be encouraged to put money into special sector or company-specific funds which give up to 49% ownership in UK companies' subsidiaries and special projects where there is close monitoring and liaison but not the option of management roles.--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------THE AUTHOR - Richard Blausten is managing director of Mitre House Publishing Ltd.  A background note about the author and company appears immediately the end of this submission.PRACTICAL EXPERIENCE - The author has had the personal experience of both a fruitful investment relationship with a foreign company and an unwelcome takeover offer from a foreign company.  A background note appears at the very end of this submission.--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------WHY IT IS TIME TO CALL A HALT TO THE TAKEOVER OF UK FIRMS BY FOREIGN ENTITIES1. BEING IN CONTROL.  Being in control of one's own country - being in control what is going on in it and not being run from abroad - was a key ingredient of the pro Leave message in the EU referendum  and has been recognised by, and incorporated in the "Brexit means Brexit" approach.  For some time too there has been a strong and now overwhelming element in the nation's thinking of wanting to do things the British way and according to British interests, as opposed to having to take heed of foreign requirements.When a UK company is taken over by a foreign entity the UK thinking, focus and orientation inevitably is diluted, if not removed altogether. The foreign parent has its own agenda, often not in the interests of the UK, the company as an entity in the UK, its employees in the UK, or indeed UK customers.  When a UK company is multinational or global in its operations a change to foreign control means the company ceases to reflect the values of its British ownership and instead reflects those of the new foreign owner. (See the next paragraph.)2. IT'S OUR COUNTRY - PATRIOTISM. Also, there is the element of patriotism, of belief in one's country and everything that makes up one's country, as evidenced for instance by the spirit of the 2012 Olympics.  UK companies are part of the make-up of our country, particularly if they are headquartered here and/or have significant workforces here. UK companies operating overseas have been a key factor over time of our economic development and represent a proud display of our economic strength and national values.3. OTHER COUNTRIES WOULD KNOW WHERE WE ARE COMING FROM ON THIS. Long before the current era of Brexit, and certainly as long ago since the end of 2nd World War, other countries have put up all sorts of barriers to takeovers of their companies by foreign firms.  While indeed Germany, France, Spain and Switzerland have long been "open for business", for them that doesn't mean making it easy for foreigners to take over their companies. Japan and China are only too happy to export to us and we can export to them, albeit with varying degrees of ease, and so intimate they are "open for business", but it is very hard for foreign companies to take over Japanese or Chinese businesses.One of the key reasons for there being so many barriers to foreign takeovers of firms in other countries is that throughout their societies - from government (and local/regional government) downwards - there is a feeling that foreign control of their firms is not good for their country.  So it's perfectly respectable and reasonable to have this feeling in the UK, and our trading partners and those countries which already have taken over so many of our companies should be expected to understand an "Enough is enough" approach on our part.In the US, despite on the surface there being little hindrance to foreign takeovers of US companies, in certain key sectors there is huge protectionism concerning their presence, as evidenced by biased and quite vicious regulatory penalties levied on a seemingly regular basis on UK financial institutions operating in America, and the massive "free for all" financial attack launched on BP following the Gulf of Mexico oil spillage within the context of unexpected Presidential anti-Britishness.The point is that the desire to protect one's country's key commercial operations is one that permeates the thinking of both our closest trading partners and those we would like to do more business with (e.g. India).  So to talk about protecting our companies from foreign takeovers is not outlandish.  We shouldn't be intimidated by the noise coming from the big multinational/global corporations, Remainers in government, and trade and business organisations like the CBI.4. THE NEED TO REJECT THE VIEWS OF BIG UK MULTINATIONALS/GLOBAL PLAYERS.   Big UK companies which see themselves as global players say there mustn't be any restrictions on foreign takeovers of UK companies because, in effect, it would restrict their own takeover activities overseas.  (They also say it would restrict investment in the UK and growth, etc. - but more about that later in discussion of alternative mechanisms.) This needs to be put into sharp perspective.  As just mentioned, opportunities to take over companies in other countries are significantly limited and restrictions are unlikely to be relaxed sufficiently, particularly in (newer or emerging) markets where attractive growth is perceived. The prospect of these countries satisfactorily reducing their restrictions is a mirage.At the same time, the Brexit decision involved a specific rejection of the thought processes of the multinational/global establishment.  Its values were not those of the British people.5. FOREIGN TAKEOVERS OF UK COMPANIES CAN LEAD TO FOREIGN STRATEGIC CONTROL OF KEY SECTORS IN THE UK. Over time leading British companies in important sectors have been taken over, one after the other, similar to being knocked down like ninepins.  Foreign companies now control key sectors such as cement, bricks, glass, chemicals, passenger shipping, seaports, airports, computers, micro chips.    The insurance sector is a major example of this happening.  We only have two really significant general insurance companies left and they are constantly being subject to takeover approaches and takeover rumours.  Our major general insurance brokers have all been taken over, with one solitary exception.  Thus foreign companies control all our non-Lloyd's international insurance brokerage business. We have no reinsurance companies and no significant reinsurance brokers left. Even at a regional level in the UK, foreign general insurance brokers have been buying up our major regional brokers.   THE MOTOR INDUSTRY. Only in one special case can foreign strategic control of a whole sector be regarded as acceptable - the motor vehicle industry.  Repeated ghastly failures led to it being obvious that we were incapable of running our motor manufacturing on our own, and it required completely new  ideas, management skills from Japanese and Indian companies in particular, to give us a viable and growing motor manufacturing sector.But in no other sector can foreign control be justified in a similar way.  No other major EU country or, indeed the US, has foreign control of a key industrial or financial sector. 6. WE NEED NATIONAL CHAMPIONS AND TO SAFEGUARD THEM. Surviving large UK-owned companies and leading UK-owned medium sized companies should be seen as national champions. They are important centres of British-owned commercial activity and should be sustained as such.  This means both protecting them from foreign takeovers and providing them with new partnership and financial structures as options.  7. THE NEED TO PRESERVE THE START-UP SPIRIT AND SUSTAIN THE RESULTING SUCCESSES.  A fantastic British success story are the ongoing launches of innovative British-based start-ups, either in niche or completely new areas, sometimes with applications right across whole industries, ranging from fruit drinks and chocolate to digital, telecoms and pharma.  However, just as these new companies start to show significant signs of success they are taken over.  Napoleon called us a nation of shopkeepers, sneering at our stubborn independent-mindedness. In our modern equivalent we are a nation of start-ups. We need to preserve this element of our independence.The time has come to provide compelling partnership and investment alternatives to allow start-ups to go forward and become bigger companies and, ultimately, national champions - in the same way as so many of America's biggest corporations started in kitchens and garages and were able to grow to their large sizes still maintaining their pioneering spirit. Ideally home grown support, including via government and institutional investment, would be preferable, but foreign partnership and investment have a part to play.                             ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ADDRESSING THE NEED TO PROVIDE AN ALTERNATIVE TO FOREIGN TAKEOVERS1. THE NEED TO CHALLENGE THE DYNAMICS OF FOREIGN TAKEOVERS. There are two main drivers of foreign takeovers of UK companies - where there are perceptions that a company is doing very well and so makes an attractive target for a predatory foreign entity; where a whole army of investment bankers and stock market players (including hedge funds) spend their time in sourcing, fomenting or encouraging the next big deal so that they can make money from either arranging the deals or selling out of positions they have taken in a company's shares. These two drivers usually come together - the investment bankers coming to the predatory foreign companies with takeover propositions and the significant shareholders, with their short term horizons, always keen to take a good profit as fast as possible. The result is that they overwhelm the desire of management and smaller shareholders to resist the takeover attempt. So we have a situation of "success in the corporate world leads to a foreign takeover"; investment bankers and major shareholders believe "short term profit is the way forward and that requires a foreign takeover".There is another driver of foreign takeovers of UK companies, albeit it is in a different category.  This is where short term poor results or ongoing poor results collapse a company's shares and leave it vulnerable to an opportunistic takeover.  This is a problem and needs addressing but not by a foreign takeover.  If foreign involvement is needed then this can be organised according to the recommendations below.2. FORMING NEW CORPORATE AND FINANCIAL STRUCTURES TO PROVIDE ALTERNATIVE WAYS FOR FOREIGN ENTITIES TO INVOLVE THEMSELVES WITH UK COMPANIES. The following are outline suggestions but should be treated as a serious basis for discussion and taking them forward.THE PRINCIPLE OF FOREIGN PARTNERSHIPS RATHER THAN TAKEOVERS.  Further foreign entity involvement in the affairs of UK companies should be in the form of partnerships, with the foreign shareholding/ownership being anything up to 49% but no more.  This would not be in the main company, i.e. what could in fact become the holding company, but rather in existing subsidiaries or new subsidiaries or specific projects.  Thus if a predatory foreign company was interested in a UK company because of a particular activity it carried out, it would then be able, subject to the consent of the UK company, to have the option to negotiate a minority partnership participation in that activity, up to 49%.  Equally, if a foreign company wished to have a new base in the UK via a UK company for a particular activity, instead of being able to take over the company, it would be able to set up a joint venture with the UK company, taking up to a 49% share.  The top and other management of the new joint venture could come from the foreign entity but the foreign entity's share could not rise over 49%.THE PRINCIPLE OF SPECIAL FOREIGN INVESTMENT PARTICIPATION RATHER THAN TAKEOVERS. Often foreign entities buy UK companies really as a home for their funds, albeit to grow these funds, rather than as a desire to become actively involved in management.  Nevertheless, they do prefer a closer relationship with their investments compared to simply investing in an independent regulated fund run by an investment manager and open to other investors (whether private and/or institutional).  Special sector or company-specific investment funds should be set up in the UK run by UK-based managers which enable foreign entities to directly invest in specifically focused subsidiaries or new projects of companies - up to 49% .  The investments made would bring with them the ability of the investors to have much closer discussions with and monitoring of the management of these subsidiaries and projects than in a strictly arm's length situation.  It would be a true investment partnership between the UK company and the foreign-owned but UK-based investment fund, with both partners being prepared to increase their investment when jointly judged to be appropriate.THUS FOREIGN INVOLVEMENT IN THE FORTUNES OF UK COMPANIES WITHOUT TAKING THEM OVER.    The proposed partnership and investment structures/mechanisms above would encourage positive involvement by foreign entities in the affairs and activities of UK companies within the constraints of the overall benefit being to the UK company - its fortunes and therefore the fortunes of its employees and the overall fortunes of the UK.  Both thriving UK companies and those facing difficult challenges could find these arrangements to their advantage. Majority ownership at all stages and whatever the proposition would remain in the hands of  the UK company, while foreign involvement could bring new management skills where appropriate, new perspectives and additional financial resources. END OF CRITIQUE AND RECOMMENDATIONS--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------THE AUTHOR AND HIS COMPANY - A BACKGROUND NOTENow coming to the end of his career, aged 68 in December, Richard Blausten had an extensive career during the 70s and early 80s in general insurance, life assurance, shipping and investment (mining, shipping, oil and chemicals sectors) before embarking on a new career in journalism (including for the Sunday Times Business News) and then setting up his own publishing company, Mitre House Publishing Ltd in December 1984.  At the height of its fortunes in the mid 90s Mitre House Publishing produced 14 titles taking in the investment, general insurance, risk management, international insurance, reinsurance, IT and charity sectors.  As personal retirement beckons, Mitre House is now a truly micro, indeed virtual,  business, publishing an online magazine and website for the charity sector (Charities Management), and a website for consumer and business purchasers of general insurance (Having Insurance). -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------PRACTICAL PERSONAL EXPERIENCE OF FOREIGN INVOLVEMENT IN MY COMPANY - GOOD AND BAD!I have had two contacts with foreign companies, both Dutch; one a partnership arrangement and the other a rejected takeover approach.THE PARTNERSHIP ARRANGEMENT. In the early 90s I was approached by the German managing director of the investment/development capital side in the UK of Amro Bank, as it was then.  With his boundless enthusiasm he saw what he perceived as the potential for my investment magazine, Investment Management, and wanted in particular to take it into foreign markets, e.g. Saudi Arabia and Switzerland.  He organised the bank to take a 9% stake in Mitre House Publishing and both pushed me hard in the direction he wanted me to go and supported me with unflagging optimism.  He was a truly exciting and supportive partner.  Unfortunately, the relationship came to an end when a financial crash in the early 90s forced the closure of his division in the UK - but we parted on amicable terms and to my net financial advantage.THE TAKEOVER APPROACH.  In the latter half of the 90s after the dot com crash Mitre House was struggling.  I then had a takeover approach from a Dutch publishing company.  The proposed deal was to strip out my magazines and move them to Holland, dispensing with myself and my 13 staff. Not quite the foreign involvement I would be comfortable with.  I decided to soldier on putting my own personal resources into the company and we eventually worked our way out of our problems.  The Dutch predator only wanted our products, not our people or our skills.I have had other takeover approaches in the past from UK companies.  These involved continuing to utilise my services and the skills of my employees.  Ultimately, though I decided to continue on my own in my own way.----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------28 October, 2016

ISG0149 - Evidence on Industrial strategy     Written evidence from the Society of Chemical Industry (ISG 149)  EXECUTIVE SUMMARYIn the light of the UK’s decision to leave the European Union the UK Government is increasing its focus on an Industrial Strategy as a means of providing economic and societal benefit for the UK.  An Industrial Strategy is critical in order to rebalance the economy. Industry has the potential to provide an engine for long-term, sustainable growth and prosperity to society.  Industry works in a global marketplace and the Industrial Strategy needs to be focussed on driving the UK’s competitiveness in key global markets.  Industry is a broad term, covering a complex mix of different industries and segments. The UK Government needs to address the whole spectrum of industry, including infrastructure and energy, and be able to bring together the key elements in an over-arching National Strategy.  It is critical that the Government sets up a systematic approach to developing a new Industrial Strategy for the UK that is (i) long term in nature, (ii) broad industry and (iii) engages the key stakeholders.     ABOUT THE SOCIETY OF CHEMICAL INDUSTRYThe Society of Chemical Industry (SCI) is a learned society, established in 1881 specifically to promote the application of science into industry for the benefit of the public. SCI’s founders were scientists, inventors and entrepreneurs who went onto form industrial businesses that formed the heart of the 20th Century Industrial Revolution. The Society is unique, being a multi-science, multi-disciplinary and international community. SCI today continues to work at the interface between science and industry, conducting a range of activities that focus on supporting innovation and the commercialisation of science. We welcome the opportunity to provide input into the Inquiry into Industrial Strategy launched by the Business, Innovation and Skills Committee (BIS). When it comes to science based industries and innovation, we believe the following are of specific concern and need to be taken into consideration: Strategic Context  Brexit creates an historic opportunity to reset the UK’s economy. The UK needs to have a balanced range of sectors providing growth, value and societal benefit. Industry creates a significant opportunity to rebalance the UK’s economy and provide both growth and economic value for the UK. 1.1           Rebalancing the UK Economy by rebuilding an Industrial Base Over the last 50 years the UK economy has become highly dependent on the Services sector. Parts of this sector have proven to be highly volatile, requiring significant government investment and intervention in recent years.  Over the last 50 years manufacturing has declined as a proportion of overall GVA. Going forward the UK Government needs to re-address the imbalance and seek to ensure a range of sectors where there are opportunities for growth and value to the UK.   Figure 1 Manufacturing as % of UK GVA, source PWC: the future of UK manufacturing[1]   *Other production includes Agriculture, Mining, Forestry & Fishing, Energy and Utilities, and Construction **Total services excludes Governmental services      1.2            Industry brings Economic and Societal Benefits Industry provides many economic and societal benefits, specifically:(i)                  Industry provides a multitude of products to address societal needs and enhance everyday life. (ii)                Industry provides a significant number of jobs, many regionally dispersed and many of them skilled. (iii)               Industry facilitates the opportunities for significant investment in the UK – both from onshore and offshore companies and investors. (iv)              Industry is highly creative. Many sectors of industry rely on science and innovation to create competitive advantage and to form a basis for sustainable growth.(v)                Industry has provided the focal point for many communities around the UK – offering longevity of employment to successive generations and supporting business providing local services. Following the Brexit decision, the Government needs to develop a long-term overarching national vision for economic growth of the UK and an Industrial Strategy will be pivotal in delivering that vision.  1.3 The Structure and Interdependency of Industry Industry is a generic term that encompasses many different sectors. These sectors are intertwined as they provide critical raw materials and/or services to each other.  When considering an Industrial Strategy, care needs to be taken not to view individual sectors in isolation as the health in one sector often directly impacts on the health in another sector. Lack of competitiveness in one sector can result directly in the loss of competitiveness in another sector and can lead, in some cases, to loss of manufacturing and jobs. The Energy, Chemicals and Process Industries are important parts of the industry infrastructure – providing vital raw materials for a wide range of ‘downstream’ sectors, including coatings, paints, pharmaceuticals, agrochemical, food, personal care, automotive, aerospace and electronics. Ensuring a competitive infrastructure is a key component of any Industrial Strategy. Of note is that all of these sectors operate in global markets therefore an Industrial Strategy needs to be cognisant of the UK’s current competitiveness and needs to ensure that the UK establishes a position as one of the top locations to establish and grow businesses.  1.4   Erosion of the UK’s standing in Global Industrial Markets Industry is well established in the UK, and indeed the UK was at the heart of the industrial revolution of the 20th century. Industries were created at that time, mainly due to the successful commercialisation of new technologies and processes.  However arguably the UK’s competitiveness has been in decline in recent decades. This decline has been accelerated by an increasingly uncompetitive infrastructure, more active competition from overseas, and increasing regulation all of which has hampered innovation and growth.  Over the last 50 years the UK has enjoyed GDP growth but not at the rate of other economies, in particular the USA and China as highlighted in Figure 2 below.  Figure 2: GDP Growth (US$)  Globally industrial markets are growing driven by increasing consumer and societal needs. However, despite this global growth the UK has not always been able to take its share of this growth.  Within key markets the UK’s position globally has been in decline as shown in Figure 3 overleaf. In both the Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals Industries the UK’s position has significantly declined over the last 10 years, despite these markets showing growth globally.       Figure 3: Comparison of UK Chemical and Pharmaceutical Industries between 2004 and 2014[2]   ChemicalIndustry PharmaceuticalIndustryGlobal Market Size 2014 € Million  3’100’169  715’99210 year market growth2004-2014 cagr %   8.1%  4.6%UK Market share 2004  3.6% 5.0%UK Market share 2014  1.3% 2.4%change (64.5)%  (50.8)% In the case of Chemicals, China has been the major growth area, driven by low cost labour and competitive raw materials and energy.  Figure 4: World Chemical Industry sales by Region 2004-2014 [3]  There are a number of reasons for the declining position of the UK as a global manufacturer but significant reasons include:(i)                Erosion of the UK’s cost base – energy, infrastructure and raw materials.(ii)              Increasing globalisation and the rise of Chinese manufacturing. (iii)            Increasing regulatory burden. If an Industrial Strategy is to be effective, it needs to address the critical reasons why the UK is losing competitiveness and then be able to execute actions to address these issues.  An Industrial Strategy  With the view to rebalancing the economy and to provide long term sustainable growth, the UK needs to develop an Industrial Strategy which, at its heart, needs to focus on ensuring a competitive infrastructure and support system to both support existing industry and to attract new industry, whether that is via inward investment or new ‘home grown’ businesses.  The UK has a number of significant assets, notably a strong manufacturing base, a legacy of developing a sizeable manufacturing industry and some skills (although these are in decline).   Many industries require a strong innovation pipeline quite often, but not exclusively, driven by science. The UK has undoubtedly one of the best science bases in the world and produces high quality scientists and engineers.  The UK has a strong financial services sector, a long history of trading and has a supportive government. Brexit clearly presents a number of challenges but presents a unique opportunity for the UK to reposition itself as a focused, flexible, industry friendly environment.  However, there are significant issues to be addressed and the Industrial Strategy needs to ensure the UK builds a highly competitive and innovative industry fit for the 21st century that is driven by an understanding of how the UK can best compete in global market needs.   2.1 Key Underpins of an Industrial Strategy (i)   Based on Long Term time horizons Industry works on long term time horizons driven by the nature of investments and the innovation pipelines. The Industrial Strategy needs to address, as a minimum, a 20 year time horizon. Other countries work on the basis of long term plans to ensure the right investment decisions in infrastructure and innovation can be made. One example would be China. China’s Made in China 2025[4] plan, details the introduction of high level targets and long-term deadlines giving clear objectives to the scientific and business communities on the key issues to be addressed as priorities to help grow China’s position as a leader in manufacturing. A clear statement of intent and a focus on the key enablers required, delivered consistently over a long term time horizon are important.   (ii)                A Competitive Fiscal Environment to support growth  As has been commented on many times the UK needs to establish a highly competitive fiscal environment to support growing industry and inward investment.  The fiscal framework needs to support medium and large corporations in the following ways:(a) encourage corporations to be headquartered in the UK(b) ensure businesses carry out manufacturing in the UK(c)  ensure companies base their Research & Development centres in the UK(d) ensure significant investments are made in the UK.  (iii)               Ensuring access to Competitive Energy  One of the priorities is to ensure access to competitive, sustainable and reliable energy sources, along with increasing efforts in reducing energy demands. Whilst it is difficult obtaining absolute comparators of energy costs for industry available reports indicate that the UK is one of the most expensive global locations for electricity and that these costs are an order of magnitude different to those of the US and China.   Figure 5: Energy Prices Comparison by Country[5]    The Industrial Strategy needs to address the issue of how to ensure that industry has access to highly competitive and secure sources of energy. It needs to develop both short term and a long-term plan.  In the short term shale could provide a significant step change in energy costs for industry. In recent years the US, in particular, has driven a step change in energy costs via the shale gas revolution. Shale gas has been a strategic investment, securing local energy supplies and providing significant competitive advantage for industry.  Figure 6 shows the decline in reliance in imports of natural gas since the US has embraced shale exploitation.  Figure 6: US Natural Gas Net Imports 1973-2015[6]   Shale gas has had a direct and significant impact on the US economy, resulting in inward investment, a decline in imports, additional jobs and it has precipitated growth in the downstream industry.  Over the longer term priority should be given to innovation to help support a long-term energy solutions plan by identifying and developing new sources of energy generation.   (iv)              Ensuring access to competitive raw materials  Though industry is prepared[7] to make the investment necessary to ensure continued access to essential feedstocks, considering how to supplement them with alternative local sources of energy and raw materials is essential to underpin a sound manufacturing base.  Many metals have long been recognised as critical raw materials. Many chemicals[8] are just as critical to society. These include simple acids, a number of gases such as chlorine and some basic petrochemicals.   Industry, in particular the science based manufacturing industry, is highly integrated and key decisions in one part of the supply chain have been shown to have a considerable adverse effect in other parts in the chain.  Removal of any major building blocks from local supply can have a critical impact on the sustainability of the rest of the supply chain. Businesses that previously purchased raw materials within a couple of miles are forced to source materials from overseas – adding considerable transport costs which then need to be passed on through the finished product prices to the next producer.  In the face of competition from other countries, production of some of these substances, such as ethylene oxide, has significantly reduced in the UK. Yet, the production of many of the complex chemicals that are used today and that are essential to innovation come from combining these most basic of chemicals as building blocks.  Pending a thorough analysis of their economic viability and with the right investment, alternative feedstocks from plastic waste and biomass may contribute to the mix of feedstocks used by industry in the UK to produce building blocks to all the other sectors however it is highly unlikely they will be able to fully replace synthetically derived materials.   (v)      Developing Competitive Advantage via Accelerating Innovation  The UK has long been recognised as an international centre of excellence for science and innovation. The existing innovation ecosystem that was created through the work of Innovate UK and its Catapult Centres will act as an excellent base to build upon with the UK aiming to become an international hub for the commercialisation of the most promising technologies and sectors.  The UK needs to be cognisant of the impact of Brexit on the withdrawal of the UK from the European innovation infrastructure currently in place. The various supporting mechanisms, are all required to ensure that the UK entrepreneurs and industry can successfully continue to commercialise new products in the UK.  Issues identified, such as why the UK appears to lag other European countries in private investment in R&D, should be studied in more detail and addressed.  It is important for the UK to attract and retain the Research and Development Centres for large companies. They are the vehicle for taking the science from the laboratories into commercial scale manufacturing. They provide critical mass and are the mechanism for nurturing and developing a very important set of industrial skills that are required for successful scale up. These skills are often broad ranging – from analytical to process chemistry and include a range of engineering skills. Whilst being STEM graduates, these teams often come with many years of industrial experience that is essential to ensuring efficient market adoption of new technologies. The significant work on developing innovation pipelines already carried out in many sectors should be reviewed and brought together in an over-arching innovation plan for the UK industry and this should be linked to the science plan. This will help to focus and prioritise the underpinning science and technology base that will facilitate accelerating innovation.  (vi)              Ensuring an efficient Regulatory Regime Above all, the future UK regulatory regime needs to be stable, pragmatic, coherent and to be based on sound science. It should enable trade with key trade markets – specifically the USA and the EU.  When it comes to health, safety and environment legislation, it is important for any new framework to offer the same level of protection to workers, the public and the environment. Over time, opportunities to reduce the legislative cost burden on businesses should be sought however in terms of simplified compliance requirements. When it comes to employment law, the UK has one of the most flexible regimes in the world. Bearing in mind that many sectors are short of skilled workers, any change to British law in terms of social and employment legislation needs to be mindful of not damaging the job market attractiveness to non-British workers. After 40 years of EU membership, the EU regulatory framework is thoroughly embedded into UK businesses’ practices and any move away from current regulatory requirements is likely to come with some costs to businesses. Careful consideration should be given to any changes as a consequence. In terms of science based businesses, the most challenging of the EU legislation to be addressed will be REACH[9] in light of its complexity and the fact that compliance is centralised with one of the EU agencies.   The regulatory regime affecting industry needs to be mapped out and opportunities to simplify and reduce cost should be identified.   (vii)             Investing for the long-termInvestment cycles in industry, innovation and infrastructure are long by nature with some coming to fruition many years after the decision to invest or build was reached.  Supporting established older industry sectors is important to help preparing manufacturing for the future through the implementation of different potentially disruptive approaches.   Investment should also be focussed on building up the resilience of companies in terms of climate change adaptation, exploring digitalisation and new process techniques with the aim of improving efficiencies and ultimately competitiveness. In terms of support to existing manufacturing businesses, this could potentially involve, in the medium term, supporting companies in modernising their processes to improve their financial viability. This would be done through improved access to funding. In terms of interventionism, visible governmental support for British takeover of innovative non British companies should be given priority as well as promoting all sectors of industry abroad.  Finally, it is important to scrutinise the reasons behind the takeovers of British businesses to ensure they are not just a cost saving exercise likely to lead to closures creating a potential irreparable gap in terms of industrial feedstocks, infrastructure and core skills.   Purchases motivated by the exploitation of innovative ideas and research likely to benefit the UK economy and improve its industrial resilience should be stimulated.  (viii)           Skills and Education In the context of science-based industry, it is also absolutely essential to improve and retain access to STEM qualified personnel. Recent data[10] suggests that between 180,000-260,000 new scientific staff will be required for science based industries by 2025 in the UK and that experienced staff as well as graduate level intake will be required. On-going efforts to attract more people to the manufacturing sector will need to be intensified so as to secure its future. Apprentices form an important part of the workforce for industry and schemes such as the Cogent Gold Standards for apprenticeships are a good example of training for the next generation.  Schemes designed to encourage young people into science and ultimately industry, industrial internships and active campaigns targeted at the younger generations designed to improve the reputation of the manufacturing sector and encourage people to study scientific disciplines should all be encouraged and financially supported.   (ix)              Addressing the public perception of Industry An Industrial Strategy needs to address the public perception of industry. There are significant economic and societal benefits from industry – not least the myriad of everyday products that are often taken for granted. The Industrial Strategy needs to have an educational plan designed to address this issue.    3. Summary The government’s focus on an Industrial Strategy is strongly supported. Industry can provide opportunities for growth, economic value and societal benefit. Industry is a complex network of inter-related businesses and the Industrial Strategy has to ensure an overarching approach that addresses the issues documented here.  There are many documents already in existence which set out key parts of an Industrial Strategy, such as the Chemistry Growth Partnership Strategy (leading to the formation of the Chemistry Growth Partnership[11]), the Agritech Industrial Strategy: Evaluation Scoping Study and Baseline[12], and the outputs from the Food Innovation Network Workshop run by Defra and these should be taken into account.  Fundamentally the Industrial Strategy needs to address the UK’s competitiveness as a place to grow and develop manufacturing industry.  Every effort needs to be made to make the UK an attractive place to do business through supportive financial incentives, long-term planning to establish a refreshed industrial base fit for the 21st century and by retaining access to the best talent and skills from the UK and beyond. To compete in the future, the UK has to focus on driving innovation rather than competing on cost. Providing the right environment to embrace new manufacturing sectors is as important as supporting established sectors to help them evolve to remain competitive in a global environment. The new Strategy also needs to identify means to support businesses as they prepare to operate under new disruptive business models, such as those detailed in the Chemical Industry Vision 2016 report[13], published recently by Accenture. SCI’s ResponseWhat does the Government mean by industrial strategy, and what does the private sector want from one?The Government needs an Industrial Strategy in order to rebalance the economy and drive growth. Industry needs a competitive and supportive infrastructure to enable it to compete in global markets. The competitive infrastructure needs to address energy, key enabling technologies, investment, fiscal incentives, regulation and skills. How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy, for example in preventing foreign takeovers of UK companies?Government needs to consider the impact on the UK industry of the loss of any critical part of the infrastructure such as critical mass in research and development, intellectual property and skills.What lessons can be learnt from:• Previous governments' industrial strategies?• Other countries' attempts to develop industrial strategies?The Industrial Strategy needs to have a long term vision with a roadmap set out of how to deliver this vision. This vision needs to be sustainable through political cycles. A cross-party approach including involvement from Key Stakeholders from industry is vital to ensure the Industrial Strategy is supported and sustainable. An example of a well implemented Strategy is China's Made in China 2025 plan which sets out a long term vision but then details high level targets. It provides a framework which allows Government to focus on delivering the aspects relating to regulation, fiscal incentives etc. and allows industry to focus on driving growth. What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions?There are tensions that already exist between policies, regulation and enabling a competitive industry. There is an opportunity, through the process of developing an Industrial Strategy, that these tensions can be addressed in a systematic manner and actions agreed to remove them. What are the pros and cons of an industrial strategy adopting a sectoral approach?• Should the Government proactively seek to ‘pick winners’?• What criteria should be used to identify which sectors are supported?• Should the Government prop up traditional industries that it considers to be in the national interest?• If not a sectoral approach, should the industrial strategy have a broader objective, such as improving productivity?All sectors need to be addressed in the first instance as a whole. Lack of clarity as to how the industry is meshed together and the key inter-dependencies will lead to the wrong decisions being made. There are key sectors that underpin other sectors and these need to be supported. From a mapping of these interdependencies, the growth potential for each sector and the drivers for growth then sectors could be highlighted for additional support but this can only be done in the context of the whole of industry.Should the industrial strategy have a geographical emphasis?• How should an industrial strategy link with devolution initiatives aimed at devolving taxation and decision making away from Westminster?• What examples are there of interventions from central Government that have successfully supported economic growth away from London and the South East of England?• How should the industrial strategy work with local authorities and Local Economic Partnerships, reconciling a U.K.-wide strategy and local, regional and devolved nations' priorities?Industry is spread geographically across the UK, with job, skills, resources and capabilities in different regions. Prominent regional areas are the North East, North West, Scotland, Humberside and Yorkshire and the South East. There needs to be an overarching national plan, but with a regional delivery focus. This will aid in the development of the regional agendas and, will help government allocate resources and will ensure focus and alignment across the UK. 30 September 2016 [1] https://www.pwc.co.uk/assets/pdf/ukmanufacturing-300309.pdf  [2] http://www.efpia.eu/uploads/Modules/Documents/the-pharmaceutical-industry-in-figures-2016 [3] Cefic  [4] Centre for Strategic and International Studies – Article ‘Made in China 2025’ - 1 June 2015 https://www.csis.org/analysis/made-china-2025 [5] Source DECC 30/06/2016Prices converted to pounds sterling using annual average exchange rates.Prices include all taxes where not refundable on purchase.Some prices taken from Eurostat.Prices excluding taxes have been estimated using a weighted average of general sales taxes and fuel taxes levied by individual states. [6] US Energy Information Administration[7] Ineos press release - Grangemouth to process imported shale gas after £150m investment - 4 December 2013 - http://www.ineos.com/Sites/Grangemouth/In-The-News/STV-news-Rafnes[8] The University of York – The Essential Chemical Industry online-  http://essentialchemicalindustry.org/chemicals.html  [9] REACH is a relatively new piece of legislation focussed on the supply and use of chemicals in the single market. As chemicals are used in pretty much all manufacturing sectors, the legislation impacts nearly all manufacturers in the UK in some way.  [10] Science Industry Partnership (SIP) - The Demand for Skills in the UK Science Economy:http://www.scienceindustrypartnership.com/media/529050/sip_science_industry_demand_for_skills_final.pdf [11]  http://ukchemistrygrowth.com/Partnership.aspx[12]  https://www.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/536388/bis-16-18-agri-tech-industrial-strategy-evaluation-and-baseline.pdf[13] Accenture’s Chemical Industry Vision 2016 report - https://www.accenture.com/t20160609T025416__w__/us-en/_acnmedia/PDF-22/Accenture-Chemical-Vision-2016.pdf#zoom=50

ISG0079 - Evidence on Industrial strategyWritten evidence from the Specialist Engineering Contractors’ (SEC) Group (ISG 79) The SEC Group represents the largest sector (by value) in the UK construction industry.  The specialist engineering sector comprises over 60,000 firms (the overwhelming majority are SMEs) employing approximately 300,000 people.  SEC Group is an umbrella representative body representing the industry’s premier trade associations: British Constructional Steelwork AssociationBuilding and Engineering Services AssociationElectrical Contractors’ AssociationLift and Escalator Industry AssociationPlumbing and Heating Contractors’ AllianceSELECT Question 1.  What does the Government mean by industrial strategy, and what does the private sector want from one? 1.1         At this stage it is impossible to guess the Government’s intentions.  However, the Government’s industrial strategy should, in the view of the SEC Group, seek primarily to promote economic growth, deliver a highly skilled and adaptable workforce and remove the obstacles to productivity.  Many of the issues germane to this question were highlighted in the Government’s 2015 Productivity Plan, which sought to address the underlying reasons for productivity failure in the UK economy.   1.2    The major weakness in the Government’s Productivity Plan was that it was too generic and failed to address the reasons for low productivity in each of the major sectors of UK industry.  Our concern is that the construction sector which, whilst contributing 6.7% to GDP – and critical to the future growth and prosperity of UK Plc – should be given some priority in the proposed industrial strategy. 1.3         The reasons for the UK’s lower productivity levels are multifarious and each sector has its particular problems which detract from achieving improved productivity.  It is all too easy to fall back on reasons such as the lack of R&D and innovation or the lack of appropriate skills without considering the underlying structural weaknesses that act as barriers to improved productivity.  At Annex 1 we suggest a way forward for improving construction productivity.  Overall it is imperative that the construction industry becomes leaner and fitter in order to meet the post-Brexit challenges, whatever they may be. 1.4         Currently the most challenging issue facing construction and the specialist engineering sector is skills shortages.  The Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors has estimated that this will affect 27,000 projects up to the end of the decade.  A year ago the Treasury’s National Infrastructure Plan for Skills indicated that the infrastructure pipeline will require 100,000 additional workers by 2020. This problem of skills shortages is also spilling over into specialised sectors such as renewables and utilities.     Question 2.  How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy, for example in preventing foreign takeovers of UK companies? 2.    If the Government wishes to effect change through the promulgation of an industrial strategy it will need to be interventionist in order to achieve the necessary change.  This is the logical consequence of having any strategy; otherwise the strategy becomes rather meaningless.  In Annex 1 we highlight the extent of interventionism required to improve construction growth and productivity.  The purpose of intervention should be to stimulate and achieve long-term behavioural change. Question 3.  What lessons can be learnt from: Previous governments’ industrial strategies?Other countries’ attempts to develop industrial strategies? 3.1    Unless the matters discussed in Annex 1 are adequately addressed in the Government’s industrial strategy, productivity levels in construction will remain below those of other sectors and could serve to undermine the key objective of economic growth.  Given the industry’s critical importance to UK Plc this shouldn’t be an option.  But, unfortunately the Government seems to have relegated to the sidelines the previous coalition government’s industry strategy for construction, Construction 2025.  This could be rejuvenated with an effective delivery body.  Without such a properly resourced delivery body an industrial strategy is unlikely to have significant impact.  The other key point that arises is that there has been a lot of changing priorities between governments over the years and so long term planning has been very difficult in this environment.  A plea from us would be for long term consistency in a strategy that would provide some comfort that there was a stable background against which to plan.  3.2    Industrial strategies in developed countries take many forms from general monetary and fiscal measures to stimulating the economy by focusing on specific sectors.  Our preference is for the Government to prioritise sectors that are in need of substantially improving their competitiveness and productivity levels.  In particular we would suggest a more interventionist approach to deal with poor practices in the construction sector that generate wasteful and non-value added activity.  This depresses margins for SMEs and adds unnecessary costs for the industry’s clients.  The mantra should be better value for less rather than less for more. Question 4.  What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions? It’s difficult to answer this in a vacuum when, as yet, the objectives of the proposed strategy have not been revealed.     Question 5.  What are the pros and cons of an industrial strategy adopting a sectoral approach?  Should the Government proactively seek to “pick winners”?The Government should pick sectors that are critical to the future prosperity of UK Plc  Construction is such a sector because the facilities and infrastructure it provides are essential in promoting economic growth and the well-being of the population. What criteria should be used to identify which sectors are supported?A key criterion should be the potential for developing and exploiting cutting edge technologies.  Within the specialist engineering sector there have been major developments in renewables technologies, digital engineering and smart technologies such as intelligent building controls, security and energy management systems.  Developing the full potential of all these technologies requires substantial investment by engineering firms, particularly in engaging the necessary skills and upskiling their existing workforces. As a society our largest investment is in the built environment.  Well-engineered facilities operating efficiently and cost-effectively also contribute to improving the productivity of the people working within them. Unfortunately the built environment still contributes almost half of the UK’s carbon dioxide emissions.  Due to the lack of front-end collaboration between the different parts of the supply chain, whole-life costs (primarily maintenance and operational costs) remain unnecessarily high for many facilities.  Recent studies have indicated that energy consumption can be up to 10% higher than that contemplated at design stage. The specialist engineering sector is pivotal in addressing these issues; but radical changes in procurement and financial security for the sector are necessary to ensure that firms can respond to the challenges.  Given the Brexit vote it is vital that these changes are made in the short to medium-term to enable firms to become more competitive and to reduce their costs resulting from poor and outdated practices. Should the Government prop up traditional industries that it considers to be in the national interest?Yes, but we dislike the reference to “prop up”.  Whenever industries stop operating in a purely commercial environment, they inevitably become less efficient which is not desirable. We would be seeking proactive support from the Government to overcome barriers to poor productivity in construction. If not a sectoral approach, should the industrial strategy have a broader objective, such as improving productivity?Yes.  A broader objective of improving productivity can run in tandem with a sectoral approach.       Question 6.  Should the industrial strategy have a geographical emphasis. How should an industrial strategy link with devolution initiatives aimed at devolving taxation and decision making away from Westminster? We would suggest a devolved approach to the development of an industrial strategy.  Each devolved government has its own priorities and the pace of economic growth varies widely within the UK.  For example, the growth of the construction industry in Northern Ireland has lagged far behind that of the rest of the UK.  Scotland intends to invest £20bn over the next 5 years in infrastructure facilities and, therefore, is likely to give added focus to ensuring that the construction industry delivers cost effective outcomes. What examples are there of interventions from central Government that have successfully supported economic growth away from London and the South East of England? The Cardiff Capital Region (CCR) City Deal is one example.  This is a £1.2bn investment in infrastructure in South Wales.  The UK Government is contributing £500m to the CCR Investment Fund.  The Deal could mean up to 25,000 jobs and realise approximately £4bn in private sector investment. How should the industrial strategy work with local authorities and Local Economic Partnerships, reconciling a UK wide strategy and local, regional and devolved nations’ priorities? Our preference is to focus on improvements to business and procurement practices in the construction industry in order to improve on the industry’s low levels of productivity.  As we have already indicated the devolved governments could be encouraged to reflect this approach through their own industry strategies. Nonetheless the industrial strategy should resolve the current uncertainties over whether there will be regional powerhouses.  We fully support the concept of regional powerhouses since they would be better placed to prioritise their infrastructure needs, to leverage the necessary funding and (within the national strategy) develop their own min-strategies that will allow them to deploy available resources to best effect. We also believe that having regional powerhouses will provide greater opportunities for innovation in procurement approaches that enable better value to be delivered for less.  September 2016Annex 1  ELEMENTS OF AN INDUSTRIAL STRATEGY FOR CONSTRUCTON  An industrial strategy, (that includes construction) should address the gap between productivity levels in UK construction and those in most other sectors.  According to the latest ONS statistics this gap is growing.  The prime reasons are: major structural weaknesses in the process by which the industry’s outputs are procured and delivered;lack of barriers to entry to the industry, andbusiness models in the industry being driven by cash flow manipulation and the need to offload risk.   The previous coalition government introduced sectoral industry strategies including a construction strategy (Construction 2025).  Targets were set but there was little attempt to measure progress.  Moreover there wasn’t an effective delivery body.  A Construction Leadership Council was established but this was more of a “sounding board” than a delivery body. Procurement and Delivery Unlike many other sectors, construction firms deliver their outputs in conjunction with other firms as part of the process of delivering projects.  Most firms have little influence on how projects are procured, on the design solutions and on the risk management processes.  As part of a very disaggregated and fragmented supply chain they have to work around project practices and processes which are often inefficient and positively harmful to their businesses.   By way of example the cost of re-design and re-work due to design shortcomings and poor or late information tends to fall on SMEs in the supply chain.  Construction collaboration platform GenieBelt.com has estimated that £2billion is spent annually on re-work; this comes at a time when the industry is complaining of skills shortages.  But this wasteful activity detracts from investment in the technologies and skills necessary to raise productivity levels. As far as government procurement is concerned an attempt was made to address this state of affairs by the Cabinet Office in 2011 when it published its Construction Strategy.  The Strategy recommended the piloting of three model procurement routes.  The updated 2016 Strategy (Government Construction Strategy 2016-2020) continues to advocate the piloting of these models in order to obtain efficiency savings.  One of these was Integrated Project Insurance (IPI).  Much of the developmental work for IPI was resourced by SEC Group.  It facilitates a far more efficient approach to procurement and delivery by allowing for early appointment of supply chain firms to “buy into” the key decisions on design, planning, risk and cost.  The emphasis is on collaborative working with the team coming under one insurance umbrella.  The insurance policy underwrites the cost plan subject to an excess shared by team members.[1] Whilst this initiative (alongside the other two model procurement options) remains the best hope for improving construction procurement (and, thus, the productivity of the firms involved), there has been little enthusiasm on the part of government to put forward pilot projects.  However the first IPI pilot is now underway and it is hoped that others will follow.  ALL DEPARTMENTS AND AGENCIES (AND THE WIDER PUBLIC SECTOR) SHOULD BE NOMINATING PROJECTS IN THE PUBLIC SECTOR PIPELINE FOR PILOTING EACH OF THE MODEL PROCUREMENT ROUTES SET OUT IN THE 2016 CONSTRUCTION STRATEGY.  Lack of barriers to entry into the construction industry  In 1944 the Simon Report (The Placing and Management of Building Contracts) lamented the lack of barriers to entry to the construction industry which resulted in a culture of lowest prices and lowest standards.  This has not fundamentally changed in the 72 years since publication of this Report.   “The City regards construction as a business that is unpredictable, competitive only on price not quality, with too few barriers to entry for poor performers”  para.6, Chap.1, Rethinking Construction, 1998, Sir John Egan. Reputable SMEs in the industry regularly complain that they do not compete on a “level planning field”.  They are disincentivised to invest to any great extent in the drivers of productivity – such as upskilling of staff, innovation and technological advances - because they are often out-bid by firms which offer lower prices; since such firms have little or no interest in investing in training or technology.  This problem is exacerbated by the high incidence of false self-employment in the industry. Furthermore the general lack of enforcement of existing regulations governing standards in the industry only serves to encourage entry by businesses which often do not intend to comply with the relevant standards. The majority of the States in the United States (where productivity levels are higher than in the UK) have corporate licensing schemes.  To obtain work a contractor must be licensed.  This means that it is able to demonstrate that it possesses the requisite technical standards, its health and safety performance is satisfactory and it is appropriately resourced.  GOVERNMENT SHOULD WORK WITH THE CONSTRUCTION INDUSTRY TO DEVISE PROPOSALS FOR CREATING A LEVEL PLAYING FIELD FOR COMPETITION.  TO BEGIN THIS PROCESS THE GOVERNMENT SHOULD RATIONALISE THE VARIOUS STATUTORY AND QUASI-STATUTORY COMPETENCE/QUALITY SCHEMES APPLICABLE TO CONSTRUCTION FIRMS AND THEN CONSIDER WHETHER SUCH RATIONALISATION SHOULD FORM THE BASIS OF A STANDARD QUALIFICATION SCHEME FOR FIRMS ENTERING THE INDUSTRY.  AS PART OF THIS EXERCISE CONSIDERATION SHOULD BE GIVEN TO IMPROVING ENFORCEMENT OF CURRENT REGULATIONS GOVERNING STANDARDS IN CONSTRUCTION.  FINALLY, CONSIDERATION SHOULD BE GIVEN TO LEGISLATION TO OVERCOME THE PREVALENCE OF FALSE SELF-EMPLOYMENT.   Construction Business Models 10.    Poor capitalisation inevitably drives the commercial imperative for many of the largest companies in construction.  Their focus is on placing all risks with SMEs in their supply chains; sub-contracting has become a vehicle solely for the purpose of effecting risk transfer.  Supply chains in construction are not treated as collaborative entities where all firms are working together to deliver the most cost effective project outcomes and, in so doing, are enabled to improve their respective productivity levels.  Wasteful activity generated by poor practices such as open tendering procedures (allowing all and sundry to tender); repetitive pre-qualification processes and onerous contracts add up to major barriers to firms intent on raising productivity.[2]  Their own internal efficiencies are constantly undermined by the external inefficiencies of the procurement and delivery processes. 11.    By far the greatest barrier to improving productivity in construction is the widespread abuse of the payment process – lengthy payment periods, late payment and spurious reasons for non-payment.  Since 2007 there have been approximately twenty initiatives aimed at addressing this problem.  Recent surveys have warned that this problem is now becoming more acute especially for small firms. “Construction companies registered more payment delays than any other single UK sector last year [2015]……..the sector suffered a 26% year-on-year rise in the number of overdue payment incidents in 2015 [i.e. overdue being more than 2 months after the payments should have been discharged].”  Euler Hermes Quarterly Overdue Payments Report (Euler Hermes is a trade credit insurance company). 12.    The only initiative which is having an impact is project bank accounts but only a small number of government departments are using them regularly.  Highways England is using them on all their projects with the result that sub-sub-contractors now receive their payments within 19 days of the assessment dates under the main contract.  If Highways England can achieve this, so can the whole of the public sector.  In its report in January 2015, Paying government suppliers on time, the NAO recommended that the use of project bank accounts should be widened. 13.    A particular practice which has a detrimental impact upon SME productivity is the retentions system.  At any one time approximately £3 billion is outstanding by way of retention monies.  These monies are deducted from due payments, ostensibly as security against defects.  In practice they support the cashflow of the parties deducting them (including public bodies).  14.    These monies actually belong in law to the firm from whom they have been deducted but they are at risk in the event of insolvencies up the supply chain.  Last year almost £50m worth of retention monies was lost by small firms as a result of upstream insolvencies!  If this £50m was to be restored to the industry the benefits in terms of improved skills levels, more apprentices taken on and increased investment in technology and plant could be substantial.The cashflow uncertainty in the industry presents a major obstacle to small firms when seeking to borrow from banks.  If, for example, cash retentions were protected in a secure account, they could be used as collateral for any additional borrowing: “The evidence shows that construction contracting SMEs face more difficulties than other SMEs in accessing finance from banks.”(UK CONSTRUCTION: An Economic Analysis of the Sector.  Published in July 2013 by the Business Department.)  15.    Bank lending to the construction contracting sector decreased both in absolute terms and relative to other sectors during the recession – from approximately £32.2 billion in early 2009 to £19.9 billion in December 2012.  This represents a fall of almost 40% compared with a fall of less than 5% on average across other industries! For many SMEs in the industry the only way to source additional credit is to persuade suppliers to extend their trade credit.  Alternatively they may be forced to seek out alternative credit facilities which may be far more expensive than the traditional sources of finance.  Furthermore the availability of trade credit has almost dried up in the industry.   Unless the cash flow security in the industry is improved there’s no reason to believe that the problems facing SMEs in accessing finance will reduce.  This has, of course, adverse implications for productivity.    THE WAY FORWARD PROJECT BANK ACCOUNTS ARE ADOPTED THROUGHOUT THE PUBLIC SECTOR IN ORDER TO GENERATE GREATER PRODUCTIVITY IN CONSTRUCTION SUPPLY CHAINS; ANDLEGISLATION SHOULD BE INTRODUCED TO REQUIRE THAT ALL CASH RETENTIONS ARE RING-FENCED.DIGITAL PAYMENT PROCESSES SHOULD BE INTRODUCED SO THAT ALL BUSINESSES CAN SEE PROGRESS ON DEALING WITH THEIR APPLICATIONS/INVOICES   The Integrated Project Insurance (“IPI”) Model Background and concept            Since Sir Michael Latham’s report “Constructing the Team” in 1994 and Sir John Egan’s report “Rethinking Construction” in 1998 the mission to achieve genuine integration and collaboration, as opposed to the traditional fragmentation and confrontation, on projects has been promoted by successive UK governments, the Strategic Forum for Construction, the National Audit Office, and the Select Committee “Construction Matters”. But what all the reports recommend rarely happens in practice. Even where discerning clients promote integrated teams and collaborative cultures the “silo” structures remain, particularly down the supply chains. Even the more progressive clients are complaining that they do not get proper access to the supply chains –where over 80% of the costs are incurred. There is still a long way to go to achieve truly “lean” integrated delivery. The Specialist Engineering Alliance[3], supported by “movers & shakers” across the breadth of the construction and insurance industries, took action on the opportunity highlighted in “Never waste a good crisis”[4] to make a proposition to Government that the supply side can “deliver more for less”. The proposal, which was supported by 30+ practices and companies (large and SME) across the breadth of the supply chain, was in a nutshell: Select the integrated team - including specialists and key suppliers from the outset – on a balanced scorecard[5], not lowest price tendering. They are appointed under a no-blame/no claim “Alliance Agreement”  The integrated project team will then target to take 15% - 20% out of the cost by eliminating the waste of needless processes – leaving room for investment in low carbon/sustainability The agreed design solution and cost plan will have been subjected to “technical and financial risk assurance”, and if passed will be insured (with associated latent defects cover) under a new product called “Integrated Project Insurance” (“IPI”) Gain-share and pain-share will apply, and the maximum pain-share will equate to the excess under the IPI policy. Beyond that (and subject to a maximum indemnity and limited exclusions such as war) the insurers meet any overspend, waiving rights of subrogation. This means that each member of the team knows his maximum exposure is limited to his pre-agreed share of the pain-share, and does not need to build in unnecessary contingencies. The cost of IPI, including the associated independent facilitation and risk assurance, will be 2.5% where the project cost lies in the range of £10m - £25m – which is no more than the combined cost of the traditional CAR, PL and Professional Indemnity insurances it replaces throughout the integrated team and the supply chains. Government endorsement 4.              The “Government Construction Strategy”[6] published in May 2011 gave in principle endorsement of the IPI model for trial on pilot projects, adopting the target of up to 20% cost savings by the elimination of process waste.  5.              Integrated Project Initiatives Ltd was formed in July 2011, immediately following the publication of the Government Construction Strategy, to be the “delivery vehicle” for the IPI model in close association with the Cabinet Office. The Directors are Martin Davis and Kevin Thomas who were responsible for developing the IPI model in conjunction with leading insurance brokers Griffiths & Armour over the last decade. 6.      Guidance on the IPI model was published at the Construction Conference on 2 July 2014 and is available on the Government website[7]  Implementation through pilot projects 7.              The phase of pilot projects has at last commenced – which are to prove the proposition that 15% - 20% of the project cost – and significantly more in terms of time - can be saved under the IPI model. The first public sector pilot IPI project is a new Advanced Technology Building (“Advance2”) at Dudley College. The project, with an investment value of £11.6m, is being carried out under an Alliance Contract prepared for use with IPI, and procurement of the alliance team of designers, constructors, specialists and a project co-ordinator – to sit alongside the Client on the Alliance Board - was carried out in accordance with the EU Procurement Directives and accomplished in 6 months. The project has three phases: Phase 1 commenced on 8 May, and Phase 2 is expected to start, with the inception of the IPI policy, by the end of this year; Phase 3 starts with Completion, which has to be by end March 2017. 8.              Other projects in the public and private sectors are set to follow. R&D support via Innovate UK 9.              The process of testing and development of the IPI model is being supported by funding awarded by the Innovate UK under its “Rethinking the Build Process” competition. A consortium was formed comprising:                Rider Levett Bucknall (lead partner)               Integrated Project Initiatives Ltd               Thomas Telford/NEC               Laing O’Rourke               Stepnell               The University of Reading (academic partner)               BSRIA               Activeplan Consulting (software)               Griffiths & Armour (brokers for IPI) 10.              The Innovate UK project is of 4 years’ duration, and the 50% funding exceeds £750k. Principal objectives of this project are: Updating of the Integration Toolkit that was created for the Strategic Forum for Construction to make it an inter-active hub for modern practitioners wanting to understand and indeed influence the process, culture and tools for integrated collaborative working Trialling of an innovative Alliance Contract which embodies the processes of the IPI model, and with this experience, development of a final version which can hopefully become the first “NEC Alliance Contract” Trialling and development of the new Integrated Project Insurance product which, in addition to the usual CAR,PL and PI coverage, insures the agreed budget and gives latent defects cover for 12 years - which two leading insurers have already agreed to support on our first three pilot projects  11.              There are other supplementary objectives, including  Use of an appropriate level of BIM to assist a fully integrated collaborative approach (there is no liability problem because of IPI) Critical review of codes and standards,  and recording reasons why they inhibit innovation, sustainability, speed and efficiency Reviews of product selection, and whether criteria of whole life cost and sustainability are being applied 12.              This Innovate UK project is directly linked to the pilot IPI project at Dudley Advance2 – which in turn comes under the surveillance of the Trial Projects Delivery Group set up by the Cabinet Office (supported for arrangements for monitoring and mentoring). Everything is therefore in place to ensure that both successes and difficulties are identified, analysed, and lessons are learned so as to optimise future performance.  For further information on the IPI model and progress in proving it in readiness for roll-out, please contact Martin Davis at martin.davis@ipinitiatives.com Martin DavisIntegrated Project Initiatives                                                         June 2015Letter from SEC Group Chairman to the Prime Minister  The Rt. Hon. Theresa May MPPrime Minister10 Downing StreetLONDONSW1A 2AARef: TGH/RK/cc09 August 2016                  The Government’s Industrial Strategy I write as chairman of the Specialist Engineering Contractors’ (SEC) Group.  SEC Group represents the engineering sector in the UK construction industry; the sector contributes over 40% to the industry’s total output.  The overwhelming majority of firms in the sector are SMEs. First, I would like to commend you on setting up the new Economy and Industrial Strategy Committee to promote productivity and innovation in industries that will provide long-term competitive advantage for the UK.  We hope that construction is within the remit of the Committee given that it contributes over 6% to UK economic output and provides for 2 million jobs. In representing the largest sector (by value) in the industry we are keen to offer our support to the Committee as it seeks to develop an industry strategy.  The recent Markit/CIPS survey (the first since the BREXIT vote) indicates a continuing decline in construction activity with a significant slowdown in activity in the commercial buildings sector. Having run an engineering business for over 40 years it seems to me that the best response from the industry is to address its low levels of productivity.  To that end my suggestion would be that we have targeted and measurable action plans to raise productivity levels in all industry sectors. For construction such action plan would comprise three priorities: Overcoming the lack of cashflow security which has bedevilled the industry over many years – largely driven by poor capitalisation of the largest companies.   Cashflow security has not improved despite the introduction of many and various government-sponsored initiatives including formal guidance, charters and directives.  According to the National Audit Office (Paying government suppliers on time, January, 2015) this is equally applicable to central government procurement.  Replacing outdated and inefficient procurement and contracting practices with modern methods involving collaborative delivery techniques, increased off-site fabrication and the exploitation of digital technologies. Many of these objectives are already being promoted through the 2016 Government Construction Strategy and Trial Projects Working Group.  Given the amount of time and effort put into the process, it seems sensible to build on the work done to date.  The priority now is for government and industry enthusiasts to work together to drive the necessary changes and ensure that they are implemented.     Developing a level playing field for competition that encourages reputable SMEs to improve output through more efficient use of existing resources.  Ease of entry into the industry, false self-employment and lack of regulatory enforcement contribute to poor skills levels and ultimately to poor quality outcomes. I and industry sector colleagues would be very happy to develop this proposal with your policy advisers  when convenient but suggest this is best achieved through meeting to discuss practical steps which can deliver real progress towards realising your objectives. I look forward to receiving your response and assure you that our specialist engineering sector is committed to supporting the Committee’s work in any way thought appropriate. I am copying this letter to Greg Clark, Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy and to Lord O’Neill, SEC Group President.        Trevor Hursthouse OBEChairman, Specialist Engineering Contractors’ Group Cc:              Rt Hon Greg Clark MP              Lord O’Neill, President, SEC Group   [1] A short briefing on IPI is attached at Annex 2 to this submission.[2] In 1994, Sir Michael Latham recommended in his report, Constructing the Team that there should be one central register of all firms pre-qualified to carry out public sector work.  This was aimed at reducing the need for firms to fill in lengthy questionnaires time and time again.  This recommendation was never actioned.  Consequently construction firms have wasted £20 billion since the Latham Report in repetitive pre-qualification activity![3] The Specialist Engineering Alliance comprises CIBSE, BEAMA, ACE, BSRIA FETA and SEC Group[4] www.constructingexcellence.org.uk/news/article.jsp?id=10886 [5] As set out in the Construction Industry Council’s “Selecting the Team” see www.strategicforum.org.uk [6] https://www.gov.uk/government/uploads/system/uploads/attachment_data/file/61152/Government-Construction-Strategy_0.pdf  Please refer to paragraphs 2.28 and 2.44 and Action Plan items 6 and ISG0127 - Evidence on Industrial strategy  Written evidence from Policy Links, Centre for Science and Technology Innovation Policy (CSTI) University Cambridge (ISG 127)  EXECUTIVE SUMMARY•         Opportunities exist to more systematically collect, integrate and interpret evidence for industrial strategy. There is a need for representations, going beyond sector and technology boundaries, that more accurately describe the way in which modern economic activities are organised.
