A better understanding of industrial structures can help ensure that the legitimate interests of the whole range of actors that underpin industrial competiveness are appropriately accounted for. Similarly, a more sophisticated appreciation of industrial and technological interdependencies can help ensure that evidence across relevant industrial policy areas, including technology, manufacturing and skills, more clearly ‘talk to each other’. A clear area for opportunity is the coordination and integration of any potential successors of ‘Future of Manufacturing’ projects, ‘Great Technology’ studies, and ‘Sector Strategies’. •         There is value in considering manufacturing as a cross-cutting theme in industrial strategies. The linkages and interdependencies between manufacturing and innovation are increasingly recognised in the international policy community. Asking common manufacturing questions across industries can be extremely useful to explore themes relevant to national competitiveness, including: interdependence of manufacturing and innovation, requirements for manufacturing employment in high wage economies, and sustainability trade-offs. Countries like South Korea and Japan, for example, consider manufacturing a cross-sectoral theme and have established institutional set-ups that allow them to systematically capture common manufacturing issues across industries and technologies. •         There is a need for institutional capability that helps capturing ‘bottom-up’ knowledge while enabling ‘top-down’ coordination. There might be value in reviewing the contribution of national institutions not only as ‘honest brokers’ acting at arm’s-length from government, but also as ‘privileged observers’ taking advantage of their industrial expertise and networks. Germany, for example, benefits from well-established processes and institutions (from chambers of commerce and regional innovation centres to national academies) built over many years that allow eliciting insights from a range of self-identified industrial communities in an effective and timely manner. There might also be value in interagency forums in ensuring awareness and alignment of policies in key national innovation areas (such as the interagency working groups for key technologies in the US at the National Science and Technology Council).Opportunities exist to more systematically collect, integrate and interpret evidence for industrial strategy.1.1.   There is a need to rethink the way we represent economic activity. Modern economic systems are becoming increasingly complex and interdependent. Challenges and opportunities are constantly evolving due to issues such as increasing global competition, disruptive technologies and changing market demands. Traditional classifications of sectors and technologies are increasingly unable to fully reflect the structures and interdependencies of modern economic activity. As software and services become ever more integrated into products, for example, a number of ‘unmeasurable sectors’ such as embedded software and the ‘app economy’ are simply not monitored.  Instead of more complex statistical analyses based on traditional sectoral and technological boundaries, new approaches are necessary to more accurately represent the way in which modern economic activities are organised, and to understand the key issues enabling (and hindering) competiveness, value creation and value capture. In the US, for example, calls are being made for novel approaches to better understand economic activity through the modelling of ‘value networks’[1]. 1.2.   A better understanding of industrial structures can help ensure inclusiveness. Better representations of economic activity can enable a more systematic engagement with the whole range of actors underpinning industrial competitiveness. In most modern industries these include not only final assemblers but also, critically, production technologies providers, component and materials suppliers, complementary technology producers and even competitors and customers. In the US and Germany, for example, efforts tend to be made to systematically engage with stakeholders in an inclusive way, with the list of those consulted widely published. Inclusive industrial strategy requires ensuring that the legitimate interests of sectors, sub-sector, and technology communities are appropriately and comprehensively accounted for. 1.3.   Opportunities exist to more effectively integrate evidence informing industrial strategy and related policy areas. There are opportunities to better structure evidence across policy areas relevant to industrial strategy, including technology, manufacturing and skills, so that they more clearly ‘talk to each other’. Three key areas for potential improvement include: developing more integrated approaches to data collection and analysis; more effectively and appropriately tapping into qualitative evidence and expertise; and more effectively sharing evidence accumulated know-how within and between the Department for Business, Energy & Industrial Strategy (BEIS) units and agencies. A clear area for opportunity is the coordination and integration of any successors of GO-Science’s ‘Foresight Future of Manufacturing’ project, ‘Great Technology’ studies, and BEIS’ ‘Sector Strategies’. All this has implications for inter-agency collaboration and structure, sequencing of initiatives, and efficiency of information sharing.    There is value in considering manufacturing as a cross-cutting theme in industrial strategies.2.1.   Manufacturing is an enabler of innovation across industries. The linkages and interdependencies between manufacturing and technological innovation are increasingly recognised. High profile studies that analyse the negative effect of the deterioration in a nation’s manufacturing capabilities on its ability to innovate new generations of technology-based products have received significant attention from the international policy community. Such studies have found that once production activities are off-shored, it may not be possible to retain critical process-engineering expertise and other know-how (which are sustained by regular interactions with manufacturing)[2]. Without these capabilities, it becomes much harder for firms to carry out advanced R&D on next-generation process technologies, which in turn means they may lose the ability to develop such new processes, and ultimately the ability to develop innovative new products and generate new business opportunities[3].2.2.   A manufacturing perspective can reveal common issues across industries. Considering manufacturing as a cross-cutting theme provides an opportunity to address issues (challenges, opportunities, strengths, weaknesses) which cannot be fully revealed by analysing individual technologies or particular industry sectors alone. This approach can provide a better understanding of industry-specific implications of cross-cutting trends and the extent to which concerns and opportunities are shared across industries. Asking common manufacturing questions across industries can be extremely useful to explore themes relevant to national competitiveness, including: interdependence of manufacturing and innovation; requirements for manufacturing employment in high wage economies; sustainability trade-offs; and the evolving global distribution of value added. 2.3.   Mechanisms can be put into place to ensure that the cross-cutting nature of manufacturing is accounted for in industrial strategy. There are opportunities to ensure that common manufacturing themes are appropriately accounted as part of the evidence-gathering process informing industrial strategy. Countries like South Korea and Japan, for example, consider manufacturing a cross-sectoral theme rather than a sector in its own right. In such countries, significant efforts are made to carry out manufacturing analyses annually, which consist of cross-sectoral reviews of challenges and opportunities. In some ministries (e.g. Japan’s Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry), the manufacturing unit is parent of ‘sector units’ and systematically captures cross-sectoral issues.There is a need for institutional capability that helps capturing ‘bottom-up’ knowledge while enabling ‘top-down’ coordination. 3.1.   National institutions can help capturing ‘bottom-up’ industrial evidence in an efficient and transparent way. The national institutional infrastructure can play a critical role in eliciting information from industrial communities ‘bottom-up’, beyond what would be possible by government alone. Germany, for example, benefits from well-established processes and institutions (from chambers of commerce and regional innovation centres to national academies) built over many years that allow eliciting insights from a range of self-identified industrial communities in an effective and timely manner. There might be opportunities to review the contribution that such institutions in the UK may be able to make not only as ‘honest brokers’ acting at arm’s-length from government, but also as ‘privileged observers’ taking advantage of their industrial expertise and networks.3.2.   A variety of institutional structures, mechanisms and strategies can help ensure ‘top-down’ coordination of policy delivery across relevant areas. Significant efforts are required to ensure effective coordination across policy areas. Potentially useful insights can be gained from historical international experience in terms of the variety of structures and mechanisms that have been put into place to support this aim (including national committees, specialised policymaking units and ‘superministries’). Recent interagency working groups/units with the aim of coordinating policy efforts across government can be observed in countries like the US (including the Advanced Manufacturing Office at the National Institute of Standards and Technology) and in Japan (including the Council for Science, Technology and Innovation at the Cabinet Office). National plans and foresight studies suggesting long-term visions can also play an important role in aligning/coordinating priorities across government (e.g. Basic Plans in Japan, High-Tech Strategy in Germany, President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology’s Reports in the US).3.3.   Opportunities exist to more systematically share practices across relevant government departments and agencies. There might opportunities to adopt formal and informal processes to more effectively share evidence and knowledge accumulated within the Department for Business, Energy & Industrial Strategy (BEIS), its agencies, and other relevant government areas. In the US, for example, central policy units and interagency working groups provide forums for federal agencies and departments to continuously ensure awareness and alignment of policies in key national innovation areas (examples include the National Science and Technology Council’s interagency working groups for key technologies, such as the National Nanotechnology Initiative). Policy Links is the knowledge exchange unit of the Centre for Science, Technology & Innovation Policy (CSTI), University of Cambridge. It aims to support the work of policy officials and civil servants working in the areas of technology, manufacturing and innovation policy by generating novel evidence, tools and insights grounded in the latest academic research.This note was written by Dr. Carlos Lopez-Gomez, head of Policy Links, drawing heavily from the work of researchers at the Centre for Science, Technology & Innovation Policy (CSTI). All errors are the author's.4 [1] NAE (2015). Making Value for America: Embracing the Future of Manufacturing, Technology, and Work. Committee on Foundational Best Practices for Making Value for America. National Academy of Engineering.[2] Pisano, G.P., Shih, W.C. (2009). Restoring American Competitiveness, Harvard Business Review; Berger, S. (2011). Why Manufacturing Matters, Technology Review.[3] Fuchs, E., & Kirchain, R. (2010). Design for location? The impact of manufacturing offshore on technology competitiveness in the optoelectronics industry. Management Science, 56(12), 2323.

ISG0191 - Evidence on Industrial strategy Written evidence from Arts Council England (ISG 191) About Arts Council England 1.1.   Arts Council England is the national development agency for the arts, museums and libraries in England. Our work is directed by Great art and culture for everyone, our 10-year development strategy for the sector[1].  1.2.  We invest public funds in great art and culture for everyone, boosting our world-leading creative industries, the UK’s global reputation, regional regeneration, education, health and wellbeing. 1.3.  In 2015 - 18, we will invest £1.1bn from government and an estimated £700m from the National Lottery[2]. We invest through three main strands:National Portfolio Organisations (NPOs) and Major Partner Museums (MPMs), the backbone of England’s cultural infrastructure. Grants for the arts, our rolling, open access grants programme.Strategic Funds, to target specific gaps, challenges and opportunities. 1.4.  We also invest £75m p.a. from the Department for Education in 121 Music Education Hubs and support the strategic development of public libraries. 1.5.  The Arts Council is committed to balancing funding across England, increasing diversity and investing in areas of historic low engagement in the arts. By 2018, 75% of our National Lottery funding will be invested outside London.  What does the Government mean by industrial strategy, and what does the private sector want from one? 2.1.  The Government says its industrial strategy will identify “industries that are of strategic value to our economy” and promote them “through policies on trade, tax, infrastructure, skills, training, and research and development”[3].  2.2. Given their growth potential, role in regeneration, trade and tourism, and contribution to other industries, the cultural and creative industries should be recognised in the industrial strategy and policies enacted to support them. This can build on the valuable work of the Creative Industries Council.  2.3. We welcomed the Prime Minister’s reference in her Party Conference speech to the creative industries as an industry of strategic importance. The new BEIS Secretary said that the creative industries “set the global gold standard”[4]. 2.4.                    Publically-funded arts and culture are integral to the success of the creative industries. Arts and culture contribute £27bn to the UK economy, employ 642,000 people and incubate talent that drives the commercial sector. Public funding enables innovation, such as the R&D for West End transfers War Horse and Matilda. Reports by the Centre for Economic and Business Research demonstrate significant economic spillovers, through tourism, regional regeneration, increases in productivity and culture’s contribution to the creative industries[5]. 2.5. Culture can deliver the Government’s commitment to growth outside London. The Culture White Paper noted “cultural place-making can shape the fortunes of our regions, cities, towns and villages”[6]. Investment in infrastructure such as the Lowry and BALTIC was the starting point for the regeneration. A recent report by NESTA shows creative clusters driving growth across the country[7]. 2.6.                    Arts and culture have long been a source of soft power, the means to build relationships and enhance the UK’s reputation aboard. They can show the world that Britain is open for dialogue and business post-referendum.   2.7. The creative industries are a significant part of the UK economy, generating £87.4bn (5.3% GVA) in 2015 and growing faster than the economy as a whole. They represent 9.1% of UK exports and are a major source of employment, supporting 1.9m jobs and 248,800 enterprises (12% of UK total) in 2015[8].  2.8. Creative skills are vital to the creative and other strategic industries, including science, technology and engineering. The Government’s Chief Scientific advisor said “it’s STEAM that we put out, and by that I mean science, technology, engineering, arts and mathematics. All this [new technology] only adds value to our lives if it is well-designed and easy to use.”[9] 2.9. As the national development agency for arts and culture, the Arts Council intervenes strategically to support the sector across England. We target investment to enable culture to drive economic growth outside London and work with LEPs to embed culture in their Strategic Economic Plans. We are ideally placed to work with Government, the Creative Industries Council and other partners on the strategy for the cultural and creative industries.  How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy? 3.1.  The Government should consider the following areas in the development of an industrial strategy for the cultural and creative industries:  Sustained public investment3.2. Publically-funded arts and culture contribute to the UK economy in their own right and nurture the talent and ideas that drive the wider creative industries.  3.3. Local authority funding for arts, museums and libraries has decreased by £236m since 2010. While the Arts Council received a good settlement in the 2015 Spending Review, we do not have the resources to replace this. Further cuts would harm the sector and its contribution to growth, jobs and skills. Support for local growth and creative clusters3.4. The arts are often the starting point for local growth: creative businesses increased by 8% during Liverpool’s year as Capital of Culture. New creative clusters are emerging in cities such as Sunderland, Hull and Bournemouth. The strategy should support the growth of creative clusters across England. 3.5. A joined up approach to public investment enables the cultural industries to generate growth and leads to potential commercial success. For example, the Arts Council and Visit England’s £3m Cultural Destinations programme enables arts and tourism organisations to grow the economic contribution of local visitor economies.     International collaboration and trade3.6. Arts and culture can play a vital role in showing that Britain is open for business post-referendum. British Council research found involvement with British culture is strongly associated with interest in doing business here[10].  3.7. The Creative Industries accounted for 9.1% of total exports of services from the UK in 2015. The strategy should consider further support for trade in the cultural and creative industries. Creative education3.8. A high-quality cultural education, sitting alongside science, technology, engineering and maths, is essential to inspiring the next generation of creative talent. The industrial strategy should make provision for creative education in and outside school and clear pathways to Higher and Further Education.  3.9. The strategy should advocate STEAM and give an improved status to arts, design, creative problem-solving, and innovation in schools and colleges. New, Arts Council-supported local Cultural Education Partnerships will provide focus for new thinking about creative education to nurture home-grown talent.   Higher education, training and skills3.10.       The success of the cultural and creative industries relies on an appropriately skilled workforce. The industrial strategy should support creative Higher and Further Education, training, skills and employment.  3.11.         HE and FE institutions are key partners to the creative industries, educating over 166,000 students on specialist creative courses each year and nurturing the creative thinking that drives high-performing, high-growth industries.  3.12.        Universities are increasingly important investors in arts and culture e.g.  Teesside University runs mima (Middlesbrough Institute of Modern Art) and the University of the West of England collaborates with Arnolfini, Bristol. We have produced a guide with the University Alliance to encourage partnerships.   3.13.        Creative HE and FE faces a number of challenges. Some courses have been lost and there is a threat to EU research funding post-referendum. Creative employers have also reported that some graduates are not ‘job ready’. The use of unpaid work experience or lengthy internships and the low level of apprenticeships exacerbates skills gaps and reduces talent mobility.  3.14.        The Government’s industrial strategy should support creative HE and FE and address skills gaps. Arts Council initiatives in this area include:  £10.2m Creative Employment Programme, which created 4,380 apprenticeships, paid internships and traineeships for young people[11].An ambition to support a range of new apprenticeships in the culture sector, including higher and degree level, as part of the Government’s commitment to 3 million apprenticeships by 2020.  Arts Fundraising Fellowship, which equipped emerging creative leaders with skills to reduce reliance on public funding: 80% were outside London. Museums Resilience Leadership programme, which helps museums leaders gain the skills to face local authority cuts. Diversity3.15.        The Arts Council’s Creative Case for Diversity makes clear, that in order for the cultural and creative industries to be strong, effective and sustainable, their leadership and workforce must reflect the diversity of our society. Encouraging diversity should be at the heart of the Government’s strategy for the creative industries.  3.16.        Diversity runs through all our investment. Our funded organisations are now responsible for delivering and reporting on progress on diversity, and we provide targeted support such as Change Makers, a £2.6m fund to develop black and minority ethnic and disabled leaders through leadership training.  Tax reliefs and financing 3.17.        Successive governments have recognised the economic value of the cultural industries through tax reliefs for film, TV, animation, video games and theatre, and forthcoming reliefs for orchestras, museums and galleries. Theatres received £25m in reliefs in the first full year of the theatre tax relief. The strategy should support existing reliefs and explore potential additions.  3.18.       New finance models could drive growth in the sector. The Arts Council is exploring innovative financing options, such as the Arts Impact Fund, a £7m initiative to demonstrate the value of social investment in arts and culture.  Digital3.19.        Digital technologies transform how we make, distribute, and exchange arts and culture. The Royal Exchange Theatre’s Hamlet was screened to 310 UK cinemas. The industrial strategy should explore digital opportunities, increasing culture’s reach and economic contribution.  3.20.      The Arts Council will invest £26m in creative media activities in 2015-18. Our Digital R&D Fund for the Arts enabled arts organisations to collaborate with tech companies and researchers to explore new ways to reach audiences or generate income through digital innovation.  What lessons can be learnt from previous governments’ strategies? 4.1.  The previous government’s industrial strategy did not initially recognise the significance of the cultural and creative industries. We welcomed the creation of the joint BIS/DCMS Creative Industries Council in 2013 and have played a role in its work to develop a strategy for the sector. The new industrial strategy should build on the work of the Creative Industries Council. What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions? 5.1.  Local governmentChanges to local government’s financial settlement through business rates reform and the impact of devolution may affect how local authorities support arts and culture. Given the importance of local government to the arts, creative industries and tourism, the implications of these changes should be taken into account.   5.2. Planning, Regulation, licensing, business rates, etc.The Arts Council is exploring how inflexibility in the legislative and regulatory system restricts innovation. Greater collaboration on planning between the arts, business and communities could encourage growth of jobs and services. 5.3. Exiting the European UnionThe Arts Council asked the cultural sector post-referendum how leaving the European Union might affect them. We will publish the results of our survey soon.  What are the pros and cons of an industrial strategy adopting a sectoral approach?  6.1.  The Arts Council supports a sectoral approach in which the government recognises sectors of strategic importance and enacts policies to support them. Criteria to identify these sectors could include economic growth rate, contribution to other government policy objectives (e.g. social, educational), global reach, use of digital technologies and employment levels. The cultural and creative industries should be named as an industry of strategic importance. 6.2. A sectoral approach does not preclude strategies for growth across all sectors, for instance, on global competitiveness, innovation and productivity. Named sectors could be enabled to take the lead in economy-wide initiatives. If the industrial strategy does not take a sectoral approach, it is possible that more resources will need to be committed to work effectively across all sectors, regardless of their strategic importance to the UK economy.  How should an industrial strategy link with devolution initiatives aimed at devolving taxation and decision making away from Westminster? 7.1.  The Arts Council would welcome a geographical approach to the industrial strategy, supporting growth in the creative and cultural industries across the country and linking to devolution initiatives.  7.2. As the national development agency, the Arts Council takes a national and expert overview. We invest at scale alongside local government and other partners to grow the cultural sector and its economic contribution locally. Local government is the Arts Council’s key strategic and delivery partner. Local authorities understand local need, leverage resources and broker partnerships. 7.3. We are already working with combined and local authorities on devolution deals, including Tees Valley and Liverpool City Region Combined Authorities.  7.4. The industrial strategy should consider further initiatives by the Arts Council and local government to support local cultural and creative industries clusters. For example: The development of new local revenue streams to support cultural infrastructure, e.g. hotel bed tax – revenues would increase as the enhanced cultural offer drove growth in the tourist economy New capital investment mechanisms, e.g. revolving investment funds, which could be made into the cultural sector, increasing the value of broader capital and regeneration schemesEnsuring the Local Growth Fund, and any replacement for European Structural and Investment Funds, supports the creative and cultural sector What examples are there of interventions from central Government that have successfully supported economic growth away from London and the South East of England?  8.1.  Targeted investment in arts, culture and the creative industries by the Arts Council and others contributed to significant cultural and economic growth in cities such as Manchester, Liverpool and Newcastle.   8.2. The Arts Council remains committed to increasing investment outside London, in part to underpin growth in regions where there is unrealised potential for growth in the creative industries. For example, the £3.8m Creative Local Growth Fund supports the growth of the cultural sector and its contribution to economic growth and the creative industries outside of London.  How should the industrial strategy work with local authorities and Local Economic Partnerships, reconciling a U.K.-wide strategy and local, regional and devolved nations' priorities? 9.1.  The Arts Council works to a national strategy while recognising the value of place-based working and the role of local government, LEPs, etc. This experience can provide useful learnings to policy makers reconciling UK-wide strategy with the local, regional and devolved nations’ priorities. 9.2. For example, there is great value in a clear national approach to sector development which is defined nationally in consultation with sector experts, national and local partners and stakeholders – this makes the strategy robust and realistic but also secures buy in. National and subnational policy makers must both recognise that national level priorities and programmes are appropriate, but that they will very often be realised in place-based policy making – which will vary from place to place and bring its own complexity. 9.3. The Government should also consider an enabling power to collaborate for all UK public bodies, including those in devolved administrations, to enable cross-UK collaborations which can be restricted by administrative boundaries.    October 2016               [1] http://www.artscouncil.org.uk/sites/default/files/download-file/Great_art_and_culture_for_everyone.pdf[2] http://www.artscouncil.org.uk/about-us/how-and-where-we-invest-public-money[3] Prime Minister’s speech at Conservative Party Conference, 5 October 2016[4] Prime Minister and BEIS Secretary’s speeches at Conservative Party Conference, 5 October 2016[5] The contribution of arts and culture to the UK economy, Cebr research – 2013 and 2015[6] The Culture White Paper, March 2016[7] https://www.nesta.org.uk/sites/default/files/the_geography_of_creativity_in_the_uk.pdf[8] DCMS Statistical Handbook September 2016[9] Interview in Prospect Magazine, 4 October 2016 [10] Culture Means Business, British Council, 2013, https://www.britishcouncil.org/organisation/policy-insight-research/research/culture-means-business [11] Building a Creative Nation: Putting Skills to Work’ Report by Creative & Cultural Skills, on the impact of the Creative Employment Programme (CEP) 2015.

submitter: Social Research Institute, University College London
text: ISG0190 - Evidence on Industrial strategy  Written evidence from the Office of National Statistics (ISG 190)    Official Statistics and UK Industry; an Introduction Official statistics are for the benefit of society and the economy generally.  They allow the formulation of better public policy, and the effective measurement of these policies.In response to the Business, Innovation and Skills Committee’s call for evidence on the Government’s Industrial Strategy, this note summarises what statistics are available to policy-makers, to assist in the development of an industrial strategy.  It also sets out what high-level trends these statistics currently suggest.In particular, reflecting the themes highlighted in the Committee’s call for evidence, this note summarises:what statistics are available to help us understand trends across different industry sectors, in the context of a continuing reorientation of UK industry toward the services sector;how data might help us identify different trends in the performance and concentration of industry in different regions and countries of the UK; andwhat we currently understand about the productivity of UK industry, as well as the ways in which the Office for National Statistics (ONS) is improving the UK’s suite of productivity measures.   (Please note that this document is not intended to provide an exhaustive account of trends in UK industry; for further detail on any of the statistics in this note, please contact the Office for National Statistics).  Understanding the performance of UK industry by sector The Office for National Statistics publishes a range of data to assist decision-makers in understanding how economic activity in the UK is evolving across different industry sectors. How is industry classified?   Information on industries is generally presented using the UK Standard Industrial Classification.  The UK Standard Industrial Classification classifies businesses by the primary type of economic activity in which they are engaged.  Using this system has the advantage of providing a relatively uniform set of definitions which can be applied across all countries, and used to compare trends internationally. The process of standardisation across countries, however, poses some challenges in the measurement of emerging industries.  What data are available?    Initial estimates of the quarterly value of the goods and services, broken down by industry sector, are published around four weeks after the end of the quarter, as part of the Preliminary estimate of Gross Domestic Product (GDP).  More detailed, monthly data are published on the performance of the services, production (including manufacturing) and construction sectors, approximately eight weeks after the end of the reference month.Although the way these estimates are produced varies by industry, for most industries data are collected from ONS’ Monthly Business Survey.  The Monthly Business Survey collects data about the turnover of over 45,000 businesses; this is then adjusted to remove the effect of changes in the price level (inflation).Even more detailed industry breakdowns are published based on results from the ONS Annual Business Survey. However, this output only covers non-financial businesses and is produced around 9 months after the end of the year. The business level data from both the annual and monthly business surveys are available to government users for research purposes via the ONS Virtual Microdata Laboratory (VML). ONS also publish regularly on the number of workforce jobs by 20 key industrial groups in its regular Labour Market Bulletin. Employer surveys are used to measure the number of filled jobs in the economy (different from the number of people in employment, as some people have more than one job). This information is typically published for the third month in the quarter, around ten weeks after the end of the month. Given the prominence of these economic statistics in understanding the development of the economy and detecting turning points in economic growth, the methodologies for these statistics are a key part of ONS’ development programme. In addition, ONS also plans to use administrative data from Value Added Tax (VAT) returns to supplement data from the Monthly Business Survey to improve these estimates. What do the data tell us?As Table 1 sets out, services accounted for nearly 80 per cent of the UK economy in 2013. This proportion has been increasing over time, reflecting a continuing reorientation of the UK economy towards services output. Production accounted for around 15 per cent of the output of the UK economy in 2013; within this, the manufacturing industry group accounted for around 10 per cent of the UK economy’s output.  As Figure 1 makes clear, the trend of continued reorientation of the UK economy towards services is shared by other similar developed economies. Notably, the services industrial grouping has been a larger share of the UK economy since 2004 than the group of comparable economies.            Table 2 draws from more detailed quarterly data to show the change in contribution of selected industries to GDP, between 1997 and 2013. Industrial GroupContribution to GDP119972013Manufacture of basic pharmaceutical products & preparations0.70.9Manufacture of motor vehicles, trailers and semi-trailers1.10.7Manufacture of air and spacecraft and related machinery0.60.4Electricity, gas, steam & air conditioning supply1.51.5Financial and insurance activities6.07.6Professional, scientific and technical activities5.57.3Education5.16.3Table 2: Contribution of selected industrial groups to GDP, 20131 Contributions are to Output GVA, used as a proxy for contributions to GDP Understanding the performance of UK industry by region The Office for National Statistics also publishes a range of data to assist decision-makers in understanding trends in industry by region.  How are regions defined?   The principal official statistic for measuring the performance of industries across the regions of the UK is regional Gross Value Added (GVA), which is comparable with GDP, differing only in the exclusion from GVA of taxes (less subsidies) on products (e.g. VAT). Regional GVA is compiled within the National Accounts framework, which ensures consistency of regional and national statistics. Currently, regional GVA estimates are produced for areas defined by the European classification system called the Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics (NUTS). In the UK, estimates are published for the top three levels of the NUTS hierarchy, which are:NUTS1: 12 areas, comprising Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland and the 9 English regions (an England aggregate is also produced to allow country comparisons)NUTS2: 40 areas, mostly comprising groups of counties and unitary authoritiesNUTS3: 173 areas, mostly comprising single counties and unitary authoritiesThere is also a category called Extra-regio, which covers GVA that cannot be assigned to a region or regions of the UK. This category includes activities on the continental shelf (oil and gas extraction from the North Sea) and the activities of UK embassies and armed forces overseas.What data are available?At the NUTS1 and NUTS2 levels, regional GVA is published with an industrial breakdown to 33 industries. At the more detailed NUTS3 level, 11 broad industry groups are provided. Regional GVA is compiled using the income approach, and in addition to GVA, estimates are provided for the various components of income that make up GVA, such as Compensation of Employees, Mixed Income of the self-employed, Rental Income, and Gross Trading Profits (GTP) of business corporations. These components allow users to understand in greater depth the nature of industrial performance in an area, as they show which components are driving growth or decline. A range of labour market statistics are available which give employment numbers by region and industry and regional unemployment figures. The Business Register and Employment Survey publishes employee and employment estimates at detailed geographical and industrial levels. High level estimates are published on the ONS website and more detailed estimates are published on the Nomis website. Employment data from the survey have also been used by ONS to perform analysis of the geographical concentration of industries, the industrial specialisations of areas and the spatial distribution of industries. Earnings estimates are also produced, broken down by full-time and part-time working patterns. Used with regional GVA these data can also provide regional productivity estimates. ONS’s Annual Business Survey (ABS) provides a wide range of data (including historical data) on business enterprises, and can provide estimates for many different levels of geography with far greater detail of industry than any comparable source. The latest ABS regional results show the size and growth of the UK non-financial sectors within the regions of the UK. Data on the regional breakdown of exporters and importers from the ABS are also published on our website. These data are not compiled within the National Accounts framework and can exhibit a high level of volatility, particularly at low levels of geography or industry. They can, however, be used in conjunction with regional GVA to inform analyses requiring a greater degree of industrial precision than is normally available. The Inter-Departmental Business Register is a further source of regional data. Business counts by region are given in our latest publication of UK Business: activity, size and location, and business births and deaths by UK region are published in our Business Demography release. What does the data tell us?As set out in Table 3, there are some common trends in the growth of industry across regions; with the effect of increasing property values producing widespread growth in real estate, and the equally widespread decline in mining and quarrying affecting a large number of regions.  Table 3 also highlights different trends in the growth of industry across regions; for example, arts and recreation (including sports) show strong growth in the North, and manufacturing is faring badly in the North East, South East and Scotland. Table 3: NUTS1 regions, growth in nominal GVA 1997 to 2014, highest and lowest SIC industry sectionsRegionStrongest Growth%Weakest Growth%North EastR: Arts and recreation190C: Manufacturing16North WestR: Arts and recreation195B: Mining and quarrying16Yorks. & HumberL: Real estate194B: Mining and quarrying-52East MidlandsL: Real estate203B: Mining and quarrying-6West MidlandsL: Real estate181B: Mining and quarrying-64East of EnglandL: Real estate225B: Mining and quarrying-30LondonD: Electricity and gas356B: Mining and quarrying-15South EastL: Real estate222C: Manufacturing11South WestL: Real estate212B: Mining and quarrying-8WalesL: Real estate*175A: Agriculture-30ScotlandN: Admin. and support188C: Manufacturing13Northern IrelandE: Water and waste325A: Agriculture-25*growth in the activities of households as employers is greater, but is not considered here as GVA is very small The distribution of industries varies across the UK.   If we look at two contrasting industries in England and Wales, manufacturing, and information and communication, we can see the areas with the greatest activity in the more traditional (and generally declining) industries and the newer (and generally growing) technology industries.                   The places with the largest GVA in 2014 in manufacturing were Hertfordshire (£3,514m), Cheshire East (£2,997m), and Berkshire (£2,883m). The first two of these have very large contributions from the pharmaceutical and transport equipment industries, while Berkshire has a much broader base across a range of manufacturing industries. The places with the largest GVA in 2014 in information and communication were Berkshire (£7,834m), Westminster (£7,247m), and Camden and the City of London (£6,961m). This shows the concentration of technology industries in and around the capital, but it is also interesting to see that Berkshire is even stronger in technology than it is in the more traditional manufacturing activity.  Understanding the productivity of UK industry  Given the importance of productivity for firm growth and survival, international competitiveness and living standards more broadly, it seems a natural focus for industrial policy.  As the committee may be aware, ONS’s main measure of labour productivity is calculated by dividing a measure of output by an indicator of labour input. The output measure ONS uses is Gross Value Added (GVA). Labour inputs are measured in terms of workers, jobs, and hours worked, and using these data ONS can calculate productivity metrics for a range of industries and regions as well as for the UK as a whole.  What does the data tell us?ONS’s preferred measure of labour productivity – output per hour – has grown very slowly by historical standards since the start of the recent economic downturn.  Figure 4 shows the ‘Productivity Puzzle’ in its most simple form. It shows the development of two measures of productivity – output per hour and output per worker – alongside their projected path had they continued to grow at the 1994-2007 rate over the full period. It shows that productivity growth slowed markedly after 2008 and that, as a result, productivity as measured by output per hour 15.5% was below its pre-downturn trend in Q1 2016. ONS produces these headline labour productivity data each quarter.  Figure 4: Output per hour and output per workerSeasonally adjusted, quarter 1 (Jan to Mar) 2008 to quarter 1 (Jan to Mar) 2016, UKSource: Office for National Statistics UK productivity by industry Alongside productivity statistics for the whole economy, ONS also produce quarterly estimates of productivity at the industry level, enabling analysis of how different industries have been affected by the recent slowdown in productivity growth.  Figure 5 shows the average quarterly rate of output per hour growth in a range of different industries between (a) Q1 1994 and Q1 2008 and (b) the period since Q1 2008. It suggests – consistent with a large academic literature on this matter – that the slowdown of productivity growth has been broadly based. While specific industries account for a large portion of the slowdown in productivity, output per hour growth has weakened markedly in almost all industries in recent years.  Figure 5: Average quarterly productivity growth across services industries and manufacturing, Q1 1994 to Q1 2008 and Q1 2008 to Q1 2016.Output per hour, average quarterly growth, UK, %Source: Office for National Statistics To enable international comparisons of productivity, ONS also produces a set of biannual articles examining labour productivity in the UK and a range of other countries. While the focus of these articles is on headline labour productivity, the most recent edition also included a set of experimental estimates of labour productivity in different industries across countries which may be of interest to the committee. Productivity by UK regionONS also publishes annual estimates labour productivity for the high-level, NUTS1 regions, as well as experimental estimates for the smaller NUTS2 and 3 sub-regions, Local Enterprise Partnerships and selected city regions. These enable a comparison of output per hour across different parts of the UK, which reflect both the mix of industrial activity and the quantity of labour input in each region.  Figure 6 shows two alternative measures of labour productivity in each region expressed relative to the UK level, and indicates that productivity in London was almost 30% higher than the UK average on both metrics in 2014. The only other region with productivity above the UK average was the South East of England (9% above the UK average), while output per hour in the north of England and across the midlands was between 9 and 13% below the UK average. However, unlike ONS’ other estimates of labour productivity, these data are expressed in nominal terms, which means that they cannot account for variation in price-levels or price-growth across regions. This is an area in which ONS is planning to make improvements in the future.  Figure 6: Labour productivity by NUTS 1 region or country, 2014GVA per filled job and GVA per hour worked, UK=100             Source: Office for National Statistics  More detailed measures and analyses of productivity Alongside these estimates of labour productivity, ONS also produces a range of more detailed statistics and analyses of productivity. ONS’ estimates of multi-factor productivity (MFP) offer particularly detailed insights into the performance of the UK economy and its constituent industries. These experimental estimates – published annually at present – divide output growth in to the contributions from changes in the (a) quantity and quality of labour and (b) the volume of capital services applied to the production process. These analyses enable ONS to disentangle output growth which is due to: Changes in the number of ‘effective’ hours being worked in a given industry – accounting for both changes in the quantity and quality of labour used in production (‘Labour composition’)Changes in the amount of capital being applied to the productive process – accounting for differences in capital: labour ratios in different industries through time (‘Capital deepening’)A measure of technical progress – capturing the efficiency with which different factors are combined in production (‘Multi-factor productivity’ (MFP)).  Of these, the last measure of MFP growth is considered particularly important for changes in living standards. Figure 7 shows the contribution of each of these effects to labour productivity growth over the last four decades. It shows that MFP fell sharply during the recent economic downturn, as it did during previous downturns. However, the failure of MFP to rebound from this period (as it has done following earlier recessions) is one of the defining characteristics of the UK productivity puzzle. Figure 7: Decomposition of labour productivity growth, 1971 to 2014, UKSource: Office for National Statistics Finally, ONS also produce a range of analytical pieces on productivity which are designed to enable users to better understand developments in the headline statistics. The newly formed ‘Productivity Bulletin’ now provides the vehicle for these outputs. In the past, these analyses have included an examination of industry contributions to labour productivity growth and the distribution of firm level productivity, as well as stand-alone articles on productivity puzzle, including 'What is the productivity puzzle?', ‘The productivity conundrum, explanations and preliminary analysis’, and 'The Productivity Conundrum, Interpreting the Recent Behavior of the Economy'. Planned developments As the committee will be aware, the measurement of productivity was at the centre of the recent Review of Economic Statistics conducted by Professor Sir Charles Bean. Bean The review made a range of recommendations on the existing suite of productivity outputs, which ONS are now working to implement.[1] In broad terms, these include: Enhancements to ONS’ Multi-factor Productivity estimates: This work will involve developing a greater degree of industrial detail available in our MFP statistics, and include a shift from producing them on an annual cycle to the same, quarterly cycle as the labour productivity estimates. These data will enable ONS to support the policy-making process by producing a conceptually stronger measure of productivity in something closer to real-time. Quarterly MFP statistics are planned for publication from January 2018 onwards.  Improving estimates of labour productivity: ONS will also be working to enhance the robustness and coverage of existing measures of labour productivity. This will include changes to existing estimation systems, analysis of the accuracy of our estimates of hours worked by industry and changes to market sector measures of labour input to enhance consistency across the National Accounts.  Developing micro-data resources: ONS is also undertaking work to improve the availability of survey- and other micro-data resources for productivity research. This work – carried out in concert with academics, the ONS Fellows and the National Institute for Economic and Social Research – will enable more detailed analyses of firm-level productivity.   October 2016  [1] More detail on these planned changes can be found in the recent Economic Statistics and Analysis Strategy and the Q1 2016 Productivity Bulletin.

ISG0188 - Evidence on Industrial strategyWritten evidence from CityREDI (ISG 188)   Timely re-adjustments by the Select CommitteeCityREDI is a new research institute focussed on understanding major city-regions across the world to develop better national and local policy and practice for economic growth.In May 2016, CityREDI provided the Committee with evidence on Government policy for powerhouses in the Midlands and the North. CityREDI welcomes the re-focusing and re-naming of the Select Committee to reflect the new Government’s leanings towards a new industrial strategy. We still see a strong case for Government support for the Midlands Engine as part of these developments. We see stronger devolution as the primary focus for a more effective industrial strategy set within a collaborative national framework.  The early reform of BIS is welcome. The inclusion of industrial policy signals its importance. It should be established on a par with monetary and fiscal policy.  Equally the inclusion of energy is important. Access to reliable and cheap energy and its security is crucial to industry, especially in the wake of Brexit and the prospect of tariff charges on imports.Background  The recession and Brexit have shaken the roots of UK competitiveness and highlighted both a centralised and divided nation. In August the new PM chaired the first meeting of the Cabinet Committee on Economy and Industrial Strategy. The PM said the objective should be to deliver an economy that works for all. The Chancellor prioritised reducing the productivity gap between London and the South East and the rest of the UK, but so far there have been no announcements on how they intend to achieve this. Ministers asserted that the strategy should be strength-based and create an economy open to new industries.  Government pressures There are a number of immediate pressures facing government:  Uncertainty and impact of Brexit.  Spatial imbalance and the concentration of economic, administrative and financial power in London and the South East (Martin, R and Tyler, P, 2015). Sectoral imbalance and the consequences of an over-reliance on the financial sector. Social imbalance (Martin, R and Tyler, P, 2013) and the search for a more ‘inclusive growth’ (Mazzucato, M 2015). Public-private sector imbalance, austerity and the impact of Brexit on private sector future investment decisions.Temporal imbalance and an excessive and prevailing short-termism.Pressures from the sale of ARM, the future of Tata Steel, the uncertainty and security around the Hinkley Point decision and a more general concern about how to protect the public interest (Aiginger, K et al, 2015).  Set out below is a response to the questions set out by the Committee. What does the Government mean by industrial strategy, and what does the private sector want from one?Summary pointsIndustrial strategy is shorthand for state support for industry.The private sector want stable macro economic conditions, political and social stability as well as access to finance and financial incentives to support investment opportunities.Industrial strategy is defined as government intervention to support or develop some industries to enhance economic growth (Krugman, P and Obstfeld, M. 2003).  The Government currently has a strategy inherited from the Coalition Government based on horizontal and sectoral policies (BIS, 2013, 2014, Heseltine, M. 2012, Adonis, A. 2014). The current strategy provides a framework but there is a lack of geographical specificity. The horizontal policies address market-wide issues. They provide the resources and economic environment which make it easier for businesses and people to be more productive. They cover adjustments to regulation, tax regimes, innovation and skills. Sectoral policies are directed towards specific sectors in the economy. This includes subsidies to support the production of low carbon technology, providing ‘seed funding’ for high technology clusters and support for research and development in particular industries (Rhodes, C. 2014). The scale of government intervention is relatively weak, circa £600M (2014). The scale and scope of overall support compares unfavourably with the past and with our main competitors. Advantage West Midlands for example invested £3bn in 10 years. The US invest circa $40bn per annum in business support.  In the US in 2006, this was $92 billion, with the state responsible for 60% of research and 25% of all R&D.  A strong private sector provides the means to secure a high quality of life and more cohesive society. Setting the framework of conditions to stimulate the private sector is crucial. This requires conditions, often taken for granted but currently challenged, such as political and economic stability. A national framework for competitiveness  based on macro-economic and monetary policy, fiscal policy, employment law, trade law and higher education policy is essential. There is a view that a new national Office for  Industrial Responsibility (and Innovation and Regions) could put industrial policy on a more equal footing with monetary (Bank of England) and fiscal (Office for Fiscal Responsibility) policy. This body could be an independent national body responsible for monitoring, impact and evaluation (Bailey, D et al, 2015). It would bring important oversight. A long term approach is also desirable (Foresight, 2013, Wright, M. 2014). Germany for example last changed its skills policy in 1951 (Bryson, J, 2016). Cross party political support for a long term industrial strategy, with some sort of binding agreement to make it difficult to override, could also contribute to certainty and confidence for investors.  A spatial framework that recognises major endowments and assets should also feature. Competitiveness is primarily determined at city region levels within the traded economy (Porter, M, 1998, 2011). So spatial and regional dimensions to industrial strategy are crucial. Securing cross-sector stakeholder ‘buy in’ for the city-region industry vision and long term direction and differentiation is also crucial. City regions should clearly diagnose their evolving comparative industry advantages based on granular analysis of their distinct structural economies. This will include things like the on-going science and innovation audit work but cover whole economies and then determine the policy adjustments and incentives to be applied based on local conditions. How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy, for example in preventing foreign takeovers of UK companies?Summary pointsPost recession and Brexit there appears to be a desire to be more interventionist and prioritise certain industries as part of a re-emergence of a stronger nationalism and the vulnerabilities arising from globalisation.Public Interest Tests could be used to better protect the national interest in response to mergers and takeovers. Industrial policy can be applied with differing levels of intervention or intensity. At one end of the spectrum an intensive industrial policy could mean nationalisation, as iwitnessed by the emerging policies of the Labour Party.  At the other end government would allow its own industries to collapse under the pressure of international competition. There is a prevailing assumption in most of the academic literature that there needs to be a mixed economy approach based on private enterprise embedded within a framework of public action that encourages restructuring, diversification and technological dynamism and that this in the main delivers beyond market forces alone. However, the balance within the ‘mix’ is more difficult. Perhaps it should depend on the context and the political choice over the desirability of different objectives. Despite two recent growth reviews leaving no stones unturned (Heseltine, Adonis) there are no universal interventions that work in all circumstances. However, the case against industrial polic is seen in the academic literature as primarily resting on practical difficulties with implementation. The previous government (post-Coalition) by and large relied on market forces to shape activities with an ideological belief that in the long run an equilibrium would be achieved as businesses and people adapted to them (Overman, H, and Rice, P, 2008). Its rebalancing policy in practice was largely about reducing the scope and scale of the public sector because of a further belief that it crowded out the private sector. It also loosely encouraged specific priority sectors in a very light touch way through sector councils and coordination.  To establish a more ‘active’ industrial strategy means there is a need to understand in depth the prevailing conditions across Britain as the foundations from which to build.  This is important because the more we understand the detailed dynamics of local economies the easier it s to know how to intervene to accelerate desirable outcomes.  It is also important as some argue (Pitelis, C 2015, Khan, M 2015,Coates, D 2015, Bowman, A et al, 2015) a process of re-industrialisation is unachievable in Britain because of the extent of de-industrialisation as demonstrated for example by multinational companies off-shoring activities (and the limited re-shoring) as well as the scale of existing skills gaps, deficits and shortages. These prevailing conditions need to be understood otherwise interventions ito improve the supply of certain skills will never work if there are other barriers constraining demand. It is therefore essential to have a comprehensive understanding of the whole system and its narrative. To do this well industrial policy should adopt a strong city region dimension where functional economic market areas can be more deeply understood at a granular level within place context and different adjustments made to the framework of conditions to more realistically respond to the different needs of different parts of the country.  This question above raises the specific issue of takeovers as an example. There is a case to establish some form of Public Interest Test. A set of principles, values and other criteria could be set to deal with foreign mergers and takeovers with a view to protecting the national interest. This would be done on a case by case basis, to allow for the prevailing circumstances. An independent body could be established, to sit from time to time, to examine the public interest of takeovers above certain thresholds. Evidence suggests the world is predicted to be on the verge of another enormous takeover movement driven by the increasing concentrations of wealth and power. In an uncertain Brexit frame these forces could have devastating consequences. Cadbury and Astra Zeneca demonstrate the case (Singh, A et al, 2015). There is also scope to revise the Takeover Code to give greater protection to defending firms, by for example ‘raising the bar’. The implications of coming out of the EU Takeover Directive also could be looked at (Singh, A et al, 2015).  What lessons can be learnt from: previous governments' industrial strategies?; other countries' attempts to develop industrial strategies? Over recent times there has existed a broad consensus across Britain that intervention should only go as far as improving the conditions and coordinating and supporting markets, not subsidising specific industries.The Labour Party more recently has been pushing for a much more interventionist and protectionist industrial policy, based on re-nationalisation of industries, £500 billlion additional borrowing and removing the private sector from public services and utility industries.Japanese state directed economic growth is a useful model to show how intervention can outperform market forces. The sort of intervention has successfully been adapted in South East Asia and Scandinavia.US and German more federal structures also provide useful insights, especially in realising the full productive potential of all parts of their nations.  The narrative stemming from Britain as the first industrial nation (Mathias, P, 2001) and the post-war period that ended in government subsidising industrial failure is fairly well-documented (Bowman, A, 2015). The industrial failures around massive subsidies to British Leyland, Chrysler and Rolls Royce  to name but a few are well known. It has been argued amongst many other issues such as industrial relations, that successive British Governments made the major mistake of trying to hold back against globalisation. And of course there iare the lessons about propping up failure.   Equally, there is now a prevailing view that Britain in more recent times has been one of the more open world economies and this has come to disadvantage it. Whilst other countries, particularly the US, openly embraced globalisation and free market forces, the underlying reality compared to Britain, was very different. As a result the British de-industrialisation process and openness to free market forces has perhaps  secured short-term advantages but in the longer term damaged the national interest.  The Japanese model of state-directed economic growth and its later replication in South Korea and the South East Asia Tiger Economies is worthy of some assessment, even though it has been a more closed economy.  The Japanese created state-owned financial institutions linking individual savers to industries short of capital, by deciding which industries should receive priviledged access to that capital was only granted in return for long-term improvements in export performance. Post-war Japanese governments inherited from the pre-war state and economy a planning ministry – MITI equipped with skilled personnel to play that role and a structure of networked companies also able to effectively deploy investment and labour resources as agreed. Using these institutions and networks, successive governments moved the entire centre of gravity of the Japanese economy first out of agriculture and textiles into steel, chemicals, machinery and other heavy industries, then later out of those into transport and electrical goods (cars, motor bikes, televisions, videos, calculators) then later still, into a range of high technology products (Coates, D 2000). The model used the threat and reality of the withdrawal of state support to head off any protected markets and managed competition. The successful newly industrialised countries (S. Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, Malaysia) showed how total factor productivity could grow rapidly with targeted investments in skills and capital goods (manufacturing automation); and, how a precise industrial strategy, focused on specific industry sectors and firms could boost competitiveness as infant industry protectionism gave way to open trade and FDI. Nevertheless there are key differences in that these economies were in the catching-up phase of growth with a strongly interventionist state (and different kind of ‘democracy’) and as a result had more control over trade and capital markets; and the initial focuswas, like Japan and now China, manufacturing. Intervening in service-based economies is much more complex. A manufacturing revival is not enough in terms of our needs, or something that is easily achievable, although the sector is important for the West Midlands than for most regions. International comparative evidence from the US and Germany additionally demonstrates why strong regional institutional structures embed and sustain the competitiveness of their industries. Germany’s more federalised set of institutions and networks with univesal regional banks with state support for skilled training seemed to be particularly successful: a sort of strong regonal coordinated system. What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions?Summary pointsThe tensions arise from misalignment at the national level.City region industrial strategy provides a means to reconcile these tensions based on local conditions and circumstances.  Greater devolution of powers and resources for industrial strategy to city regions would reduce the tensions.To maximise the effectiveness of industrial strategy there is a need not only to join up the priority sectors, technologies and research capabilities so that major innovation and breakthroughs are secured and happen here, but also government policy across Whitehall, in terms of  skills, housing and higher education as key ingedients to support them.  As already mentioned, competitiveness that enables business and industry to ‘compete’ in the traded economy and raise living standards is essentially determined at the city region functional economic market area, where the real differntiation is made. On that basis, we strongly believe the tensions between specific policies at the weight given to them, such as supporting clusters or agglomeration or infrastructure  or procurement issues are best determined at the local level through a strong city region dimension to industrial strategy with support from national government where the issues have national or global dimensions.  What are the pros and cons of an industrial strategy adopting a sectoral approach? Should the Government proactively seek to ‘pick winners’?  criteria should be used to identify which sectors are supported?  Should the Government prop up traditional industries that it considers to be in the national interest? If not a sectoral approach, should the industrial strategy have a broader objective, such as improving productivity?Summary pointsSectors are an important focus but there should be more integration with horizontal policies including geographical specificity as part of a place-based approach.Supporting productivity, output and employment and exports are crucial.Certain industries should be prioritised and protected. Re-industrialisation through manufacturing is not enough, even if desirable. Servitisation, end-user services and creative industries  need to feature much more strongly in UK industrial strategy and regional strategies – but there is a lack of evidence and analysis on these sectors. Moreover, productivity and GVA are more difficult to pin down in these sectors. – but this  could be where we need to focus more analysis and creative thinking for innovation policy. Sectoral policy must be sensitive to place. Just as place based economic development must be sectoral in focus. The only way that this can be achieved is by significant devolution of industrial strategy responsibility to sub-national levels – within a coherent national policy framework that prioritises productivity enhancement. The existing strategy of picking winning priority sectors, technologies and the associated policy instruments, such as Catapults nevertheless provides a working national framework. The Government analysis for determining the priority sectors (Industrial Strategy, UK Sector Analysis, BIS, 2102) is good in so far as it goes. Putting to one side the weaknesses of the industrial classification system, the focus on output and employment, productivity and export markets and future potential seem the right broad aims as well as distinguishing important enabling sectors such as energy and construction.  However, there does appear to be a misalignment with the identification of the priority technologies and there is also a lack of geographical specificity in the policy design as well as appreciating the potential for sub national sub sector specificity that could also establish a new lens for rebalancing and helping ‘lagging’ city regions.  There is a case for stronger industrial policy integration and alignment so that sectors, technologies and policies work as whole systems. These essential alignments and developments could be worked up in collaboration with city regions that could provide strong regional coordination through elected mayors and devolution. Government needs to demonstrate it is investing in ‘local’ industrial growth. There is a strong need for industrial policy alignment across Whitehall with the full range of Government policies.  Protecting and supporting firms or sectors where there are no sustainable competitive advantages, often for social and political reasons rather than sensible economies, can result in support for dying industries; where capital markets are at risk of being biased by political intervention; where key technologies or new business models are backed by government without any guarantee that they will succeed in the market.  In the final analysis the government must decide as to whether the security of certain industries because of what they provide such as energy or steel or rail warrant protection because they are public goods or have substantial positive externalities. Equally, decisions about supporting the exploitation of specific strengths, such as specific sectors, large firms, technologies or research capabilities need to be determined.  Elected mayor city regions should be free to set regional industrial policies that increasingly adapt and adjust against the national priorities and framework. Competitiveness is primarily determined at city region levels within the traded economy (Porter, M, 1998, 2011), and especially once the framework of conditions is set. City regions should clearly diagnose their evolving comparative industry advantages based on granular analysis of their distinct structural economies. This will include things like the on-going science and innovation audit work but cover whole economies. Place based whole system industrial strategies that can demonstrate agglomeration growth should be co-produced with central government and be fully supported as part of SEP development within the national framework. City regions need devolution of funding and delivery responsibility to help in securing investment in their industrial base where value propositions can be realised.  The West Midlands is a clear leader in the automotive sector and provides a significant contribution to four of the eleven national priority sectors. When the sectors are cross referenced with the national priority technologies and catapults there is one whole system in place in the West Midlands. This sits within automotive, with the energy storage research and the high value manufacturing catapult bolstered by other relevant strategic interventions such as the Manufacturing Technology Centre, the Advanced Propulsion Centre and National Automotive Centre. There is a need for more academic-industry hybrids like these as a matter of government policy. There is a role for city region leaders to join-up these collaborations so many more synergies are explored and realised. There is benefit in strong and direct alignment between the sectors, technologies, research and innovation. This is happening in a fairly high level way currently, in terms of physical space and economic planning zones. There is a need for some government investment to build capacity to support academic and business collaboration, innovation and commercialisation of research within these niche sub-sectors.  Central government could provide a new phase of Catapults that operate within targeted and specifically relevant regions. These sub-catapults or Catalysers/Stimulators, so-called to differentiate them from the national catapults, would support and focus on sub sectors within or as spin outs from the priority sectors. Examples in the West Midlands would be around rail that would link the impressive policy join-up around HS2 developments with the HQ; the two station development opportunities; supply chains; skills and the college; and the maintenance plans and links with the metro; as well clear technological synergies with automotive and aerospace and the links to academic excellence in these fields in the main universities. Should the industrial strategy have a geographical emphasis? How should an industrial strategy link with devolution initiatives aimed at devolving initiatives aimed at devolving taxation and decision making away from Westminster? What examples are there of interventions from central Government that have successfully supported economic growth away from London and South East of England? How should the industrial strategy work with local authorities and Local Economic Partnerships, reconciling a UK-wide strategy and local, regional and devolved nations’ priorities?  Summary Geographical emphasis should become a primary focus of industrial strategy.Devolution of industrial strategy should mean priority sectors, technologies, Higher Education priorities and ‘catapult’ type interventions are increasingly shaped, tailored and determined in and for the regions, adapted by granular strength-based differentiated city region strategies.Industrial strategy in this context would require a more federated set of institutional arrangements.The decentralisation and devolvement of  large sections of public finance to support industries, based on functional economic market areas.Devolvement of  property taxation and other fiscal mechanisms such as tourism taxes, congestion charges, and powers and freedoms for serious financial innovation.Establisment of regional development banks to support more mature relationships between industry, business and commercial banking outside London.Empower independent new economy hubs in the regions to support strategy development and intelligence.  The Sectoral prioritisation characteristic of recent industrial policy in the UK has always had a spatial implication because the spatial distribution of sectors is uneven. We feel that the industrial strategy must, therefore, be far more sensitive to the distinctive characteristics of place and the structure of local and regional economies. The economic challenges of Brexit make it all the more important that sources of regional and national comparative advantage are exploited to the full. This means empowering sub-national economic development bodies to develop industrial strategies sensitive to the specific sectoral composition of the local and regional economies for which they have responsibility. The ability to do this is a function of strong analytical capacity and a locus of decision making that is embedded in the industrial milieu that is to be developed. There is a need to blend a place-based understanding of economic development with the sectoral approach adopted by previous governments. Businesses are located both within supply chains and markets, which may be regional, national or global, and within a specific location.  Conditions must be right within both dimensions for the specific businesses which make up an industry to thrive, become more productive and benefit the communities within which they are located. Contemporary academic thinking about drivers and determinants of regional and local economic growth have increasingly emphasised the importance of strong and effective institutions in supporting growth. The institutional architecture underpinning industrial strategy development and implementation in the UK has become increasingly complex with the emergence of city–deals, devo-deals at varying spatial scales, local enterprise partnerships, combined authorities. It is welcome that Government has reaffirmed its commitment to the Northern Powerhouse and Midlands Engine. However, policy in this arena requires greater clarity and consistency. In particular the asymmetry between the funding and powers devolved to these initiatives should be addressed. The scale and functional significance of the Midlands to the nation’s economy demands it. City regions are at the right spatial level to join up policy so that research, industry and expertise is aligned and industrial priorities are also then integrated with other policies such as skills, FDI, education, infrastructure and exporting.  City regions are able to pull together the right mix of industry, research and institutions and collaborations. These propositions require incentives and investment to get them over the line and should be supported as part of the national framework. - By driving the knowledge economy, productivity, skills and innovation that connect with the existing local strengths – the sectors, the assets, large firms  and Higher Education capabilities, will make Britain stronger,  The industrial policy should be formulated or adjusted at city region level. For example the West Midlands has a high dependency on a few large multinational firms. In the automotive sector we know reshoring Tier 1 suppliers is worth £4bn and Tier 2 and beyond a further £2bn.
