Its findings should provide useful policy recommendations for vertical interventions in construction. Broadly, any industrial strategy for construction must have skills at its heart, as it is a critical and enduring issue for the sector. The strategy needs to consider challenges such as:How to maintain investment in skills in a sector that is particularly vulnerable to cycles of boom and bust.How to effectively link locally devolved skills and training policies with the needs of an industry with a highly mobile workforce and an ever changing pipeline of projects.How to encourage an industry that is heavily reliant on a sub-contracting model to recruit, retain and train the future workforce.While the industrial strategy will need to intervene in education to address these issues, construction itself can help attract talented people into the industry. In September 2015, CITB launched Go Construct, the first industry-wide careers site, co-designed with over 100 leading employers, training providers and the Government, to address this need. The site advises young people about the exciting careers across the industry and provides hands on experiences through an Experience Matching Service. So far the site has attracted over half a million visitors. To improve workforce retention, construction employers need to ensure their workplaces are fair and inclusive, encouraging greater diversity and a bigger pool of talent to enter the industry. Clients of construction, the largest of which is the Government, have a key role to play in driving best practice across the sector, transforming how it supports its skills base.  Devolution and the industrial strategyThe strategy will need to support industry across England, Northern Ireland, Scotland and Wales, and within the regions. Looking at skills, each nation has a different approach to qualifications and skills funding, which are becoming more rather than less disparate. This can lead to a clash with UK-wide policy, as we are seeing with the Apprenticeship Levy. The strategy needs to ensure geographic and industrial growth strategies complement rather than compete with each other. To support this, training and skills interventions must respond to evidenced skills needs within local areas. Key enabling sectors of the UK economy, such as construction, must be seen as priority areas for skills and training investment in local skills plans.    Key to the success of the industrial strategy will be its longevity and the support it receives from industry, across the Government and all areas of the UK.   Yours sincerely,   Stephen Radley Director of Policy and Partnerships 2

ISG0154 - Evidence on Industrial strategyWritten evidence from the Publishers Association (ISG0154)IntroductionThe Publishers Association is the leading trade organisation serving book, journal, audio and electronic publishers in the UK. Our members represent over 80% of the UK publishing industry and include academic journal publishers such as Elsevier, Wiley, society publishers and University presses. We are responding to this inquiry to highlight the importance of including the creative industries within any industrial strategy the Government decides to pursue. In previous attempts to develop an industrial strategy, the creative industries have been overlooked despite their strong performance and global significance. For example, the creative industries were absent from a list of 11 sector specific strategies compiled by the Department for Business, Innovation and Skills in 2013, despite fulfilling the department’s criteria on which sectors should be supported by an industrial policy, namely that they should have prospects for future success.Any industrial strategy should focus on the sectors where the UK has a clear competitive advantage, is capable of rapid expansion and providing high skilled jobs. Although the creative industries are performing well by themselves, the sector would still benefit from an industrial strategy. Areas where an industrial strategy would be beneficial would include ensuring a strong intellectual property framework, helping the sector take advantage of fast-growing markets and making sure the government’s policies post-Brexit do not impede the sector’s growth. The importance of the creative industries The creative industries are one of the UK’s greatest strengths, contributing a substantial amount to the UK economy. It is one of the few sectors where the UK is a true world leader, helping to boost its reputation internationally and grow its soft power around the world. Government statistics show that the creative industries were worth £84.1bn in 2014, accounting for 5.2% of the UK economyThe creative industries grew 8.9% in 2014 - almost double the UK economy as a wholeBetween 2008 and 2014 Gross Value Added for the creative industries grew by 37.5%, faster than any Blue Book sector over the same period The creative industries supports 1.8m jobs, with employment in the sector growing 5% from 2013 to 2014, more than double the average growth of 2.1% across the wider UK economyThe value of services exported by the creative industries in 2014 was £19.8bn, accounting for 9% of total export services from the UK that year Global trade in creative goods grew on average 8.8% from 2002 to 2011, reaching $624bn in 2011. Growth in developing country exports averaged 12.1% during this period.The UK has the third largest film market in the world (BFI 2015), is the third largest exporter in the world for audio-visual and related services (Bakhshi et al 2013), the fourth largest market for book publishing (The PA 2015), and is the fourth largest producer of recorded music in the world (BPI 2015)Therefore any industrial strategy must feature the creative industries and recognise their importance to the UK economy as a whole.  Importance of the publishing sectorWithin the creative industries the book publishing sector is a success story in itself, and an integral part of the creative industries overall strength. Publishing exports from the UK grew the fastest out of all the creative sectors from 2009-2014, jumping 165.7%. In 2013-14 exports grew by 62.8%, again putting publishing ahead of the other creative industries. Statistics from the Publishers Association shows that the UK publishing industry was worth £4.4bn in 2015, with digital sales contributing 32% to this totalExport revenues reached £1.42bn in 2014, with physical and digital book exports contributing 43% to the industry’s total sales revenue, 54% when including journal salesOver 35% of physical book export revenues came from Europe last year. Exports to the Middle East and Asia and South America are increasing, and now make up 16% and 17% of the export market respectivelyUK books are behind the top three grossing film franchises of all time – James Bond, Harry Potter, Lord of the Rings. Five of the 20 global box office successes of the last 15 years are based on novels by UK writers.Wolf Hall, the current winner of the BAFTA award for the best drama series and Game of Thrones, current winner of the Emmy award for best Primetime drama, were both based on novelsTop West End and Broadway hits such as Matilda, Charlie and the Chocolate Factory, Les Miserables and Wicked were based on or adapted from novels Recommendations to government As the Government looks to develop its industrial strategy, the Publishers Association has four main recommendations: At a time when the UK is looking to negotiate a new relationship with the world post-Brexit, it is vital that it takes advantage of its strongest sectors. This means that the government’s industrial strategy should focus on sectors which have a strong growth potential and where the UK is already a world-leader. As the creative industries are one of the UK’s fastest growing and most successful sectors, it must be included in any industrial strategy. A recent report from the Confederation of British Industries (CBI) highlighted the importance of having an industrial strategy for the creative sector. As it notes, the UK’s strong reputation in this area means it is in a formidable position to take advantage of rising global demand for the creative industries. A strategy for the creative industries should not overlook the importance and strength of the publishing industry, both in terms of the value it adds to the UK economy and also to its importance to the creative economy.  The government should support the creative industries by ensuring its value can continue to be protected through a strong intellectual property regime, ensuring it retains easy access to existing markets and can take advantage of growing ones, and ensuring it can continue to have access to the talent and skills it needs. We would also echo the recommendations made in the CBI’s report referenced above.  September 2016

ISG0153 - Evidence on Industrial strategy Written evidence from BT (ISG0153) BT is pleased to respond to the Committee.  In light of the strategic nature of this inquiry, rather than address each question, we will provide a general response in order to try to help the Committee with its inquiry. We believe that a long-term industrial strategy is needed to ensure that the UK can thrive post Brexit.  The outcome of this industrial strategy should be sustainable and responsible economic growth that benefits all.   This will require increased focus on global competitiveness and raised productivity across all sectors.  To help achieve this we need the right conditions for private investment along with clarity in the role of the Government who should promote good regulation and remove red tape.   Past industrial strategies have seen patchy success.  Experience has highlighted that the Governments have found it difficult to predict the future and pick winners either by sector or technology.   A new industrial strategy needs to fully understand the lessons from the past and why some sectors have become winners, eg, the UK car industry, while others have not.   The Government needs to fully evaluate the costs and benefits before making industry intervention decisions.   A successful industrial strategy is one that plans and prepares for the long term but does not seek to predict exact solutions, rather allows for change with extensive input and cooperation from business. Key policies should aim to deliver: Raised productivity levels across all industry sectors. There is growing evidence that effective use of technology combined with investment in skills, intellectual property and new management processes offer significant potential to improve business performance and drive productivity growth[1]. An industrial strategy needs to drive this across all sectors.   There is also a need for organisations that can operate with effective scope, scale and pace acting as the economic engine for sector ecosystems.TechUK, the UK’s prominent trade association, has highlighted the potential for technology-driven productivity growth.  It has stated that the technology sector has demonstrated the highest level of productivity of any sector in the UK economy pre and post the 2008 financial crisis[2]. The reason for this is that tech firms of all sizes have been early adopters of new ‘general purpose technologies’ (GPTs) emanating from the sector and have been effective at using those technologies to disrupt traditional ways of doing things and developing new, more efficient business models. By contrast, in many other areas of the economy, we have seen reduced business investment in new technology and a lack of investment in intangible capital, leading to low or negative productivity growth.   We are now seeing the importance of broadband to driving the economy and increasing productivity.    A KPMG report published in September 2015, Delivering Britain’s digital future: an Economic Impact Study for BT, which considered the economic impact of potential advancements in the UK fixed-telecoms access infrastructure found:increased broadband speeds, available as a result of further investment in broadband infrastructure, will contribute to GDP growth of £3.6 to £6.3 billion per annum between 2015 and 2025. Faster broadband speeds will enable businesses to execute tasks more quickly and embed increasingly sophisticated software into their processes. It will also allow firms to develop new products and services to offer to consumers. All of these will add to GDP.A study by economic  consultants Regeneris ‘Superfast broadband – boosting business and the UK economy’,  in 2012 estimated that fibre broadband could generate an annual increase in Gross Value Add (GVA) of 0.3% - 0.5% per annum over 15 years.  The study included case-studies of a rural area in the UK, a UK town, a UK city and London.A study by economic consultants SQW, UK Broadband Impact Study, published in November 2013 found that public intervention in fibre was projected to return approximately £20 in net economic impact for every £1 of public investment.      A supercharged digital economy as the foundation of the UK economy.  The UK’s digital economy is a global success story and a sector that should be at the heart of the UK’s industrial strategy.  The digital economy and technology sectors now impact and enable all consumer and business activity. The UK is at the forefront of the digital economy and digital infrastructure developments today and it is a leader in the vast majority of indices of digital readiness in terms of infrastructure and its application, eg: the UK’s digital economy is the largest as a proportion of GDP in the G20 at 10% of UK GDP[3] the UK leads EU’s Big 5 for digital performance[4]. the UK is a Global Broadband Internet Leader[5]one of the quickest and most ambitious rollouts of superfast broadband anywhere in the world. More UK households are using superfast broadband than in Germany, France, Italy or Spain.  Nine out of ten UK homes have access to superfast broadband. And we are adding another home every 30 seconds.Over 91% of UK premises have access to superfast broadband and 96% have access to 10Mbps (higher than any other major EU Member State)We are on track to help government achieve its target of 95% coverage by the end of 2017. Furthermore, commercial investors in digital infrastructure will of course promote the take-up and use of this infrastructure as far as they can, but this is an area where Government policy more generally can assist.  For example, the Government could ensure that consumers and businesses have the skills and the knowledge to understand and exploit the benefits such an infrastructure is capable of delivering, ensuring that the Government online services are the “go to” method of interaction for the vast majority, and ensuring that consumers are aware of and encouraged to take up new, faster services.To help speed up superfast broadband infrastructure roll out the Government should continue to help reduce unnecessary red tape, cost and delay associated with civil infrastructure deployment, eg, planning regulations, road-closure permitting, access to power, etc.  An effective, stable, evidence-based and consistent regulatory framework with joined-up Government that drives continued investment and innovation.  BT has played a vital role in the making the UK a world-leading digital economy.  It has invested nearly £25bn in the UK over the last 10 years with £20bn invested in UK networks and over £10bn invested in Openreach.  However, the significant investments being made in new ICT infrastructure in the UK, by BT and others, is long term and not without risk. This needs to be reflected in the regulatory environment with a longer term strategic approach to regulations that removes uncertainty and risk from these long-term investments.   Critical to this is ensuring that the UK continues to benefit from fit and proper oversight of the quality of regulatory decisions. Regulators do not always get things right and a reliable merit-based appeals mechanism is essential to promote the development of better regulation in the first place, and when it fails to do so to ensure mistakes can be effectively corrected.  It is this environment that promotes investment and promotes good outcomes for consumers and businesses.  Proposals under the Digital Economy Bill would lead to greater uncertainty and foment a more unpredictable regulatory regime brought about by an unappealable regulator, potentially meaning investment would be more difficult to generate.  The existing appeals regime is well-understood and any changes would inevitably result in more frequent and more protracted legal battles as the proper meaning of the new standard is worked out.  This is a view expressed by almost all participants in the UK's digital economy and the vast majority of investment providers.   A successful industrial strategy would also promote the following policy development principles:evidence–based, with decisions predicated on open and transparent fact-base and modelling assumptionstechnology neutral, with a focus on delivering outcomes efficiently and effectivelya long-term view to enhancing investment and competition to achieve consumer benefitsconsistent in interpretation and application, otherwise there is a risk of simultaneously helping and hindering the same business.  For example, the Digital Economy Bill aims “to help telecommunications providers build the infrastructure needed for faster broadband.”  However, at the same time the Valuation Office Agency is proposing a huge increase in the Rateable Value for connectivity network infrastructure which could drive significant increases in Cumulo tax across the sector (a tax on network infrastructure as opposed to buildings).  Other fibre investors will no doubt see similar increases if this is carried through.   This is being proposed at a time when the Government is calling for more investment in broadband (eg, 10Mbps Universal Service Obligation).  This huge increase in BT’s and the wider fibre investors’ tax bill will compromise investment in the UK’s broadband network.   A successful industrial strategy requires coordination of policies.    A well-educated and highly skilled workforce relative to the rest of the world, to ensure the UK remains well positioned in an increasingly competitive global environment.   Given the pace of skills development and education in other parts of the world, the UK will need to invest significantly in education and workforce skill development to retain its current position. In defining the types of skills necessary for success, workers across the wider economy will need to possess strong analytical, critical thinking and communications skills, while being highly adaptive as technology-driven innovation continues to reshape all sectors. Moreover, as all sectors increasingly become technology driven, virtually all will need to have a basic competence across a broad set of common digital skills (use of communications devices and software, internet navigation, social media, etc) if they are to be fully effective in their chosen vocation. Technical roles will require the broad competencies mentioned above but with further and deeper emphasis on specific digital skills depending on the nature of their role and sector they are in. From BT’s point of view, skilling future workers in cyber security, digital media, data science and specialised diagnostic skills, will enable us to remain not only competitive but at the forefront of customer and business global requirements. We hope the Government’s industrial strategy will consider how education can provide these skills that are becoming more vital to the UK. As a major player in the UK tech sector, BT wants to see the UK’s digital economy thrive but there is already a skills shortage that will affect future productivity and competitiveness. The UK’s potential cannot be fulfilled unless young people are ‘tech literate’. Every child needs to be able to access and use everyday technology, be confident with the concepts of how it works and embrace its impact in society. Our economy will not generate just more specialist tech jobs; almost every job in the future will have some elements of tech.  Government policy must lift the levels of UK tech literacy starting with children at primary school.    In March 2015, BT announced a long term ambition from BT, to help build a culture of tech literacy for the nation. This is a long-term commitment and we expect that it will take a whole school generation. As our first goal, we want to reach five million children by 2020. Translation of innovation and entrepreneurship into world-leading companies, and the exploitation of the UK’s world-class scientific research base. It is widely recognised that there needs to be increased focus on supporting successful high-growth companies that have the potential to ‘scale-up’ and take the world-stage.   The Scale-Up Report (Sherry Coutu, November 2014) identifies the key barriers facing high-growth companies with the potential to scale-up.  The report states clearly that access to sufficient talent to staff business is the priority, followed by leadership skills necessary to grow a company many times faster than “normal.” Finance is not in itself the primary issue. However, the short-term investment mind-set that is prevalent in the UK does lead to companies turning to investors in the US and Asia for follow-on funding. This in turn means the UK can lose out not only on the financial gains of successful companies, but the skills and experience that might be re-cycled into new companies.  National initiatives (eg, to address skills and finance) must be complemented by regional programmes that proactively build upon the UK’s strategic R&D assets.   With an investment of £2.6Bn in research and development over the last five years, BT is the largest investor in industrial R&D in the ICT sector in the UK.  BT’s Adastral Park, Suffolk, is a world-renowned research centre with recent achievements including world-firsts in quantum communications (quantum key distribution over deployed fibre), high-capacity optical-fibre transmissions (largest ever optical ‘super-channel’) and the demonstration of the fastest G.Fast copper broadband speeds in the world (5Gbps). Adastral Park is also home to over 70 start-up companies as well as R&D teams from companies such as Huawei, Cisco and Intel.    Whilst our achievements are the result of extensive collaboration between BT, UK universities and industry there is a clear opportunity to go further and provide co-located training that ensures that graduates and post-doctoral students have the skills necessary to work in both corporations and high-growth scale-up companies. Specifically, the Government’s industrial strategy should:identify UK R&D centres of strategic importance within their industries and the academic community;invest in co-located academic training centres that enable universities to draw upon the industry expertise;support the creation and re-location of high-growth companies such that they have direct access to talent and the sector ecosystem. BT is an active participant in the current EU-funded research programme (H2020) and was the largest UK industry participant in the preceding EU research programme (FP7). In addition, BT led the establishment of the EU-funded EIT (European Institute for Innovation Technology) Innovation Node in London.    The Government should seek to minimise the potential negative impact of Brexit on the UK research eco-system by:making an early statement, jointly with the EU, confirming the status of EU researchers in the UK, and UK researchers in the EU, after the UK’s exit;publicising the Treasury commitment to fund the remaining costs on projects that are “live” at the date of exit to European organisations which are the potential partners for the UK organisations participating in these funding programmes;establishing a means of utilising the EU research programmes post-exit that is equally accessible to academia and industry (large, medium, and small);negotiating a post-exit agreement that allows UK organisations to continue to have open access to the EU research programmes;establishing a post-exit funding model for UK participants that does not cause them to be “second class” partners in project consortia;seeking continued access to those parts of the programme that support innovation (EIT) and researcher development (Marie Curie);considering making EU collaboration programmes that do not rely on EU funding (like the EUREKA!) more accessible during the negotiation phase by allocating more funding for UK participation and removing the current restriction to SME funding. A reinforced commitment to investing in science by the Government.  The UK’s world-class achievements in academic and industrial research lie at the heart of new technological breakthroughs.  To continue to maintain its record, the Government must recognise that, at less than 0.2% of GDP, the UK is now well behind countries such as Germany, China and the USA in its level of investment in engineering and physical sciences. It is essential to reverse this trend. In addition, the Government’s industrial strategy should continue to support industrial research whilst ensuring that measures such as the R&D tax credit and Patent Box are sufficiently targeted to ensure reinvestment into research.Enabling existing businesses to adapt and gain advantage from disruptive technologies is essential to ensuring the UK economy remains innovative and competitive. Organisations such as InnovateUK and the Catapults should explicitly recognise the need to support established businesses alongside the creation of new businesses. Corporates in particular provide an essential lens onto the needs and opportunities for UK industry, for small businesses as well as large; the needs of large corporates often represent new opportunities for start-up businesses, and in the case of BT, providing a world-class digital infrastructure directly translates to growth opportunities for the UK overall. Promotion of socially responsible businesses.  An industrial strategy needs to promote and ensure businesses act ethically and responsibly and in a way that respects customers, employees, suppliers and other stakeholders. This is essential to maintain their trust and strengthen reputation.   All of this supports the delivery of sustainable profitable revenue growth and helps to attract investors who include environmental, social and governance criteria when they decide which UK businesses to invest in.  A successful UK industrial strategy will be based on a positive, enduring and certain relationship with the EU. This should be based on an open trade model with an accent on zero tariffing or the minimum possible non-tariff or customs barriers, allied to a forensic focus on promoting and securing the key strengths of the UK economy.    UK and EU market confidence is interdependent and this derives substantially from similar or common economic and regulatory systems and governance. We need Single Market access and the availability of talented people and skilled labour in order for the economy to run both in the private and public sectors.  Strong foundations built on a well-trained, educated and healthy workforce, world class infrastructure e.g. transport, energy, and affordable housing particularly in London and the South East.  Without these foundations an industrial strategy will struggle to deliver the aforementioned outcomes. September 20162 [1] ITIF (2014) Raising European Productivity Growth through ICT retrieved from http://www2.itif.org/2014-raising-eu-productivity-growth-ict.pdf   [2] (2) ONS (September 2010). Multi-factor Productivity – Indicative Estimates, 2010. Retrieved from http://www.ons.gov.uk/ons/dcp171766_278729.pdf[3] Source Boston Consulting Group May 2015 % of GDP driven by Internet (eGDP) [4] Source: European Commission Digital Economy & Society Index March 2015 Digital performance - connectivity, digital skills, integration of digital technology, online activities and digital public services. [5] The Media Institute Global Broadband Internet Ecosystem Index April 2015 Categories assessed include applications and content, devices, investment in telecommunications,  fixed broadband connection speed, prices for fixed and mobile broadband services, available WiFi locations, 4G LTE penetration

ISG0152 - Evidence on Industrial strategyWritten evidence from Tata Steel UK (ISG 152) Tata Steel UKTata Steel UK is one of Europe’s leading steel manufacturers, with steel making facilities located in Port Talbot, Wales and Rotherham in South Yorkshire, as well as rolling and processing facilities across the UK. The company currently employs over 10,500 people in the UK, including 300 apprentices and 60 graduate trainees. The company also supports more than 50 PhD and Engineering doctorates across the UK, some of whom go on to work at Tata Steel’s own UK R&D centres, which we are currently seeking to upgrade and expand. Since its purchase of Corus in 2007, Tata Steel has invested over £1.5bn into its UK operations to upgrade plant and technology and develop its cutting-edge capabilities. This includes the complete refurbishment of one of Port Talbot’s two blast furnaces in 2013 as part of a £250m investment, a new £25m ‘reversing rougher’ at the Strip Mill in Wales in 2014 and the installation of a new, highly innovative £15m aerospace Vacuum Induction Melting furnace at Stocksbridge last year. Tata Steel has also committed large sums in working capital support to the UK operations, which continues to face significant financial and legacy challenges. Alongside these continual investments, the business has made huge progress on its strategy of increasingly focusing on premium, differentiated products for the most demanding customer sectors. This is backed by the business’ cultivated, domestic R&D capabilities - which have introduced more than 100 new products to our UK offering in the last few years; from 3D printing to advanced alloys and graphene applications. Tata Steel is actively developing the digital capabilities to succeed in an Industry 4.0 world. This ongoing transformation has not been without challenge and pain however – with major portfolio restructuring taking place (including the sale of our Long Products business earlier in 2016) as well as multiple rounds of rationalisations across the business. All of these actions bring strategic focus and demonstrate the relentless need for cost competitiveness and long-term, countercyclical planning. Steel – a strategic Foundation Industry for the UKSteel represents the foundation of many of the UK’s strategic supply-chains; suppling products and services to customers across numerous high-value, perennial manufacturing sectors. The automotive, construction and aerospace industries are major steel consumers for example – from tractors to tanks, stadiums to skyscrapers. Almost 50% of UK carmakers’ steel requirements are met by Tata Steel UK, with chassis, axels and body panels supplied to the likes of Nissan, BMW and JLR. Every 1p, 2p and 5p coin in circulation in the UK originates from steel forged in Port Talbot. British-made steel has been used to build some of the UK’s most recognisable landmarks, including The Shard and Wembley Stadium. An effective industrial strategy should encourage and support greater use of UK-made materials within these and other sectors, in turn capturing greater value for the UK and filling-in existing gaps in supply chains. As a highly productive industry, steel represents a significant growth engine - with productivity levels 1.4x higher than the manufacturing sector average and employment multipliers of 2-3x. This impact is concentrated in areas across Wales, Yorkshire and the Humber, the North East and the Midlands that unfortunately have not typically seen the same economic opportunities as London and the South East.A prosperous domestic steel sector will not only capture considerable value for the UK but it will also act as the catalyst for an effective industrial strategy. This results in shared innovation, best practice and a critical mass of skills development and retention, as associated sectors and industries within supply chains proliferate. Large, established organisations such as Tata Steel can act as anchors and engines of growth within this common industrial environment - making UK manufacturing and industry as a whole more adaptable and dynamic.  In short, a vibrant steel sector acts not just as a foundation for the UK’s existing strategic supply chains, it can and should act as a foundation of an ‘industrial commons’ (to utilise the phrase coined by Gary Pisano of Harvard Business School); driving and maintaining tangential economic growth and opportunity nationwide. The role of GovernmentThe opportunity exists not only to safeguard the current value of the UK steel industry, but grow it. Supporting the sector is not only important in and of itself, but will also be of great significance to the industry’s manufacturing supply chains – sectors such as aerospace and automotive, which are also facing a period of uncertainty in light of Brexit.   Governments and other stakeholders such as trade unions and regulators play a crucial role in shaping the operational environment and creating a regulatory framework contusive to growth and robust in the face of cyclical market conditions. Following the UK sector Steel Summit in Rotherham in October, 2015, and the Committee’s subsequent inquiry into the industry, some worthwhile action has been taken. However, energy costs, business rates and the UK and European markets’ continued susceptibility to foreign market distortion remain a considerable drag on the UK sector. Regulation derogations and enhanced public procurement practices are welcome – but have so far merely acted to mitigate adverse conditions as opposed to offering material assistance in establishing a level playing field for the UK steel industry. In order to deliver better prospects for a sustainable environment, there are three broad areas which Tata Steel feels can be readily addressed: A level playing field: ensuring that policy driven costs (e.g. energy costs, business rates) and trade policy (e.g. anti-dumping measures) are set at a level that allows UK steel makers to compete with international competitors. The sector does not require or want subsidies, but simply the opportunity to compete fairly.  The right long term foundations: like many advanced manufacturing sectors, long term success will depend on innovation, skills and sustainability. Therefore, access for the steel sector to innovation support and skills funding support is vital. Equally, a challenging but competitive and realistic environmental framework is important to drive standards, but not drive firms out of business – which leads to both commercial as well as carbon leakage. Separately, a closer integration of sectors such as steel with procurement and consuming sector strategies would lay the foundations for capturing greater value across supply chains. Tata Steel also welcomes the potential of greater visibility of infrastructure development and the previous administration’s recognition of socio-economic factors within public procurement. These changes, whilst welcome, need to be put into practice, tracked & monitored if they are to benefit the sector.Supporting periods of transition: steel is a cyclical sector and one undergoing structural change. There is an important role for companies, governments and others to work together to mitigate the adverse social consequences of change (e.g. regeneration), maintain physical and human capital (e.g. asset maintenance, skills retention) and support ownership change (e.g. addressing pensions barriers, appropriate financing support). Demand for steel fluctuates in line with macroeconomic conditions and it is vital that the UK sector remains in a position to capitalise on increased domestic and global demand, as well as continuing to support UK manufacturing. Market entry costs to the steel sector are considerable.  These more sector specific points sit, of course, upon a number of critical ‘horizontal’ developments of broad economic benefit – e.g. STEM in schools, lifelong learning, infrastructure development (both physical and digital).  Tata Steel strongly welcomes the new Government’s renewed emphasis on industrial strategy and the Secretary of State’s recognition that policy making should be long-term, predictable and sustained in order to create an environment in which businesses can expand and prosper. We believe this joined-up approach provides the best opportunity to catalyse the growth of value for the UK steel sector. We stand ready to play a full part in the Government’s development of this strategy.  30 September 2016

submitter: Association of Directors of Environment Economy Planning & Transport (ADEPT)
text: ISG0151 - Evidence on Industrial strategy Written evidence from Groupe Eurotunnel (ISG 151) Eurotunnel welcomes the opportunity to respond to this inquiry. An industrial strategy for the UK is a welcome move, demonstrating a commitment from the Government to supporting Britain’s industries, but we also believe there are certain considerations which must be taken into account.As part of our response we will focus primarily on the questions relating to sector specific strategies, and geographical emphasis.OverviewGroupe Eurotunnel (GET) manages and operates the Channel Tunnel Fixed Link between Britain and France, providing the infrastructure for Eurotunnel’s own Shuttle services, international freight and high speed passenger trains. Completed in 1994, the Tunnel was financed entirely from private sources at no cost to the taxpayer.GET also operates GB Railfreight, as the British arm of its Europorte rail freight brand. Aquired by Europorte in 2010, the business operates a variety of services including bulk traffic, biomass, coal steel, petrochemicals and metals transportation. GB Railfreight prides itself on its innovative approach to railfreight which has seen it win a number of rail industry awards and earn the standing as Britain’s most reliable freight operator.GET also recently acquired 100% of ElecLink. ElecLink was established jointly by Groupe Eurotunnel and Star Capital in 2011 to develop a high voltage direct current interconnector to link the electricity markets of Great Britain and France via the Channel Tunnel. Once operational, ElecLink will increase the existing interconnection capacity between the two countries by 50% thereby enhancing security of electrical supply for both countries and increasing electricity supply for both businesses and domestic consumers. Due to the use of the Channel Tunnel infrastructure, ElecLink has a very low environmental impact and very high reliability supporting the efficient utilisation of generation sources in both countries.ResponseSector based strategiesIt is not yet clear the extent to which the Government’s industrial strategy will follow a sector-based approach, but GET would caution against the Government pursuing this too stringently.As the operator of the Channel Tunnel, GET provides crucial infrastructure for the transport of goods to and from mainland Europe. The opening of the Channel Tunnel revolutionised the UK’s accessibility to the European market, which can be reached faster and more reliably than at any time in the past. The UK economy directly benefits from the mature fixed link to mainland Europe. Key industry sectors have built import and export business models that are only viable because of the Tunnel. For example the automotive industry imports key components for time sensitive manufacturing, assembles high value products and then exports them; fish from Scotland and meat from Ireland can build key export markets across Europe, The pharmaceuticals, electronics and express courier markets all thrive because of access through the Channel Tunnel. Prior to the Tunnel there was no economically sustainable business model to facilitate the UK’s full participation in this trading revolution.The total value of trade which passed through the Channel Tunnel in 2014 was £91bn and represented 25% of UK trade with EU countries. This trade was spread between multiple sectors. The value of exports transported through the Tunnel in 2014 equated to £43.6bn with the top five sectors being: postal and courier (£9.9bn), computers and electronics (£8bn), motor vehicles, parts and other transport equipment (£6.1bn), and iron, steel and metal products (£4bn). And there are many other sectors beyond this which are supported by the Tunnel including pharmaceuticals, food and textiles.The Channel Tunnel is a crucial piece of infrastructure which allows these sectors to remain competitive with producers from across the world. In order to compete successfully in global markets, the most successful exporters have the most efficient operations. The Channel Tunnel supports this success by enabling streamlined production processes through the provision of a fast and reliable transport service.It is because of this symbiotic relationship that we believe an industrial strategy must consider sectors in conjunction with each other, not in isolation. For example, were the Government to decide to implement a strategy to support the automotive industry, a key consideration would have to be the infrastructure sector. The automotive industry relies heavily on ‘just-in-time’ methods and automotive companies across Europe have developed a business model which sees them source inputs from suppliers and plants spread across different geographies to be transported to one assembly and distribution site. For this model to be successful the industry has to rely on dependable infrastructure and therefore any automotive industrial strategy would be heavily entwined with an infrastructure one.The implementation of Operation Stack for 28 days last summer meant that the road network struggled to cope with traffic and many suppliers suffered heavy delays. Therefore while issues surrounding the strategic road network and the construction of a Lower Thames Crossing may seem entirely separate to the automotive sector, we know that they are not. The road network in Kent is vital for connecting the south of England to the Capital and the rest of the UK. With a predicted increase in truck traffic using the Channel Tunnel of 30% in the next five years, there is a clear need for additional capacity. Ensuring smooth traffic flows in Kent is vital to ensuring that suppliers can export their goods to and from the UK, and therefore vital for supporting industries such as the automotive industry – they cannot be considered separately.Geographically based strategiesThe second area GET would like to address in this consultation is whether the strategy should have a geographical emphasis. Again, GET’s position is that the Government’s should not become wedded to rigidly separate geographical strategies.The exports which travel through the Channel Tunnel come from all areas of the UK. The top two areas are the West Midlands and the East; 20% of exports come from the West Midlands, while 18% come from the East. But industries are supported all over the country.  The Channel Tunnel supports £1bn worth of motor vehicles, parts and other transport equipment from Wales; £300m of fish, fruit, vegetables and flowers exports from Scotland; £2.4bn worth of postal and courier freight from the East; and £530m of computers and electronics exports from the South East.Taking the fresh food sector as an example, £0.9bn of fish, fruit, vegetables and flowers, £1.4bn of meat and £2.1bn of processed and prepared food was exported through the Channel Tunnel in 2014. These are sourced from all over the UK, with the main sources of the exports being Scotland, the South, and the Midlands.The Channel Tunnel facilitates trade for these regions and should not just be considered an asset to the South East and Kent. Additionally, as discussed above, improvements to the road network in the South East should be viewed in the context of improving traffic flows and therefore import and export times for companies across the UK. This is why a strict geographical separation of strategies could be problematic -  it risks not recognising how the transport network links up different areas.ConclusionIn conclusion, the new emphasis on an industrial strategy is to be welcomed as it demonstrates a renewed commitment from the Government to ensure the success of sectors across the UK. But any strategy must take into account the ways in which all the different sectors and locations in the UK are interconnected, and be mindful not to treat them in isolation. 30 September 2016

ISG0150 - Evidence on Industrial strategy                             Written evidence from Midlands Connect (ISG 150) Thank you for your letter dated September 15th notifying us of the Committee’s intention to expand its current inquiry into the Northern Powerhouse and Midlands Engine to look more broadly at the Industrial Strategy.As the nation’s manufacturing heartland, the Midlands is the largest economic area outside of London, with the largest population, sitting at the crossroads of Britain.  The Government has confirmed the role of Midlands Engine as a key player in the Industrial Strategy and the Midlands Engine Partnership is determinant to support Government in this vitally important undertaking. I understand that the Committee’s deadline for submitting further evidence is 27 September 2016 but that you would also consider late submissions. With this in mind the Midlands Engine has prepared an initial response (see Appendix A) which we hope will prove valuable in the early stages of the Commission’s work on Industrial Strategy. In addition we are currently in the process of commissioning an independent thought leadership study in conjunction with the Policy Exchange (https://policyexchange.org.uk/) on what a modern industrial strategy for Britain might look like.  The outcomes of this important commission will be published before the end of this year when we will endeavour to submit a final response to the Committee. In light of this I would appreciate it if you could confirm the Committee’s intention to consider our final response (to be submitted by the end of December) as further evidence to inform the Committee’s’ final report in 2017.I take this opportunity to offer the Midlands Engine Partnership support, should it felt appropriate by the Committee, in any of the public evidence sessions to be held in the autumn. 30 September 2016 BIS Select Committee Inquiry – Industrial StrategyEvidence Submission from the Midlands Engine Introduction This initial evidence paper has been developed by the Midlands Engine Supervisory Board.  It sets out initial thinking in relation to the BIS Select Committee Inquiry into Industrial Strategy. It is also intended as a starting point for discussion on the future direction of Industrial Strategy with Government and the role that the Midlands Engine may play in this. The paper has been developed in conjunction with the Nottingham Business School (Nottingham Trent University) and City REDI (part of the Birmingham Business School, University of Birmingham) working with the Midlands Engine Economic Planning and Strategic Leadership Groups. The paper responds to each of the questions raised by the Select Committee.                  What does the Government mean by industrial strategy, and what does the private sector want from one? The Midlands Engine understanding of industrial strategy is that it represents: “... any type of intervention or government policy that attempts to improve the business environment or to alter the structure of economic activity toward sectors, technologies or tasks that are expected to offer better prospects for economic growth or societal welfare than would occur in the absence of such intervention,” (Warwick/OECD 2013) Within this broad and inclusive definition, we may think of ‘horizontal’ interventions intended to enhance the business environment across the board and ‘vertical’ interventions that target particular sectors, industrial clusters or technologies. A comprehensive industrial strategy should comprise both horizontal and vertical interventions and should share the overall objective of enhancing productivity. Although efforts to increase the  quantity of outputs are important in the short term, in the long term a focus on productivity is essential because it is a prerequisite for maintaining and improving international competitiveness, economic and societal wellbeing and, ultimately, living standards. “Productivity isn’t everything, but in the long run it is almost everything. A country’s ability to improve its standard of living over time depends almost entirely on its ability to raise its output per worker.” (Krugman 1994).  Furthermore, place based and sectoral approaches to industrial strategy need to be combined. To see these approaches as alternatives is to perpetuate a false dichotomy (See response to questions 5 and 6 below). An integrated strategy built upon mutually re-enforcing elements will ensure the impact of the whole is greater than the sum of the parts. Feedback from private sector partners confirms that there is an appetite among Midlands’ businesses to work closely with the public sector to develop and implement an industrial strategy along these lines that can improve productivity.  For example, some of the UK’s largest manufacturers (led by Rolls Royce) are working within the Midlands Engine to improve the resilience and productivity of supply chains, a task which benefits from strong collaboration between public and private sectors.                 How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy, for example in preventing foreign takeovers of UK companies? This question is as much philosophical-based as practical. It is also a question around which entrenched ideological positions tend to coalesce. The Midlands Engine would argue for a pragmatic ‘mixed economy’ approach to ensure that progress is not held back by debate. Where markets are capable of delivering socially desirable outcomes, they should be encouraged so to do and the case for public intervention is weak. Where this is not the case, government should be prepared to intervene using all the powers and resources at its disposal.  There are certainly risks associated with state intervention. Not all industrial policy interventions will succeed. This is in part a function of the kind of risk taking and experimentation characteristic of new path creation in economic development (Martin 2010, Mazzucato 2013). Failures of government can and will occur, but this should not be seen as an argument against intervention per se. There is a growing body of evidence (both international and from the UK) to suggest that state intervention can be instrumental in helping to create new paths of economic development, particularly at the local and regional scale. Mazzucato (2013) has highlighted the crucial role that public sector investment has played in the development of many technologies that are now taken for granted. Locally, in the Midlands, we can identify notable examples of modest public investment being used to facilitate ‘new path creation’ at a local level.  The case of BioCity Nottingham is an obvious example where public investment facilitated the re-tasking of redundant industrial laboratory that is now home to one of the biggest concentration of biotechnology companies in Europe. Outside the Midlands we can identify other instances of public investment resulting in transformative impacts; the relocation of the BBC and associated regeneration of Salford Quays as Media City is a prominent example. In relation to foreign takeovers, it is the location, scale and nature of economic activity that matters most in terms of economic and social outcomes. However, there may be wider strategic policy concerns that it is appropriate for government to consider such as food or energy security or defence.  To this end, the Midlands Engine would expect government to consult widely when determining ‘public interest’ in such cases. This consultation should extend to local and regional partners. Tests of ‘public interest’ should consider the local and regional social and economic impact of a proposed takeover, and specifically the potential for large scale redundancies that can bear heavily on communities and place a significant social security burden on the state.                  What lessons can be learnt from:• Previous governments' industrial strategies?• Other countries' attempts to develop industrial strategies? Despite recent growth reviews, such as those led by Heseltine and Adonis, development of Industrial Strategy has been challenging for government. It can be argued that a national Industrial Strategy has not been developed by successive governments for many years. Often, the development of economic strategies have been left to regional and local agencies or partnerships, working in a ‘bottom up’ manner. The case against industrial policy primarily rests on practical difficulties with implementation. Even though the issues could in principle be settled by empirical evidence, the evidence is inconclusive and recent ‘what works’ initiatives have done little to enhance our collective ability to develop interventions with impact in the sphere of industrial strategy.  Industrial policy in the UK should enjoy parity of status with other aspects of economic policy (e.g. monetary and fiscal). Midlands Engine supports David Bailey’s proposal to establish an Office for (Regional and) Industrial Responsibility. This could facilitate independent assessment and evaluation of the impacts of industrial policy. Post-Brexit reflection has highlighted a need to seek a more cohesive and economically inclusive society. The prevailing assumption in most academic literature is that there needs to be a mixed economy approach based on private enterprise embedded within a framework of public action that encourages restructuring, diversification and technological dynamism. It is suggested that this, in the main, delivers beyond market forces alone. Although not directly comparable to advanced Western economies, comparative evidence from South East Asia and the BRIC economies has highlighted the potential value of sectorally-targeted intervention.  International comparative evidence from the US and Germany also demonstrates why strong regional institutional structures embed and sustain the competitiveness of their industries. Germany, in particular, has benefited from the stability and continuity of institutions with responsibilities influencing on industrial strategy. This evidence also shows that intervention needs to be specific, targeted and contribute to wider economic goals. The Midlands Engine supports these conclusions. The partnership is willing to work with government to deliver the outcomes of the Industry Strategy at regional level; working in a “cascading” manner to interpret and deliver the regional outcomes whilst influencing strategy and policy at the Combined Authority, Local Enterprise Partnership and Local Authority spatial levels that it embraces.                  What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions? Alignment between industrial policy and other policy domains within Government could be stronger. Given the strength of transport engineering in the Midlands, for example, the apparent dissonance between industrial and transport policy has been problematic for some time. Since rail privatisation in the 1990s there have been many instances of decisions taken in relation to the procurement of rolling stock and rail infrastructure acting to the detriment of rail supply chains in the UK. The work of the Rail Supply Group and others is seeking to address this. However, using this as an example, there is a fundamental requirement for all government departments to consider the regional economic development and industrial impacts of their policies going forward, and link them to the various spatial levels of influence that operate within the UK. Midlands Engine can support government to achieve this. Separately, transport infrastructure plays a crucial role in facilitating and enabling economic and industrial development. Major transport infrastructure projects should be used proactively to stimulate and support local and regional economies. It is for this reason that the strategic transport planning work of Midlands Connect is so important. The Midlands is fundamentally polycentric in nature. An effective and integrated transport network is an essential prerequisite for any polycentric region to realise its economic potential. This is particularly so in the Midlands where poor east-west and intraregional connectivity are widely seen as impediments to growth. Further, in terms of policy alignment, the recent NAO report on single departmental plans and the OECD reports demonstrate the need for government to operate strategically as it has on the alignment of energy with industrial policy responsibilities. The interface between industrial and skills policy is another example. The move to place skills policy unambiguously with education is also positive, but must not result in any loss of focus on the need to meet employers’ demand for skills and the importance of this agenda to the nation’s productivity.Any reversion to the traditional supply side focus of skills and education policy in the UK will be detrimental from a productivity and competitiveness perspective.                 What are the pros and cons of an industrial strategy adopting a sectoral approach?• Should the Government proactively seek to ‘pick winners’?• What criteria should be used to identify which sectors are supported?• Should the Government prop up traditional industries that it considers to be in the national interest?• If not a sectoral approach, should the industrial strategy have a broader objective, such as improving productivity? Future sectoral policy must be sensitive to place; just as place based economic development must be sectoral in focus. The only way that this can be achieved is by significant devolution of industrial strategy responsibility to sub-national levels, within a coherent national policy framework that prioritises productivity enhancement (for the reasons set out in response to question 1).  Past attempts to ‘pick winners’ have proved politically and economically problematic but it is important that this is not used as an excuse to move away from sectorally-targeted interventions within a broader strategic framework. There is a need for systematic analysis of sectoral composition and performance in order to ensure that strengths, be they local, regional or national, are identified and built upon. To expect local organisations to fulfil this function alone is both unrealistic and would be likely to result in unnecessary duplication of capacity. This capacity needs to be built at the level of larger combined authorities or pan-regional initiatives such as the Midlands Engine and the Northern Powerhouse. With such capacity in place, industrial strategy should become a co-product of central government and sub-national bodies. The Midlands Engine partners stand ready to support government in this endeavour. An important goal of industrial strategy should be the development of adaptive capability, such that historic sectoral strengths can be built upon and accumulations of human and physical capital applied to new and emerging opportunities from wherever they arise (Martin 2005). Industrial strategy and state intervention have an important role to play in facilitating these processes.  Defence of traditional industries per se should not be a goal of industrial strategy. However, some sectors have a functional role, scale or wider strategic significance that will necessitate state intervention to help address challenges that they may face. The identification of sectors, sub-sectors (or indeed clusters) as candidates for intervention is a notoriously challenging analytical task. A range of quantitative indicators will generally be needed such as:  Measures of scale (employment and output);Productivity measures;Measures of geographic concentration – such as location quotients;Historic growth trends;Forecast growth;Assessments of employment quality and knowledge intensity; andPresence of head office/R&D type facilities. There will, however, always be a need for more qualitative insights drawn from ’foresight’ work undertaken in partnership with relevant industry bodies. It is important to recognise that choices of sectoral priority can seldom be reduced to a simple analytical task. Policy makers must make choices that are informed by wider policy imperatives, objectives and an understanding of the practicability of intervention. It is unrealistic to expect decisions of this kind to follow simply from purely objective analysis.                  Should the industrial strategy have a geographical emphasis?• How should an industrial strategy link with devolution initiatives aimed at devolving taxation and decision making away from Westminster?• What examples are there of interventions from central Government that have successfully supported economic growth away from London and the South East of England?• How should the industrial strategy work with local authorities and Local Economic Partnerships, reconciling a U.K.-wide strategy and local, regional and devolved nations' priorities? The sectoral prioritisation characteristic of industrial policy in the UK has always had a geographic implication because the spatial distribution of sectors is uneven. Industrial sectors are not evenly distributed across space, providing local and regional economies with unique and specific strengths, weaknesses, challenges and opportunities to which economic development practitioners and industrial strategy must respond. Any industrial strategy must be sensitive to the distinctive characteristics of place and the structure of local and regional economies. It is essential that sources of regional and national comparative advantage are exploited to the full. This means empowering sub-national economic development bodies to develop industrial strategies sensitive to the specific sectoral composition of the local and regional economies for which they have responsibility. Delegation of the resources necessary to implement these strategies must accompany this.  The ability to do this is a function of strong analytical capacity and a locus of decision making that is embedded in the industrial milieu that is to be developed. It is hoped that the current consultation on business rate localisation should tackle the issue of incentivising business growth directly, through linking additional funding released to levers of industrial strategy. Policy interventions that have sought to address the differential growth rates of regions and localities within the UK go back to the 1930s.  Despite periodic episodes in which this object has been a prominent government policy objective, differential regional growth is a phenomenon that has proved remarkably resilient to these efforts. In part this may be a function of the financial and economic scale of the policy levers that have been deployed. It may also reflect the need for a more consistent policy focus over the very long term.  However, it is possible to identify examples of successful Government intervention that have supported the transference of economic activity away from London and the South East. Despite policy interest evidenced in initiatives such as the Lyons Review, the relocation of the BBC to Salford noted above (question 2) and the transference of the Office for National Statistics to Newport are rare examples of public bodies relocating their own functions in ways that have had impacts on the economies of their host destinations.  Welcome though these initiatives are, they have not tended to impact on long term growth. Recent government policy with this objective has tended to focus on the Northern Powerhouse. A similar policy and investment focus in the Midlands is required. The most successful industrial strategies blend a place-based understanding of economic development with the sectoral approach adopted by previous governments. Businesses are located both within supply chains and markets, which may be regional, national or global, and within a specific location.  Conditions must be mutually supportive within both dimensions for the specific businesses which make up an industry to thrive, become more productive and benefit the communities within which they are located. The key to dovetailing industrial strategy with wider devolution initiatives is to consider more explicitly and consistently the relationship (or fit) between spatial scale and specific functions or policy domains. From a Midlands Engine perspective, we see strategic transport integration as a key policy and practice domain that should be addressed at the scale of the Midlands; this being the rationale for Midlands Connect. This is the implication of our response to question 4 above.  There are also other policy and delivery responsibilities that should reside at the level of the Midlands. Responsibility for inward investment and destination marketing should both be fully devolved to this level. Similarly the geographic extent of key sectoral strengths linked to manufacturing, engineering and energy, coupled with the presence of 27 universities in the region, suggests that this is also the spatial scale at which it is sensible to devolve key responsibilities for innovation policy and support. Contemporary academic thinking about drivers and determinants of regional and local economic growth have increasingly emphasised the importance of strong and effective institutions in supporting growth.  The institutional architecture underpinning industrial strategy development and implementation in the UK has become increasingly complex with the emergence of city–deals, devo-deals at varying spatial scales, local enterprise partnerships, combined authorities and wider pan-regional initiatives.  It is welcome that Government has reaffirmed its commitment to the Northern Powerhouse and Midlands Engine. However, policy in this arena requires greater clarity and consistency. In particular the asymmetry between the funding and powers devolved to these initiatives should be addressed. The scale and functional significance of the Midlands to the nation’s economy demands it.  References Bailey, D., Cowling, K., & P.R Tomlinson (2015), New Perspectives on Industrial Policy for a Modern Britain, Oxford University Press.Krugman, P. (1994) The Age of Diminishing Expectations. MIT Press, Cambridge.Martin, R. (2005) Thinking about regional competitiveness, East Midlands Development Agency, Nottingham, http://irep.ntu.ac.uk/519/1/202832_thinkingaboutregionalcompetitiveness2005.pdf .Martin R. (2010) Roepke Lecture in Economic Geography Rethinking Regional Path Dependence: Beyond Lock-in to Evolution, Economic Geography, 86 (1).Mazzucato, M. (2013), The Entrepreneurial State: Debunking private vs. public sector myths, Anthem, London.NAO (2016) Government’s management of its performance: progress with single departmental plans https://www.nao.org.uk/report/governments-management-of-its-performance-progress-with-single-departmental-plans/  Warwick, K. (2013), “Beyond Industrial Policy: Emerging Issues and New Trends”, OECD Science, Technology and Industry Policy Papers, No. 2, OECD Publishing.http://dx.doi.org/10.1787/5k4869clw0xp-en 9

ISG0145 - Evidence on Industrial strategy   Written evidence from the Royal Aeronautical Society (ISG 145)  1.              Executive Summary 1.1              Civil and defence aerospace has a central role to play in contributing to the success of the UK’s long-term economic success, in providing continued, vital national security, and in responding constructively to the continuing pressure for reduced environmental impacts.  Aerospace also provides one of the key industrial measures available to supporting the UK’s long term prosperity outside of the EU. 1.2              The 2010-2015 Government recognised that the aerospace sector was and should remain a major contributor to UK Plc. and much valued support was provided through initiatives including the establishment of the Aerospace and Defence Growth Partnerships, as well as the Aerospace Technology Institute (ATI).  Such efforts are efficient industrial means by which the UK aerospace sector can recapture and maintain its reputation as a global competitor and enhance its huge export potential 1.3              The Government, with industry, is already taking steps now to support supply chain capability and competitiveness so the industry can deliver the Government’s current and future strategic objectives, within allocated budgets, whilst also exploiting international commercial opportunities for the national economic good. 1.4              Growth partnership strategies in civil and defence aerospace are proving an effective and efficient industrial mechanism for supporting and enabling co-investment for R&D and innovation, supporting skills development and addressing capacity shortages, as well as continually improving productivity to support the UK’s international competitiveness. 1.5              The Government’s stance on the defence industrial base is more vague and ambiguous, even with the establishment of the Defence Growth Partnerhip, as its policies as a customer of defence products and services have tended to work against supporting a health defence industry.  The Government has been inclined to procure overseas designed equipment to achieve a cheaper headline costs, despite the risks of eroding the defence industrial base and the broader national economic benefits it creates.  A better balance of safeguarding operational sovereignty whilst also delivering value for money for the taxpayer, will improve the health and growth potential of the military aerospace sector. 2.              About the Royal Aeronautical Society 2.1              The Royal Aeronautical Society (the Society) is the world's only professional body dedicated to the entire aerospace community. Established in 1866 to further the art, science and engineering of aeronautics, the Society has been at the forefront of developments in aerospace ever since.  The Society seeks to: i) promote the highest possible standards in aerospace disciplines; ii) provide specialist information and act as a central forum for the exchange of ideas; and iii) play a leading role in influencing opinion on aerospace matters. 2.2              The Society was involved in the production of the recently published refresh of the 2012 Aerospace Growth Strategy (AGP) strategy. 3a.              What does the Government mean by industrial strategy? 3.1              An industrial strategy is generally a strategic effort by government to support, preserve the success and encourage the development of sectors of the economy – particularly manufacturing sectors – to enhance economic growth.[1] 3.2              UK Governments have adopted numerous approaches to the provision of support for sectors of the national economy, particularly in relation to defence and civil aerospace (including space), such as industrial strategies, growth strategies and growth partnerships. 3.3              The new prime minister, shortly after taking office on 13 July 2016, announced that the Department of Business, Innovation and Skills would become the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (BEIS), with the new Secretary of State at BEIS being “charged with delivery of a comprehensive industrial strategy”[2].  Renaming the Department for Business makes a strong statement of intent and suggests a major role for supply-side reform. 3.4              The prime minister also established a new Economy and Industrial Strategy Committee[3] of cabinet, which she will chair, focused on “delivering one of the government’s top 3 priorities – an economy that works for everyone, with a strong industrial strategy at its heart”[4]. 3.5              While it is not yet clear how a new industrial strategy will differ from recent and existing ones, it is likely going to including some of the following elements, many of which are common to industrial strategies: (i)                   Improving productivity(ii)                 Encouraging innovation(iii)                Regional growth and development(iv)               Employing latest technology(v)                 Creating competitive advantage(vi)               Job creation and skills development, especially for younger people 3.6              Unique to a new or refreshed industrial strategy(ies) should be the provision of assistance to UK industries to have a chance of taking ‘advantages of the opportunities by Brexit[5]”, and the Society looks forward to seeing details of Government plans published sooner rather than later. 3b.              What does the private sector want from one?               Civil Aerospace 3.7              Aerospace has a central role to play in contributing to the success of the UK’s long-term economy prosperity, and providing continued vital support to national security.  The sector is one of the most productive, innovative and internationally competitive in the UK economy. 3.8              The UK is Europe’s leading aerospace manufacturing nation and second only to the US in the global market.  In 2015, the sector was responsible for £27 billion worth of exports, productivity has grown by 39% since 2010 – six times more than in the economy generally – and employs over 128,000 people across the whole of the UK[6]. 3.9              The outlook for the UK, and global, aerospace sector is positive.  According to the Aerospace Growth Partnership (AGP) “by 2034 it is estimated that over 38,000 new fixed-wing passenger aircraft, worth $5.5 trillion, will be required; in a similar time-scale the global requirement for new helicopters is expected to be in excess of 40,000 units, worth $165 billion; and demand for 9,000 business work jets worth $267 billion is also anticipated over the next decade”[7]. 3.10              For this success to continue, and for the sector to take advantage of the commercial opportunities available, which would in turn generate significant national socio-economic gain, the private sector looks to government to overcome market failure that create barriers to growth and to encouraging the private sector to co-operate more closely to address industry-wide challenges.  Such challenges of and solutions to supply chain competitiveness, technological advancement and innovation, and skills development, common to industrial strategies, have already been identified by the Government and industry in the Aerospace Group Partnership (AGP). 3.11              Having access to world-class technology is essential but value added cannot be achieved without also having access to the means and readiness to convert technology into product.  The current Government’s attention to the means, resources and skills to capitalise on access to world-class technology is welcomed and supported 3.12              Industry requires long-term predictability and certainty of Government support and public sector funding for R&D that goes beyond single spending review exercises and parliamentary cycles.  The Government understands the importance of having a more certain investment climate for R&D.  Alongside Government support, the aerospace industry match-funded public sector investment in civil aerospace research and development – a combined investment total of £2.1bn over seven years via the AGP. 3.13              The Aerospace Technology Institute (ATI) was launched in 2013 to guide government and industry on future aerospace research and technology (R&T) funding in the UK.  It delivers the technology aims of the Aerospace Growth Partnership, aiming to “secure the full economic potential growth of the sector through strategic technology investment.”[8]  The Government committed £1.05 billion in 2013 and a further £900 million in 2015[9], extending the programme to 2026.  Industry’s contribution brought total investment to £3.9billion, creating the long-term certainty for industry to make investment decisions. 3.14              Equally important should be the focus on the continued development of a skilled workforce.  As is well understood, the industry faces currently a number of ongoing challenges that combine to hinder its export and growth potential, such as a rapidly ageing workforce and difficulty recruiting young people – with the right types and levels of skills – into the industry.  3.15              Finally, the UK has a highly competitive space industry that has benefited from partnership with successive Governments.  A Space Growth Action Plan[10]  is currently in place, with the stated objective to continue the 8% per annum growth of the sector so that by 2030 the UK will have 10% of the global market.[11]  The Society recommends continued Government support for the plan and its implementation as part of any new industrial strategy initiative.               Defence (including Defence Aerospace) 3.16              The UK’s defence sector, including defence aerospace, already provides world-class defence products and services, generating an annual turnover of £22billion[12], with exports of over £9.8billion in 2013[13], and directly employing 162,400 individuals[14].  UK defence is still a large industry but without major effort by Government and industry this may not be the case in years to come. 3.17              Similar to the civil aerospace sector, the defence sector operates in a global marketplace and for the UK to be more successful in defence exports, Government must be involved in selling UK products and services, supporting industry innovation, industry competitiveness and skills development. 3.18              Alongside the AGP, the Defence Growth Partnership (DGP) has been established by Government with industry to address sectoral issues to support future UK defence growth.  The creation of the DGP was acknowledgement by Government that more needed to be done to support the defence industrial sector. 3.19              The DGP is there to help create the conditions that industry needs in order for it to be able to continue to generate and enhance innovation, create and retain highly skilled jobs, increase high-value exports and play a major role within the Government’s prosperity agenda.  Understanding customer needs and, as a result, the ability and willingness to develop coordinated UK solutions that can be tailored to the needs of the export customer are very relevant to future success.              3.20              The DGP also established the UK Defence Solutions Centre (UKDSC) as an independent entity designed to unite industry players to innovate and develop world-leading products and services to ensure competitive advantage.  The UK DSC is also tasked with positing the UK
