defence industry to be more successful in exports.  The UK DSC is working closely with the UKTI DSO, which leads on promoting UK defence exports outside that that are the sole responsibility of the MoD.               Other 3.21              Cutting across military and civil aviation is the growth of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS), which, as well as presenting challenges to existing aircraft manufacturers, especially in the military areas, has resulted in significant growth in SME UAS operators and the creation of a small number of new specialist manufacturers.  UAS are likely to contribute primarily to the service sector, but the highly advanced technology demanded for the aircraft platforms and their operation and management must be considered as both an opportunity and a threat to UK technology-based companies.  The threat results from the import of mass production devices and advanced software systems due to limited UK investment and home market potential. 4.              How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy, for example in preventing takeovers of UK companies?4.1              The goal of any industrial strategy should be to overcome market failure that form barriers to growth and success that will in turn benefit the national economy and society generally, otherwise leaving the private sector to do what it does best.4.2              In the civil and defence aerospace sectors, the industry has already asked for Government support in the areas and ways described above, and through a partnership approach, Government and industry, working together to generate absolute gains for the industry itself and the national economy, are addressing sector wide challenges to future growth.5.              What lessons can be learnt from:   Previous government’s industrial strategies?5.1              For the civil and defence aerospace sector, the current Government is encouraged to build on the accomplishments of existing strategies established by the 2010-2015 Government and support the existing government-industry partnership approach.    Other countries’ attempts to develop industrial strategies?              No comment.6.              What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions?6.1               Government has widely advertised its support for UK aerospace as a core manufacturing asset, and it is providing financial support for civil aeronautics and space; however, the Government’s stance on the defence industrial base has been vague and more ambiguous, even with the establishment of the DGP.  Government policies (as customer of defence products and services) have tended to work in practice contrary to having a strong indigenous military aerospace sector.6.2              The UK has, over the years, adopted and implemented one of the most open defence market industrial policies in the NATO area.  The UK remains unique as a defence industrial power in largely defaulting to free market solutions, hoping that other countries will follow by example or by persuasion.  This approach offers little protection in a harsh global commercial environment where are nationals industries are well supported by their governments and access to markets is closely monitored and controlled.6.3              Since most other countries do not apply the same freedoms, the UK defence industry will come under even more pressure from international competitors if the Government continues to pursue a policy of open competition in an attempt to provide a better deal for the taxpayer.6.4              The UK government is a major customer of UK produced defence equipment and services.  Of late, however, the Government has tended towards the procurement of overseas designed equipment to achieve a cheaper headline costs to the UK taxpayer for the provision of essential national defence and security capability.  The pursuance of a policy of procuring off-the-shelf equipment from foreign firms would in some cases likely offer a better initial financial deal, the full implications of buying off-the-shelf could be significant, as it risks eroding the defence industrial base, which the Government is committed to preserving, and it would mean missing on the national economic benefits of buying from British defence suppliers.6.5              Desirable though in principle it might be to combine value for money for the taxpayer from all military spending programmes, as well as guaranteeing high-skilled jobs, this is unrealistic in all circumstances, especially in the current fiscal context. Value for money does not necessarily equate with cheapest initial purchase cost.  True value for money requires analysis of through-life costs to maintain a particular capability at maximum effectiveness through ongoing logistics support and upgrade, and the ability to adapt capability at short notice to meet emerging threats.  That said, UK industry has to play its part in driving effectiveness and efficiency so that the cost of each phase of the life-cycle is minimised and globally competitive. 6.6               The retention of indigenous capability is dependent on the UK having a strong defence aerospace industrial base, backed by a coherent technology programme.  Some investment will be required as well as ensuring the UK companies are in a position to assume leading roles in international programmes; this is especially true for R&D investment, which will also encourage inward investment into onshore manufacturing as well as supporting export success.  These interventions will help to retain high-value manufacturing skills.6.7              There is a case to revisit some of the UK government’s assumption in light of the UK’s economic requirements, as well as defending fundamental security capabilities.  UK companies already compete hard against formidable odds in the international markets.  While this helps to keep companies competitive, UK domestic procurement policy should not increase unreasonable the burden of competition.6.8              The UK cannot rely completely on the production of overseas designed equipment, or on the technology, important though it is, of new concepts like unmanned systems.  There is a strong security as well as economic case for supporting the UK military aerospace, and wider defence, industry; domestic procurement policies should not unreasonable increase the slop of the competitive playing field for UK-based companies.  The consequences of such a course of action will have a significant impact on the industry’s ability to create high-value jobs and increase exports levels, and mute the impact of an industrial strategy.6.9              Support does not necessarily include UK government commitment to expensive new platforms.  In the future, large new aerospace platforms will be increasingly rare – although upgrades will usually be required – but it is vital that UK systems integrators of airframes, sensors and propulsion remain in a position to assume leading roles in international programmes.  To do so, requires a concerted strategic investment in underpinning technology, particularly in the electronics and avionics sectors.  These are the foundations of future capability and export success, whether in indigenous programmes or sold into overseas platforms.  If the UK government were to pursue this goal, it would help to retain core manufacturing skills and be a strong focus for academic and other research.6.10              There is a world-class body of knowledge embedded in the UK military aerospace industry which should be defended and nurtured.  It is an investment for the future, with the potential to reap substantial benefits in military effectiveness, economic success and political influence.              6.11              The maintenance of operational sovereignty in specific areas has been a keystone of recent defence strategies.  Indigenous capability provides strategic resilience in the ability to recognise and anticipate emerging threats and to respond in an appropriate timescale.  Specifically, sovereignty is essential for military assessments of combat risk, affording priority access to industrial capability, better value-for money over equipment life-cycles and operational control over certain classified aspects of science and technology. 6.12              However, retaining indigenous capability is dependent on the UK having a strong defence aerospace industrial base and the Government has a responsibility for the health and success of the UK defence industry, in part due to the significant economic and social gains a strong industrial base provides.  Once lost, such a capability can take years to regain if indeed recovery is actually possible.6.13              The Government need to achieve a better balance of safeguarding operational sovereignty whilst also delivering value for money, and a process must be identified to balance these national defence and economic imperatives.7.              What are the pros and cons of an industrial strategy adopting a sectoral approach?7.1              By their very nature, industrial strategies are sector-specific, as opposed to broader macro-economic policies.  Industrial strategies should be complemented and supported by a cross-sectoral polices which address market-wide issues, including through regulation and fiscal policy.   Should the Government proactively seek to ‘pick winners’?7.2              The Government should focus on creating the conditions for private industry to flourish.  In aerospace this should mean removing barriers to growth and supporting innovation.  In the defence sector, the preservation of the defence industrial base is essential to the preservation of national security and operational sovereignty, the Government must find a better balance between achieving value for money and the retention of indigenous capability.    What criteria should be used to identify which sectors are supported? No comment. Should the Government prop up traditional industries that it considers to be in the national interest? No comment. If not a sectoral approach, should the industrial strategy have a broader objective, such as improving productivity? No comment.8.              Should the industrial strategy have a geographical emphasis?How should an industrial strategy link with devolution initiatives aimed at devolving taxation and decision making aware from Westminster?No comment.What examples are there of inteventions from central Government that have successfully supported economic growth away from London and the South East of England? No comment. How should the industrial strategy work with local authorities and Local Economic Partnerships, reconciling a UK-wide strategy and local, regional and devolved nations’ priorities? No comment. 27 September 2016 [1] Rhodes, C. (2016) Briefing Paper 07682: Industrial strategy.  London, House of Commons Library.[2] Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (2016) News Story:  New ministerial portfolios confirmed at Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy.  London, HMG.[3] Prime Minister’s Office (2016) Press Release: New Cabinet committee to tackle top government priority. London, HMG.[4] Ibid.[5] Ibid.[6] Aerospace Growth Partnership (2016) Means of Ascent: The Aerospace Growth Partnership’s Industrial Strategy for UK Aerospace 2016.  London, HMG.[7] Ibid.[8] Means of Ascent[9] HM Treasury (2015) Spending Review and Autumn Statement 2015.  London, HMG[10] UK Space Innovation and Growth Strategy (2015) 2015 Updqte Report.  Space IGS,[11] Ibid.[12] Defence Growth Partnership (2014) Delivering Growth: Implementing the strategic vision for the UK Defence Sector.  London, HMG.[13] Ibid.[14] Ibid.

submitter: The Road Surface Treatments Association (RSTA) Ltd
text: ISG0144 - Evidence on Industrial strategyWritten evidence from SSE (ISG 144)   SSE welcomes the opportunity to submit evidence to the Business, Innovation and Skills Select Committee’s inquiry into what the Government means by industrial strategy, and the opportunity to set out the approach that SSE would advise the Government to take.   SSE is a UK-listed company and the broadest based utility operating in Great Britain. It has operations across the UK and independent research has found it made a £8.78bn contribution to UK GDP in 2015, supporting around 106,000 jobs. SSE’s core market is the GB energy market. It is committed to the long-term and sustainable development of this market and has long sought to maximise the economic benefits that are created by its commercial activities. SSE therefore welcomes the commitment from the UK Government to introduce an industrial strategy.  Executive summary  SSE believes the Government will maximise the benefits for the UK as a whole if the Industrial Strategy is ambitious, transparent and sustainable:  An ambitious industrial strategy will ensure that the policy mechanisms reflect the rapid rate with which technology improves and seize the first mover advantage that can be realised by properly investing in, and supporting industries. This response explores the opportunity presented by developing the UK’s offshore wind industry, and how through developing a robust supply chain, the benefits of this industry can be experienced across the UK. A transparent industrial strategy will set clear and consistent aims, with a strong methodological basis for realising its goals. This will ensure that investor confidence is maintained. SSE would support an independent, periodic review of the Industrial Strategy to ensure the UK is maximising the benefits.  A sustainable industrial strategy will promote best business practice in the UK and ensure that communities in each part of the UK benefit from the businesses that operate within them. This may require industrial strategy to be driven by different geographies and their needs. In doing so it will be embedded in the strengths of that area and will put structures in place to allow long-term planning from both the private sector and the Government.   This inquiry is timely and important in not only scrutinising the Government’s direction for an industrial strategy, but also in informing and advising the direction itself.  This response considers the questions posed by the Committee inquiry in turn. SSE would welcome the opportunity to discuss these issues further. Q1. What does the Government mean by industrial strategy, and what does the private sector want from one? SSE welcomed the creation of the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy. It sends a positive message by placing the energy industry and the provision of affordable, reliable and lower carbon supplies in the context of a broader consideration of the Government’s approach to business. Given the role of energy in driving UK competitiveness and the broader economy the sector can be central to any efforts to create an industrial strategy.  An industrial strategy may be a process, not an event. SSE agrees with the BEIS Secretary Mr Clark when he said that industrial strategy has four components. Firstly, that the Government should take a long-term, predictable and sustained approach to policy making. Secondly that the Government should take an active role in promoting those things that contribute to a successful environment in which businesses can be founded, can expand and can prosper. Thirdly to connect disparate forces within the economy to work more efficiently. Fourthly to capitalise on the UK’s strengths while seeking new opportunities.[1]  An industrial strategy that allows and accounts for evolution and change is desirable for both government and the private sector. Businesses can, and do, cope with orderly change. Disorderly change however can be disruptive and unconstructive in creating the environment for the industrial strategy to succeed. Ensuring change is managed within the Industrial Strategy allows the strategy to evolve with exterior change to business environments.  SSE welcomes this approach and the commitment to working with business to deliver the Industrial Strategy. It is important that the ‘private sector’ is not considered as a homogenous entity. SSE as a UK-headquartered, UK-based company, has long acknowledged that its commercial objectives can only be achieved through maintaining a deep commitment to the communities in which it operates. These communities form its employees, supply chain, customer base and the stakeholders who determine the company’s direction. It is as a community engaged and responsible business that SSE is responding to this inquiry.  SSE wants the Government to pursue an ambitious, transparent and sustainable industrial strategy. It should focus on maximising the benefits for the UK economically, socially and environmentally. Prior to initiating its industrial strategy the Government should carry out a thorough assessment into what will deliver the maximum amount of value, considering economic, social, environmental impact, to inform its industrial approach.  During this initial assessment the Government should consider not only what has been successful in previous industrial strategies, but what practices businesses have themselves implemented, and work with them to ensure best practice is promoted and shared across the UK (see case study 1).  SSE recommends that the Government consider taking a vertically integrated approach to considering where the UK’s capabilities lie, and where it may be more cost effective to import materials. Where the UK is able to provide the content to support the Industrial Strategy the Government should explore sustainable ways of supporting these industries.  Interventions in the market can pose the risk whereby the conditions created favour less productive services, or those of a lesser quality over superior services. This would not be sustainable, or a desirable outcome for the UK.  The Industrial Strategy should be independently reviewed, with a clear framework from which to judge its success. This will ensure that the Government’s targets are being met effectively, and provide the opportunity to ensure the Industrial Strategy is not leading to unintended consequences.   The Industrial Strategy needs to be considered in the context of the EU Referendum result, and the Government should seek to maximise any opportunities that may arise as a result of the decision to leave the EU. The rules governing the business environment may change to allow the Government to provide support that may have previously not been permitted under EU rules.  The Government should consider how, through the Industrial Strategy, it can use public procurement to realise its economic, environmental, and social aims. How the Government can utilise public procurement should be revisited once there is greater clarity about the UK’s relationship with the EU.  Q2. How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy, for example in preventing foreign takeovers of UK companies? The Government should consider the broader economic impact of investors investing in the UK. It is healthy and desirable for the UK to remain attractive for outside investment. To ensure the UK remains an attractive location for investment the Government should be consistent in how it judges the merits of any takeover. This will send clear signals to investors and put pressure on participants in takeover situations to demonstrate how their plans would have a positive economic, social and environmental impact in the UK. It should also maintain a distinction between takeovers for ‘ownership’ and ‘control’.  The Government, through its industrial strategy should seek to establish an understanding about how companies operating across countries are balancing any subsidies or support they may receive from the UK with investments in other countries. An industrial strategy that picks a winner may inadvertently lead to UK taxpayers subsidising a companies’ practices in another country. In addition, as an accredited Fair Tax payer, SSE supports a transparent approach to companies on tax, including country-by-country reporting.  When the Government concludes that intervention is appropriate SSE believes the Government should provide  visibility of its ambition, ensure there is an objective and consistent framework behind it, and provides certainty for investors for as long as possible. Within energy, for example, the long lead times of larger projects benefits from as much clarity as possible in terms of the policies that will support investments over tens of years.   The previous Government was successful in promoting both onshore and offshore wind and in particular UK content to deliver on its goals of an industry in its infancy. Without government support the majority of components would have been imported from more advanced facilities that have had a longer period to mature.   SSE is supportive of the Government continuing this momentum and implementing an ambitious plan for offshore wind. Contracts-for-Difference (CfDs) have been an effective tool in signalling intent to investors and creating a competitive market process; SSE supports continuation of the mechanism for delivering the UK’s pipeline of offshore wind projects. However  the offshore wind sector is evolving, with many new potential developments having much greater capacity and increasingly being further from shore.  It is essential that Government ensures the CfD mechanism, and broader supporting framework, continues to be  appropriate to support this new scale of development.   UK customers will benefit from the Government’s ambition. The construction of larger offshore wind farms could help deliver the aim of reducing the cost of offshore wind, based on economies of scale, and opens up the potential to deliver benefits across the supply chain and attract inward investment. It can support the development of UK manufacturing if UK content is promoted through the Industrial Strategy.     Offshore wind projects such as at Doggerbank could potentially supply 4GW of energy to the UK. Some elements of the current CfD framework are not best-suited for developing large offshore wind projects. SSE considers this a real opportunity for   Government to be ambitious through its Industrial Strategy. SSE is developing its thinking on specific changes required and would welcome the opportunity to share this work with the Committee at the appropriate time. The Government has had a clear objective to support renewable technology until they mature. From this approach supply chains have been allowed to grow to support British industry. Offshore wind should be enabled to continue to follow a similar path and resist actions that may damage investor confidence in the sector. Q3. What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions? The Government has finite financial resources. A successful industrial strategy needs to be adequately funded. This could divert funds from other areas. The Government, after deciding upon its approach to the Industrial Strategy should maintain funding until delivery, against pressures calling for funding in other areas.  It is imperative that the Industrial Strategy works in harmony with other policies.  Tensions arise between businesses and government when change is implemented in a disorderly manner. It is to be expected that the Industrial Strategy may need to be altered in reaction to changes in the business economic environment in which it is being applied. It is important that Industrial Strategy is established in a way that ensures these changes are implemented in an orderly manner. GB Energy policy  SSE believes that the Electricity Market Reforms (EMR) established under the last government put the right framework in place, via the Capacity Market, CfDs, and Carbon Price Floor (CPF), to deliver the investment required in a low carbon, secure and affordable electricity system. These policies have been optimised since their implementation, and in particular SSE is supportive of the recent reforms the Government has made to the Capacity Market  which is now better able to deliver new and high efficiency gas-fired generation needed to replace ageing coal-fired power stations and maintain flexible capacity.   The CPF also plays an important part in facilitating cost-effective decarbonisation and supporting security of supply by supporting the case for investment in low carbon generation. The more the CPF is left untouched, and the more visibility investors can be given on its time horizon, the more effective it will be. A robust and assured carbon support mechanism provides confidence to the market which enhances liquidity and allows existing asset owners and new developers better opportunities to hedge their output. This reduces risk and as such makes investment cheaper. It is also important for the wider stakeholder environment. It provides clarity on the government’s direction of travel, which in turn reassures lenders and shareholders. SSE encourages the government to extend the CPF until at least 2025.  Skills and training  In 2017 the Apprenticeship Levy comes into force. It is important that the drive to improve the skills to match the ambition of the Industrial Strategy does not place an undue burden on the companies tasked with realising the Government’s goals.  The UK faces a skills shortage with an insufficient uptake of individuals choosing to pursue STEM careers. The issue facing the Industrial Strategy within skills is twofold, to encourage an increase in those choosing STEM careers, and considering what can be achieved with the current skills shortage.  SSE believes there is an exciting opportunity for the Government to capitalise within the Industrial Strategy to cement the UK’s position as the world leader in offshore wind. Current fluctuations in global oil prices have put pressure on UK jobs, particularly in the North Sea. SSE believes the Government should explore supporting the offshore wind industry to absorb and allow these skilled workers to continue employment through establishing a strong, UK supply chain supported, offshore wind industry (see case study 2).                                The UK generates more electricity from offshore wind than any other country. The Government should seek to build upon these capabilities and develop the industry. A strong offshore wind industry presents opportunities to export UK intelligence.  Q4. Should the Industrial Strategy have a geographical emphasis? SSE operates across England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland and believes more will be achieved through an open dialogue between the nations and devolved regions, in agreeing the aims of the Industrial Strategy.  Any industrial strategy must seek to harness the competencies, resources and experiences of different geographies in the UK. For example, Scotland has a rich history in renewable energy such as hydro power and, more recently, onshore wind. Scotland has benefitted tremendously from the renewable electricity industry and the Industrial Strategy should aim to provide certainty to the industry beyond 2020, to ensure these benefits are continued and expanded.  Investment in the renewable sector in Scotland has increased significantly over the past ten years. In 2015 Scottish renewables generated 57.7% of Scotland’s total electricity consumption, in comparison to 14.1% in 2004. The renewable industry has the potential for tremendous growth across the UK, and the Government should be seeking to realise its potential. This will open up opportunities to develop a rich export industry of energy intelligence and materials.                  Siemens’ announcement that it is seeking to recruit 100 more employees in its wind turbine blade factory in Hull demonstrates the ability for cities and towns to attract investment from the energy industry. The Caithness-Moray Transmission project engaged communities in Blackhillock, Spitaal, Thurso, Dounreay and Beauly. The benefits of investment in energy infrastructure can be shared across the UK in a way that other industries may struggle.  There is an opportunity to develop the UK’s heavy marine manufacturing base including cable manufacturing. JDR, based in Hartlepool was rightly praised by the previous Government, and supplied the cables for our Greater Gabbard project. The Industrial Strategy should explore how to build on this success and ensure the right structures are in place to ensure these successful industries are giving the support they need.  The Government should seek to ensure the benefits of the Industrial Strategy are spread across the UK. By developing the manufacturing base around offshore wind there is an opportunity to extend this supply chain across the UK and ensure investment is focused on areas that it is required.  29 September 2016[1] Rt. Hon. Greg Clark MP speech to the Royal Society on 1st August 2016.

ISG0143 - Evidence on Industrial strategyWritten evidence from the Institution of Mechanical Engineers (ISG 143)  The Committee will look at the following:1. What does the Government mean by industrial strategy, and what does the private sector want from one? It is expected that an industrial strategy should both support and promote British Industry and its workers as well as make connections between different industries and sectors.  Support should take the form of an enduring commitment from Government to support key technologies and sectors and include a regional agenda.  That’s not just tax incentives or subsidies that ensure growth in a particular sector but rather an approach that permeates all Government departments.  Perhaps most importantly businesses need a cross party long-term commitment to an industrial strategy, with particular focus on infrastructure investments (eg transport and energy).  Businesses will then re-invest into their own organisations as well as the supply chain that supports them, which will lead to further growth in industry and an innovative UK. The UK is currently facing an industrial challenge, where more investment is needed across the sectors, yet there is increasing uncertainty which holds this back.  The Government is still failing to use policies and subsidies to deliver security to UK industry.  A good example of this is the recent changes in energy policy that have led to increased investor uncertainty and stagnation in the development of projects, as reported in the ECC Investor Confidence report earlier this year.[1] The private sector benefits from a secure future meaning that they can invest in their UK sites, create jobs and economic growth in areas surrounding their industry.  This will then create opportunities in education programmes at further and higher levels as students will feel confident that they can have a career in a particular sector such as, manufacturing, transport systems and energy generation.   By the Government developing a long-term cross party industrial strategy it demonstrates to businesses that the UK has a plan. Cross-party must mean that decisions are made on technical and economic facts that are unhindered by politics. This will send out a positive message to engineering and manufacturing businesses that they are needed and at the centre of these investments.  Businesses looking to grow will invest in education schemes and attract skilled individuals into the profession. Rather than the feast and famine approach to investment that we have experienced over the past within our energy and transport infrastructure, there will be the commitment necessary to encourage this inward investment.  An example is that we have not encouraged engineers to specialise in nuclear skills since the 1970s and with these individuals now reaching retirement there are very few people with the right skills to support the construction of the proposed new power plants.  There has however been much analysis of the economy and particular sectors – plus creation of specific organisations – most recently the National Infrastructure Commission (NIC). If we are going to form an Industrial Strategy there need to be (a) clear objective(s) for the overall UK economy in terms of what we want to achieve (eg growth with respect to G7 productivity ratings) and (b) a statement on how the Government intends to create the long term framework for growing industry as part of this new system. It will be important that the Government makes the relationship between the NIC and the industrial strategy clear, that the direction of influence and communication is two way and does not lead to confusing messages to industry private or public sector.This industrial strategy must be joined up and aligned with other activities that are being funded.  For example the Research Council priorities should match Innovate UK priorities, which should match the skills priorities and the industrial strategies from the sector leadership councils.  In the past we have had some examples of alignment across these areas (such as Automotive), but less so in the others (e.g. Transport does not figure as a priority from any of the Research Councils).  This way we can maximise the impact of government investment with joined up and complementary strategies which will endure on a long-term basis.  Energy policy is key to an industrial strategy but this must be looked at holistically with approached such as ‘‘peak looping’ through smart connectivity could provide solutions.  Examples here could be by encouraging businesses to run their energy intensive functions at different times of the day to reduce total demand.  Through the rise of the internet of things and smart factories we will be able to get to a scenario where a network of appliances could be switched on and off throughout the day to help balance peaks in the grid.  We must ask ourselves whether or not industry can do more to work together to reduce the requirement for energy.  With a long-term cross-party industrial strategy it will send a clear and strong message to foreign investors.  This message will be that the UK has a plan both in terms of current strategy and future growth.  This may be crucial given the uncertainty surrounding the post-Brexit world.  2. How interventionist in the free market should Government be in implementing an industrial strategy, for example in preventing foreign takeovers of UK companies? It is up to national, regional, and local government bodies to offer a suitable and safe investment climate if they want to be the recipients of funds. Conversely, a failure to establish certainty and confidence could result in the loss of much-needed projects.  On very rare occasions should the national Government intervene where it considers national strategic interests might be at risk.  Whatever the changing global patterns of investment that emerge in the near future, the trend towards greater and greater dependence on FDI looks set to continue for some time. 3. What lessons can be learnt from: Previous governments' industrial strategies? In September 1965, the then Secretary of State for Economic Affairs produced “The National Plan” which sought to cover “all aspects of the country’s economic development for the next five years”. The plan was over 450 pages long and looked at everything from the running cost of schools to the future development of the electronics industry. While this plan was comprehensive in scope, our modern economy does not operate in a way which makes this approach useful and would have significant negative consequences.  However the lack of success of documents like “The National Plan” does not mean that there should not be any industrial policy in the UK or that there is not a case for a coherent document to be put together by central government outlining the support that it intends to give to grow the manufacturing sector.  Over the last five years there has been much analysis over historical trends as well as forecasting future ones, whilst looking into what a modern industrial strategy should look like. These include the work done by the All Party Parliamentary Manufacturing Group on Developing a Modern Industrial Policy [2] and the Adonis Review which was commissioned by the Labour Party in 2014. [3] If we look at the developments within the automotive industry, for example on carbon emissions, one may argue that central Government typically works well with objectives and targets which give clear focus and concentrate minds. Climate change policy is an example of how central government, when given a clear objective, can rally around to focus on the issues at hand and develop policy approaches which meet specific targets. We need to develop a similar approach across Whitehall when it comes to manufacturing / industrial strategy.  Other countries' attempts to develop industrial strategies? It is often not of much value to look at other countries’ strategies as they are based on cultures and resources not applicable in the UK.  For example China’s central government issued their Made in China 2025 10 year industrial action plan in May 2015. It identifies 9 goals:Improving manufacturing innovationIntegrating information technology and industryStrengthening the fundamental capacity of industryFostering Chinese brandsEnforcing green manufacturingPromoting breakthroughs in 10 key sectorsAdvancing restructuring of the manufacturing sectorsPromoting service-orientated manufacturing and producer service systemInternationalising manufacturing This approach is rather similar to our own 1965 National Plan and seems likely to be doomed to similar failure if applied here. What seems more sensible for the UK is the approach taken by Germany to reach out internationally on specific issues.  For example, Germany (via the Fraunhofer Institutes) has taken advantage of the growing global powers by forming strategic partnerships with them to assist with their innovation and development in manufacturing equipment. Germany has such alliances now with both China and India. For the UK to remain competitive however, we need to review how much of our public money is invested into businesses where there would be an economic return (ie Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 4 to 8).  Fraunhofer has an annual budget of £1.95 billion compared to UK Catapults which have less than £400 million 4. What tensions exist between the objectives of an industrial strategy and the objectives of other policies, and how should the Government address these tensions? It is possible that current regulations surrounding state aid and procurement activities would be in conflict with subsidies to the private sector.  If new industries or existing industries became government led/ owned there may be difficulties under existing procurement rules that do not currently ensure that other UK industries benefit from government run industry. In the post Brexit era, the Government should change these rules and regulations and ensure that UK industries, including energy generation, manufacturing and transport benefit from government led industry.  Further to this, strategic approach National plans produced by devolved administrations must be connected to the UK industrial strategy.  Recently the Welsh Government released their five year plan for Wales making the following commitments:[4] Driving forward inward investment, innovation and the creation of new jobs by providing more support for businesses, including delivering a tax cut, meaning smaller bills for 70,000 businesses and reducing business rates bills to zero for half of all eligible firms;Ensuring prosperity for all by creating a minimum 100,000 high quality, all age apprenticeships, reshape employability support for people to find and get back into work and deliver the Cardiff City Region Deal, develop a similar deal for Swansea and a Growth Deal for North Wales;It is possible that some of these apprenticeships and employability ambitions could be filled by a positive outcome to the tidal lagoon project and the new nuclear programme.  Whilst we support the work that is being done regionally by the LEPs and local governments their strategies must feed into a national one as their overriding objective. These targets will become more achievable and provide long term sustainability if they are connected to the wider industrial strategy ensuring that supply chains are connected across the UK. Devolved national plans cannot be effectively achieved in isolation. The UK should also look to create supply chains through the gradual changes and incremental innovation in sectors such as process improvements to reduce energy use and fuel and energy efficiencies in the transport sector.  This may mean that to encourage these incremental innovations in incumbent technologies the Government may need to review existing industry codes and regulations surrounding transport and industry.  This can be managed by ensuring that incumbent providers work with government to ensure they can embrace disruptive technologies and develop in parallel with emerging technologies and processes.  The Government will need to accept that some level of subsidies will be needed to support these industries to evolve. In the post-Brexit era we must address how an industrial strategy will support our engineering and manufacturing sectors to attract a skilled workforce.  Currently our educations system teaches very little about the ‘made’ world within the classroom.  This needs to be rectified to support our engineering sectors if we are to have long-term commitment to an industrial strategy with the people power to deliver it.  Schemes such as Insight into Industry should be encouraged throughout our education institutions. [5] There is also a challenge with the immigration strategy and making sure that in the negotiations post-Brexit this is aligned with our industry.   5. What are the pros and cons of an industrial strategy adopting a sectoral approach?Should the Government proactively seek to ‘pick winners’? A strategy is not effective unless it is widely understood aligned to a common vision.  The government should not seek to micro-manage, but should accept that subsidies and flexibility in policies will be seen as part of an over-arching intent. It is not necessary to pick specific winners, but important to listen to industry leaders such as those consulted during the Government Office for Science, Foresight report - The future of manufacturing. [6]  Reports such as these take a large amount of effort and time for our industry so it can be frustrating when recommendations are put forward but then not implemented by Government.  The role of a Government is to make tough decisions when they are needed delays of 10-20 years on decisions e.g. Heathrow expansion / Hinkley C lead the UK to fall behind in terms of competitiveness as well as the loss of our skilled workforce. The welcome decision to support Hinkley Point C should be the beginning of a Government led process which includes research and development of small modular reactor and drives forward the deep geological facility for the effective disposal and management of radioactive wastes.  This is the development of wider industry and manufacturing around a large government infrastructure decision and a good example of how the future could look. . As an example, Government could play an important role to promote the use of modular construction taking learning and experience from industry sectors such as offshore oil exploration and applying them to nuclear construction.  This would ensure that UK industry and the supply chain benefits from any Government investment in this area.   What criteria should be used to identify which sectors are supported? The Government would benefit from supporting industries in the UK that have a wide supply chain that can support manufacturing across the UK.  Industries, such as energy and transport provide this opportunity for manufactured parts.  The building of new power supplies whether power stations, renewables (on and offshore) as well as upstream oil, gas and extraction could create a central focus for manufacturing to build out from.  The manufacturing of new power generating equipment would support the manufacturing sector across the UK.  In China the government set targets for new technologies to be manufactured within China, initially they import parts, then they learn and begin to create the parts themselves.  A good example here is the rapid growth of wind power in China, initially importing parts, now 99% manufactured within China.  This has happened over a very short period of 7 years.  Sectors that provide opportunities for the circular economy to develop. Circular economy/material and resource constraints – need to recognise every aspect and step of the whole product lifecycle and subsequent lifecycles/material reuse as part of the manufacturing / industrial landscape.  As virgin material becomes scarcer and more expensive to extract and process our anthropogenic stocks will increasingly become the material sources for future products.  Whilst the UK may not be able to compete at a commodity level, for the more advanced components, systems and modules, the UK still has a strong international reputation which needs to be exercised and continually developed.  As an example, the competition for small modular reactors and renewal of carbon capture and storage along with similar competitions for energy and transport systems and new system architectures should be continued and used to ensure that UK industry stays at the forefront of this developing technology.  Government supporting consortia that include industry and academia as well as public sector partners that create and share knowledge should be prioritised.  Finance can be sought through innovation public/ private tools and crowdfunding activities supported by government. The initial funding of ‘Catapult’ facilities which draw together industry and academia in a practical environment is particularly valuable as initial funding will draw in private sector investment.   Ensuring particular regions are able to sustain existing and create local industries around their resources and incumbent technologies will ease the communication with communities where they are able to clearly understand their role in the growth of industry in the UK. The UK Catapults help. This is often achieved by working closely with industry sector leadership groups (such as the Automotive Council and the Offshore Wind Industry Council), and helping those cross industry groups to deliver on their industrial strategies or, for example, by facilitating effective corporate engagement with cities in solving urban challenges.  By acting as trusted, neutral and relevant organisations, the Catapults are building momentum by delivering successful projects which de-risk technologies and help to bring innovation based products and services to market for UK based organisations including SME’s. Since their inauguration many of the catapult centres have generated millions of pounds in revenue as well as the creation of further manufacturing facilities and jobs for local communities. An example with the High Value Manufacturing (HVM) Catapult’s work with Rolls-Royce. Through collaboration the development of single crystal fan blade technology and fan disc manufacturing innovation resulted in the opening in 2014 of two new Rolls-Royce production facilities in Yorkshire and the North East respectively.  These facilities are the result of technology innovation work undertaken by the HVM Catapult community and several of the centres being involved. They have generated over £100m investment and hundreds of local jobs.  The HVM Catapult worked with over 3,000 businesses in 2015-16, over 50% of whom were SMEs. Their economic impact evaluation of 2015, showed that every £1 of government core funding received generated an impressive £15 of net benefits to the UK economy. Manufacturers that have the competitive edge maximise business opportunities by developing and implementing new technology solutions.  Within the UK it is no coincidence that the two industries that spend the most on innovation (aerospace and automotive) have seen the greatest productivity gains and continue to do well. We need ensure that any industrial strategy supports a landscape of research & development particularly in a time of uncertainty following Brexit.  A catapult for modular construction is also proposed which could help to bring cross-sector learning from the oil & gas industry to the wider energy industry for the construction of nuclear modules, small CCS systems and off-shore wind facilities. Building modules where there are available skilled resources is more cost effective and socially acceptable than moving a workforce to a remote location ‘ Take the work to the workers rather than expecting the workers to move to the work’.  Should the Government prop up traditional industries that it considers to be in the national interest? The Government should only support industries that can contribute to the changing technological environment.  Materials manufacturers must be able to respond to the new requirements of new technologies and processes across the industrial landscape.  This means that if a material could become more sustainable or changed to a lighter weight alternative then the manufacturers must be able to respond and evolve and be supported to do so. In terms of materials like steel, it is very likely that these will continue to be needed in all large UK industry, and research and development activities can help our steel manufacturers to become increasingly competitive and resource efficient.  UK government support industry can create investment opportunities. It is about giving businesses the right environment to prosper.  This requires co-ordinated action across multiple Government departments associated with:Trade and exports Skills and education Access to finance Public procurement Institutional reform Design and innovation  If not a sectoral approach, should the industrial strategy have a broader objective, such as improving productivity? Productivity is easily misunderstood as being only about people.  We can become more productive in a number of ways such as better methods, better machinery, better use of space, better use of materials, and better use of working capital and of course more motivated people.  Ultimately however, productive does not imply competitive.  It is merely a necessary but not sufficient condition.  Changes in markets and technologies can often mean that the world has “moved on” and we could be left in the UK with a focus on productive but out of date industries. 6. Should the industrial strategy have a geographical emphasis?How should an industrial strategy link with devolution initiatives aimed at devolving taxation and decision making away from Westminster? All initiatives should look to support each other.  The UK will benefit most from increasing supply chains to industry across the UK.  There will likely be cluster of activities in the regions, but these should be supported by all regions in the UK. What examples are there of interventions from central Government that have successfully supported economic growth away from London and the South East of England? There could be a role here for communication and advocacy for supporting regional incumbent industry that can be grown, evolved and generate innovation clusters for a particular industry such as car manufacture, steel manufacture and fabrication, oil and gas extraction and agriculture and food processing.  Communication with communities, schools, colleges and local businesses backed up with some subsidies should lead to confidence and sustainability of the sector.Further communications could focus on ensuring that the UK population understand the role that many large industries play in their everyday lives and that economic frameworks like the circular economy and technologies that are low emissions may result in less profit for businesses and more expensive less disposable products.  The formulation of the Catapult's have been a great initiative out of central government that is already providing returns on investment as detailed earlier. Over the several decades there has been what is seen to be an unhealthy concentration of GDP generation in the South East, which has been helped by the poor infrastructure in the North (anywhere north of Birmingham).  If these northern regions are to flourish then government needs to concentrate on providing the climate for them to break out of the past. How should the industrial strategy work with local authorities and Local Economic Partnerships, reconciling a U.K.-wide strategy and local, regional and devolved nations' priorities? There has been a lot of good work done across the country by the Local Enterprise Partnerships in terms of their economic reviews and support for SMEs from local Chambers of Commerce. An example is for the North East (NE) where they have an above average proportion of GVA generated by manufacturing – 14.6% compared with 10.3% nationally. They are also the only region of the UK with a surplus of trade in goods.
